Ayahs

وَٱلصَّـٰٓفَّـٰتِ صَفًّا (١)
By the [angels] ranged in ranks, (1)
فَٱلزَّٰجِرَٰتِ زَجْرًا (٢)
by the ones who drive [the clouds] vigorously, (2)
فَٱلتَّـٰلِيَـٰتِ ذِكْرًا (٣)
by the ones who recite the reminder: (3)
إِنَّ إِلَـٰهَكُمْ لَوَٰحِدٌ (٤)
indeed your God is certainly One, (4)
رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا وَرَبُّ ٱلْمَشَـٰرِقِ (٥)
the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, and the Lord of the easts. (5)

Content of Surah "As-Saffat"

This Surah, by virtue of being "Makki," contains the general characteristics of Meccan Surahs. More than anything else, it emphasizes the principles of Islamic knowledge and beliefs regarding the "Origin" (Allah) and the "Resurrection" (Ma'ad). Through decisive expressions and short, striking verses, it subjects the polytheists to the severest rebukes and exposes the falsehood of their beliefs with clear and eloquent proofs.

Generally, the content of this Surah can be summarized into five sections:

The first section: Discusses groups of angels and the messengers of Allah, and in contrast to them, mentions a group of rebellious devils and their fate.

The second section: Speaks of the disbelievers, their denial of Prophethood and the Resurrection, and their ultimate end on the Day of Judgment. In this regard, it describes their disputes with one another on that Day, how they cast blame upon each other, and how they are all caught in the grip of Divine punishment. It also details some of the significant blessings of Paradise, as well as the pleasures, beauties, and joys of the people of Heaven.

The third section: Recounts parts of the history of great Prophets such as "Noah," "Abraham," "Isaac," "Moses," "Aaron," "Elias," "Lot," and "Jonah" in a concise yet very effective and influential manner. However, among these, the discussion regarding "Abraham," the hero of idol-breaking, and the various stages of his life is more detailed. The primary goal is to present the previous statements through these objective historical examples of the Prophets in a tangible and perceptible way, embodying general intellectual truths within sensory frameworks.

The fourth section: Discusses one of the types of polytheism that can be considered the "worst type of polytheism"—namely, the belief in a kinship relationship between Allah and the Jinn, and between Allah and the angels. In short sentences, it shatters this hollow belief so thoroughly that not the slightest value remains for it.

Finally, the fifth section: Which is the last part of this Surah and is presented in a few short verses, declares the victory of the army of Truth over the army of disbelief, polytheism, and hypocrisy. It mentions their entrapment in the grip of Divine punishment while exalting and sanctifying the Lord from the improper attributes the polytheists ascribe to Him, ending the Surah with the praise and glorification of the Lord.

The Virtue of Reciting Surah "As-Saffat"

In a Hadith from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

من قرأ سورة صافات اعطى من الاجر عشر حسنات، بعدد كل جن و شيطان، و تباعدت عنه مردة الشياطين، و برء من الشرك، و شهد له حافظاه يوم القيامة انه كان مؤمنا بالمرسلين:

"Whoever recites Surah As-Saffat will be given ten rewards for every Jinn and devil, the rebellious devils will be distanced from him, he will be cleared of polytheism, and his two guardian angels will testify for him on the Day of Resurrection that he was a believer in the Messengers." [1]

And in another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

من قرأ سورة صافات فى كل جمعة لم يزل محفوظا من كل آفة، مدفوعا عنه كل بلية فى حياته الدنيا، مرزوقا فى الدنيا باوسع ما يكون من الرزق، و لم يصبه اللَّه فى ماله و لا ولده و لا بدنه بسوء من شيطان رجيم، و لا جبار عنيد، و ان مات فى يومه او ليلته بعثه اللَّه شهيدا، و اماته شهيدا، و ادخله الجنة مع الشهداء فى درجة من الجنة:

"Whoever recites Surah As-Saffat every Friday will remain protected from every calamity, and every affliction in the life of this world will be repelled from him. Allah will provide him with the most extensive sustenance in the world and will not allow any harm from the accursed Satan or any stubborn tyrant to reach his wealth, his children, or his body. And if he dies during that day or night, Allah will resurrect him as a martyr, cause him to die as a martyr, and enter him into Paradise with the martyrs in their rank." [1]

Considering the content of this Surah mentioned in the first section—the details of which you will read—the reason for such immense rewards for reciting this Surah becomes clear. For we know that the purpose of recitation is reflection, followed by belief, and then action. Undoubtedly, one who recites this Surah in this manner will be protected from the evil of devils, purified from polytheism, and by possessing correct and firm beliefs, performing righteous deeds, and learning lessons from the stories of the Prophets and past nations, will be counted among the martyrs.

Additionally, the naming of this Surah as "As-Saffat" is due to its first verse.

[1] "Majma' al-Bayan," beginning of Surah As-Saffat. [1] Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah As-Saffat—in Tafsir "Al-Burhan," this Hadith is also narrated with a slight variation from the late Saduq رحمه الله.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ وَ الصَّافَّاتِ صَفًّا (1) فَالزَّاجِراتِ زَجْراً (2) فَالتَّالِياتِ ذِكْراً (3) إِنَّ إِلهَكُمْ لَواحِدٌ (4) رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما وَ رَبُّ الْمَشارِقِ (5)

Commentary

Angels Ready to Carry Out the Mission

This Surah is the first Surah in the Holy Quran whose opening verses begin with oaths—meaningful and thought-provoking oaths that draw human thought toward various aspects of this world and prepare it to accept truths.

It is true that Allah is the most truthful of all speakers and has no need for an oath. Furthermore, if the oath is for the believers, they submit without an oath; and if it is for the deniers, they do not believe in Divine oaths.

However, paying attention to two points will solve the issue of oaths in all the verses of the Quran that we will encounter from now on.

First: Oaths are always taken by valuable and important matters. Therefore, the oaths of the Quran are evidence of the greatness and importance of the things sworn by, and this very fact causes more reflection on the "Muqsam bihi" (the object of the oath), a reflection that introduces man to new truths.

Second: An oath is always for emphasis and serves as proof that the matters for which the oath is taken are completely serious and emphasized.

Beyond this, whenever a speaker expresses his words decisively, it psychologically has a greater impact on the heart of the listener, making the believers stronger and the deniers softer.

In any case, at the beginning of this Surah, we encounter three groups by which oaths have been taken: [1]

[1] These three phrases are, in one sense, three oaths, and in another sense, one oath with three descriptions.

First, He says: "By those [angels] lined up in rows," (وَ الصَّافَّاتِ صَفًّا). "By those who strongly drive and restrain," (فَالزَّاجِراتِ زَجْراً). "And those who recite the Message [the Reminder] in succession," (فَالتَّالِياتِ ذِكْراً).

Who are these three groups? And whom do these descriptions refer to? And what is the ultimate goal behind them? Commentators have said much here, but the well-known and famous view is that these are attributes for groups of angels.

Groups of angels who stand in rows in the universe, ready to carry out the Divine command. Groups of angels who restrain humans from disobedience and sins, and neutralize the whispers of devils in their hearts; or those who are in charge of the clouds in the sky, driving them in every direction and preparing them to water dry lands. And finally, groups of angels who recite the verses of the Heavenly Books at the time of the descent of revelation upon the Prophets. [1]

[1] Of course, other possibilities have also been given in the interpretation of the above verses, including: referring to the ranks of Islamic warriors in the fields of Jihad; those who shout at the enemies in the battlefield and restrain them from encroaching upon the sanctity of Islam and the Quran; and those who constantly recite the Divine remembrance and illuminate their hearts and souls with its light. It has also been suggested that part of these three attributes refers to angels in organized ranks, part refers to the verses of the Quran that restrain people from vices, and part refers to the believers who recite the Quran in prayer and otherwise. However, separating these attributes seems unlikely, as their conjunction with "fa" (ف) indicates they are all attributes for one group. "Allamah Tabataba'i" in "Al-Mizan" also suggested the possibility that these three attributes are for the angels tasked with conveying Divine revelation: they escort the revelation in organized ranks, push back the devils on their path, and finally recite the Divine verses to the Prophets.

It is noteworthy that "Saffat" is the plural of "Saffah," which itself has a collective meaning and refers to a group that has lined up; therefore, "Saffat" indicates multiple rows. [1] "Zajirat" is from the root "zajr," meaning "driving something away with a sound or shout," and was later used in a broader sense to include any kind of expulsion or prevention. Therefore, "Zajirat" means groups that engage in preventing, expelling, and driving away others. And "Taliyat" is from the root "tilawah," the plural of "Tali," and means groups that undertake the recitation of something. [2] Given the breadth and extensiveness of the concepts of these words, it is not surprising that commentators have mentioned various interpretations for them, which at the same time do not contradict each other. It is possible that all of them are included in the concept of these verses. For example, "Saffat" could mean all the ranks of angels ready to execute Divine commands in the world of creation, as well as the angels tasked with the descent of revelation upon the Prophets in the world of legislation, and also the ranks of warriors and mujahideen in the way of Allah, or the ranks of those praying and worshipping.

[1] From here it becomes clear that the use of "feminine plural" for these attributes is because their singular forms themselves have the meaning of "group" (jama'ah), which is grammatically feminine. [2] It is noteworthy that according to some lexicographers, the plural of "Tali" is "Taliyat" and the plural of "Taliyah" is "Tawali."

Although the context indicates that it mostly refers to angels, and some narrations also point to this. [1] Likewise, there is no obstacle to "Zajirat" including both the angels who distance the whispers of devils from human hearts and the humans who perform the duty of forbidding evil, and so on. And "Taliyat" could refer to all the angels and groups of believers who successively recite the Divine verses and the remembrance of Allah.

Here, the question arises: the appearance of the verses, by the requirement of the conjunction of these three phrases with "fa" (ف), is that these three groups are positioned one after another. Is this order in terms of performing duty? Or according to rank? Or both? It is evident that lining up and getting ready is in the first stage, then expelling obstacles from the path, and after that, expressing the instructions and implementing them. On the other hand, those who prepare to execute the command have a rank, those who expel obstacles have a higher rank, and those who recite the commands and put them into effect are the highest in rank. In any case, the Lord's oath by all these groups indicates the greatness of their status before Him, and simultaneously inspires the truth that the wayfarers of the path of Truth must also pass through these three stages to reach their goal:

First, they must organize their ranks, and each group must take its place in its row. Then, they must proceed to expel obstacles from the path and remove disturbances with a loud cry—the same cry inherent in the concept of "zajr." After that, they should successively recite the Divine verses and the commands of the Lord upon ready hearts and strive to realize their content.

The mujahideen in the way of Truth also have no way but to pass through these three stages, just as true scholars and scientists in their collective efforts must also be inspired by this same program. It is noteworthy that some commentators have interpreted the verses as referring to "mujahideen" and some to "scholars," but limiting the concept of the verses to these two groups seems unlikely. However, the universality of the verses is not far-fetched, and even if we consider them specific to angels, others can still take inspiration from the program of these Divine angels in their own lives.

"Amir al-Mu'minin Ali" عليه السلام, in the first sermon of "Nahj al-Balagha," where he speaks of the angels and divides them into various groups, says:

و صافون لا يتزايلون، و مسبحون لا يسامون، لا يغشاهم نوم العيون، و لا سهو العقول، و لا فترة الأبدان، و لا غفلة النسيان، و منهم امناء على وحيه، و السنة الى رسله:

"A group of them are in ranks that do not break; they glorify [Allah] and do not tire; sleep does not overcome their eyes, nor does error affect their intellects, nor does exhaustion affect their bodies, nor does the heedlessness of forgetfulness come upon them. And among them are the trustees of His revelation and the tongues [sent] to His Messengers." [1]

The final point regarding these three verses is that some believe the oath in these verses is taken by the Pure Essence of Allah, and the word "Rabb" (Lord) is implied in all of them, meaning: "By the Lord of those who line up in rows, and by the Lord of those who drive away, and by the Lord of those who recite the remembrance." Those who have interpreted the verses this way seemingly think that because servants are commanded not to swear by other than Allah, then Allah also does not swear by other than His own Essence. Additionally, an oath must be by something important, and the most important is His Pure Essence. But they overlook the point that Allah's account is separate from His servants. To draw human attention to the "horizons" and "souls" and the signs of His power in the earth and sky, He constantly swears by various creatures to prompt them to reflect on these signs and, through this path, to know Him. Beyond this, in verses of the Holy Quran—such as the verses of Surah Ash-Shams—oaths by the creatures of the world are placed alongside oaths by His Pure Essence, and there, implying something is not possible. He says: وَ السَّماءِ وَ ما بَيناها وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما طَحاها وَ نَفْسٍ وَ ما سَوَّاها: "By the sky and He who built it, by the earth and He who spread it, and by the soul and He who proportioned it." [1] In any case, the apparent meaning of the verses under discussion is an oath by these three groups, and implying something else is contrary to the apparent meaning and cannot be accepted without proof.

[1] Tafsir al-Burhan, vol. 4, p. 15; Ad-Durr al-Manthur, vol. 5, p. 271. [1] Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 1. [1] Surah "Ash-Shams," verses 5-7.

Now let us see for what purpose these content-rich oaths—oaths by the ranks of angels and humans—were taken. The next verse clarifies this matter, saying: "Indeed, your God is surely One," (إِنَّ إِلهَكُمْ لَواحِدٌ). An oath by those sacred things mentioned that the idols are all naught, and there is no partner, likeness, or peer for the Lord.

Then He adds: "[He is] the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, and the Lord of the sunrises [the easts]!" (رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما وَ رَبُّ الْمَشارِقِ). Here, two questions arise:

1- After mentioning the heavens and the earth and what is between them, what is the need to mention "Mashariq" (the sunrises/easts), which is also a part of that? The answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point: "Mashariq," whether it refers to the rising points of the sun throughout the days of the year, or the rising points of various stars in the sky, all possess a specific order and program. Their system, in addition to the system of the heavens and the earth, is a sign of the infinite power and knowledge of their Creator and Disposer. The sun in the sky rises every day of the year from a point different from the point of the day before and after, and the distance between these points is so organized and precise that it does not vary by even a thousandth of a second. For thousands upon thousands of years, the order of the "sunrises of the sun" has been maintained. The same system governs the rising and setting of other stars. Furthermore, if the sun did not traverse this gradual path throughout the year, the four seasons and the various blessings resulting from them would not reach us, and this itself is another sign of His greatness and management. Beyond this, another meaning of "Mashariq" is that because the earth is spherical, every point on it is considered east or west relative to another point. In this way, the above verse draws our attention to the sphericity of the earth and its multiple easts and wests (the intention of both meanings from the verse is also permissible).

2- The other question is: why is there no mention of "Magharib" (the sunsets/wests) in contrast to "Mashariq," as in verse 40 of Surah Al-Ma'arij: فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِرَبِّ الْمَشارِقِ وَ الْمَغارِبِ: "So I swear by the Lord of the easts and the wests!"? The answer is that sometimes a part of the speech is omitted due to the context of another part, and sometimes both are mentioned together. Here, the mention of "Mashariq" is a context for "Magharib," and this variety in expression is considered one of the arts of eloquence. And according to some commentators, it is also noteworthy that the mention of "Mashariq" (sunrises) is consistent with the rising of revelation, which descended through the angels "reciting the Message" upon the pure heart of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. [1]

[1] Tafsir al-Mizan, vol. 17, p. 128.

Ayahs

إِنَّا زَيَّنَّا ٱلسَّمَآءَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِزِينَةٍ ٱلْكَوَاكِبِ (٦)
Indeed We have adorned the lowest heaven with the finery of the stars, (6)
وَحِفْظًا مِّن كُلِّ شَيْطَـٰنٍ مَّارِدٍ (٧)
and to guard from any froward devil. (7)
لَّا يَسَّمَّعُونَ إِلَى ٱلْمَلَإِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ وَيُقْذَفُونَ مِن كُلِّ جَانِبٍ (٨)
They do not eavesdrop on the Supernal Elite—they are shot at from every side, (8)
دُحُورًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ وَاصِبٌ (٩)
to drive them away, and there is a perpetual punishment for them— (9)
إِلَّا مَنْ خَطِفَ ٱلْخَطْفَةَ فَأَتْبَعَهُۥ شِهَابٌ ثَاقِبٌ (١٠)
except any who snatches a snatch, whereat a piercing flame pursues him. (10)

Commentary

The Heavens' Guard Against the Infiltration of Satans!

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the various ranks of Divine angels who are entrusted with great missions. In the verses under discussion, it speaks of their opposite—namely, the groups of satans and their fate. This can serve as a preamble to nullify the belief of a group of polytheists who took satans and jinn as their deities, while simultaneously embedding a lesson of Monotheism (Tawhid) within it.

First, It says: "Indeed, We have adorned the nearest heaven (the lower heaven) with the adornment of stars" (إِنَّا زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِزِينَةٍ الْكَواكِبِ) [^1]. Truly, a single glance at the expanse of the sky on dark, star-filled nights presents such a beautiful scene to human sight that it leaves one enchanted and fascinated.

It is as if they speak to us with a wordless tongue, recounting the secrets of creation; as if they are all poets, continuously reciting the most beautiful ghazals of love and mysticism.

Their twinkling expresses secrets that exist only between the lover and the Beloved.

Indeed, the sight of the stars in the sky is so beautiful that the eye never tires of seeing them; rather, it draws out exhaustion from man's entire being (though these matters may not hold much meaning in our era for city-dwellers immersed in the smoke of factories, who naturally have a dark and murky sky; however, villagers can still witness this Quranic statement—the adornment of the sky with brilliant stars).

Interestingly, It says: "We adorned the lower heaven with stars," whereas the hypothesis prevailing over the thoughts of scholars at that time claimed that only the highest heaven was the heaven of the fixed stars (the eighth heaven according to the Ptolemaic hypothesis).

[^1]: From a grammatical perspective, "al-Kawakib" is a substitute (badal) for "zinat," and there is also the possibility that it is an explanatory apposition (atf al-bayan). "Zinat" here carries the meaning of a verbal noun (ism masdar) rather than the infinitive. It is mentioned in literary books that whenever an indefinite noun is a substitute for a definite one, it must be accompanied by a description, but the reverse is not necessary (take note).

However, as we know, the falsehood of this hypothesis has been proven, and the Quran's refusal to follow the famous incorrect hypothesis of its time is a living miracle of this Heavenly Book (take note).

Another interesting point is that from the perspective of modern science, it is certain that the beautiful twinkling of the stars is due to the layer of air surrounding the Earth, which compels them to do so. This is highly consistent with the expression "the lower heaven" (السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا). Outside the Earth's atmosphere, however, the stars stare fixedly and lack that shimmering radiance.

In the next verse, referring to the protection of the celestial scene from the infiltration of satans, It says: "And [We protected it] from every rebellious satan, devoid of good" (وَ حِفْظاً مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْطانٍ مارِدٍ). [^1] "Marid" is derived from the root "mard" (rhyming with sard), which originally means a high land devoid of any vegetation. A tree stripped of its leaves is also called "amrad," and for the same reason, this word is applied to a youth who has no facial hair. Here, "marid" refers to one who is devoid of any good or blessing—or in our own expression, "a good-for-nothing"!

We know that one of the ways the sky is protected from the ascent of satans is by a group of stars called "meteors" (shuhub), which will be mentioned in the following verses.

[^1]: According to many commentators, "hifzan" is an absolute object (maf'ul mutlaq) for an omitted verb, and the underlying structure is wa hafaznaha hifzan. Some have also suggested it is conjoined to the position of "bi-zinatin" which is a "maf'ul lahu" (object of purpose), making the underlying meaning: "We created the stars as an adornment for the sky and for protection."

Then It adds: "They cannot listen to the [speech of the] angels of the Exalted Assembly, nor hear the secrets of the Unseen from them; and whenever they attempt such a thing, they are pelted from every side" (لا يَسَّمَّعُونَ إِلَى الْمَلَإِ الْأَعْلى‌ وَ يُقْذَفُونَ مِنْ كُلِّ جانِبٍ).

Yes, "They are driven back forcefully, and expelled from the celestial scene, and for them is a perpetual torment" (دُحُوراً وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ واصِبٌ). "La yassamma'un" (which means la yatassamma'un) implies that they want to hear the news of the "Exalted Assembly" but they are not permitted to do so.

"The Exalted Assembly" (al-Mala' al-A'la) refers to the angels of the higher world. This is because "Mala'" originally refers to a community or group that agrees upon a single opinion and fills the eyes of others with this harmony and unity. The nobles, dignitaries, and those surrounding centers of power are also called "Mala'" because their outward appearance is eye-catching. However, when described as "Exalted" (A'la), it refers to the noble and high-ranking angels of the Truth.

"Yuqdhafun" is from the root "qadhf," meaning to hurl or throw toward a distant place. What is meant here is the expulsion of "satans" by means of "meteors" (shuhub), which we will explain and interpret later. This shows that Allah does not even allow them to approach the territory of the Exalted Assembly.

"Duḥūran" is from the root "daḥr" (rhyming with dahr), meaning to drive away and distance. "Wāṣib" originally refers to chronic illnesses, but generally, it means permanent and continuous, and sometimes it has been used to mean "pure." [^1]

[^1]: The meaning of "Wāṣib" was also discussed in Volume 11, page 261, under verse 52 of Surah An-Nahl.

Here, it indicates that not only are the satans prevented and expelled from approaching the celestial arena, but they will ultimately be afflicted with a permanent torment as well.

In the last verse under discussion, it refers to a group of rebellious and audacious satans who intend to ascend to the high reaches of the sky, saying: "Except him who snatches a fragment [of a secret] in a brief moment, then he is pursued by a piercing flame!" (إِلَّا مَنْ خَطِفَ الْخَطْفَةَ فَأَتْبَعَهُ شِهابٌ ثاقِبٌ). "Khatfah" means to snatch something quickly.

"Shihab" originally means a flame that leaps from a blazing fire, and it is also applied to the fiery streaks seen in the sky as extended lines.

We know these are not stars, but they resemble stars; they are small pieces of rock scattered in space, and when they enter the Earth's gravitational field, they are pulled toward the Earth. Due to the speed and intensity of their collision with the air surrounding the Earth, they ignite and become incandescent.

"Thaqib" means penetrating or piercing. It is as if, due to its intense light, it pierces the surface of the eye and penetrates into the human vision. Here, it indicates that it pierces and burns whatever it strikes.

Thus, there are two types of barriers against the infiltration of satans into the celestial scene:

The first barrier is the pelting and expulsion from every side, which apparently also occurs by means of "meteors" (shuhub).

The second barrier consists of a specific type of meteor called the "piercing flame" (shihab thaqib), which lies in wait for those who occasionally approach the "Exalted Assembly" for eavesdropping and are struck by it.

A similar meaning appears in verses 17 and 18 of Surah Al-Hijr, where It says: "And We have guarded it from every accursed satan, except one who steals a hearing and is pursued by a clear burning flame" (وَ حَفِظْناها مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْطانٍ رَجِيمٍ إِلَّا مَنِ اسْتَرَقَ السَّمْعَ فَأَتْبَعَهُ شِهابٌ مُبِينٌ). A similar expression also appears in Surah Al-Mulk, verse 5: "And We have certainly adorned the nearest heaven with lamps and have made [some of] them missiles for the satans" (وَ لَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِمَصابِيحَ وَ جَعَلْناها رُجُوماً لِلشَّياطِينِ).

Further Explanation and Completion

Regarding whether we should maintain the literal meanings of these terms or if there are indications that we should interpret them contrary to their literal sense—viewing them as parables, metaphors, or allegories—there are different views among commentators.

Some have maintained the literal sense of these verses as they first appear and have said: In the near and distant heavens, there are groups of angels residing, and the news of the events of this world is reflected there before they take shape on Earth.

A group of satans wants to ascend to the heavens and, by eavesdropping, learn something of that news and transmit it to the soothsayers—their related agents among humans. It is here that the meteors, which are like moving and elongated stars, are hurled at them, driving them back or destroying them.

They say: We may not precisely understand the concepts of these expressions today, but we are duty-bound to preserve their literal meanings and leave further information to the future.

This interpretation was chosen by the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, Sayyid Qutb in Fi Zilal, and some others.

Meanwhile, others believe the above verses are similar to those that speak of the "Tablet" (Lawh), the "Pen" (Qalam), the "Throne" (Arsh), and the "Pedestal" (Kursi), and are of the nature of parables and allegories.

They believe these verses are of the type of likening the "intellectual" (ma'qul) to the "sensible" (mahsus), and are an instance of verse 43 of Surah Al-Ankabut which says: "And these examples We present to the people, but none will understand them except those of knowledge" (وَ تِلْكَ الْأَمْثالُ نَضْرِبُها لِلنَّاسِ وَ ما يَعْقِلُها إِلَّا الْعالِمُونَ). They then added: The heavens where the angels reside refer to the realms of the Malakut (the celestial kingdom), whose horizon is higher than this sensory world. The "approaching" of the satans to the heaven, their "eavesdropping," and their being "expelled by meteors" means that whenever these satans want to approach the world of angels to become aware of the secrets of creation and future events, they are repelled by the light of the Malakut, which they lack the capacity to endure, and their falsehoods are negated by the Truth. They consider the mention of this event following the discussion of the groups of angels at the beginning of this Surah as supporting this meaning. [^1] There is also the possibility that "Heaven" (Sama') here is a metaphor for the heaven of faith and spirituality, which satans constantly strive to enter, attempting to penetrate the hearts of true believers through whispering (waswasah). However, the Divine Prophets, the Infallible Imams (peace be upon them), and the followers of their intellectual and practical path attack them with the "piercing flame" of knowledge and piety, preventing them from approaching this heaven.

We present this interpretation here only as "a possibility," and we have mentioned indications and evidence for it in Volume 11 under verse 18 of Surah Al-Hijr (for more explanation regarding these indications, refer to pages 40-51 of Volume 11).

These were three different interpretations regarding the meaning of these verses of the Holy Quran and similar verses.

[^1]: Summarized from Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 17, p. 130.

Ayahs

فَٱسْتَفْتِهِمْ أَهُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقًا أَم مَّنْ خَلَقْنَآ ۚ إِنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُم مِّن طِينٍ لَّازِبٍۭ (١١)
Ask them, is their creation more prodigious or [that of other creatures] that We have created? Indeed, We created them from a viscous clay. (11)
بَلْ عَجِبْتَ وَيَسْخَرُونَ (١٢)
Indeed you wonder, while they engage in ridicule, (12)
وَإِذَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ لَا يَذْكُرُونَ (١٣)
and [even] when admonished do not take admonition, (13)
وَإِذَا رَأَوْا۟ ءَايَةً يَسْتَسْخِرُونَ (١٤)
and when they see a sign they make it an object of ridicule, (14)
وَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٥)
and say, ‘This is nothing but plain magic!’ (15)

Commentary

Those Who Will Never Accept the Truth

These verses continue to pursue the issue of the Resurrection and the opposition of the stubborn deniers. Following the previous discussion regarding the power of Allah and the Creator of the heavens and the earth over all things, He says: "Ask them, is their creation and resurrection more difficult, or the creation of the angels, the heavens, and the earth?!" (فَاسْتَفْتِهِمْ أَ هُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقاً أَمْ مَنْ خَلَقْنا).

Indeed, "We created them from a simple matter, from sticky clay!" (إِنَّا خَلَقْناهُمْ مِنْ طِينٍ لازِبٍ‌).

It seems that the polytheists who denied the Resurrection, after hearing the previous verses regarding the creation of the heavens, the earth, and the angels, claimed that their own creation was more significant.

In response to them, the Quran says: The creation of human beings is not a significant matter compared to the creation of the vast earth and heavens and the angels who inhabit these realms, for the origin of human creation was nothing more than a handful of sticky clay.

"Istaftehim" is derived from the root "Istifta’," which originally means to request new information. The reason a youth is called "fata" is also due to the freshness of his body and soul [1]. This expression hints that if they truly consider their own creation more significant and more robust than the creation of the heavens and the angels, they are asserting something novel and unprecedented.

The word "lazib," according to some, was originally "lazim" (necessary/attached), where the "m" was transformed into "b," and it is now used in this form. In any case, it refers to clay whose parts are inseparable from one another—that is, sticky. This is because the origin of human creation was first "dust," then it was mixed with "water," gradually becoming "foul-smelling mud," and finally turning into "sticky clay" (and with this explanation, the various descriptions in the verses of the Holy Quran are reconciled).

[1]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, under the verse being discussed.

Then it adds: "You wonder at their denial of the Resurrection, yet they mock it" (بَلْ عَجِبْتَ وَ يَسْخَرُونَ‌). With your pure heart, you see the matter so clearly that you are sunk in wonder at their denial, whereas these foul-hearted individuals consider it so impossible that they resort to mockery.

The cause of these wicked deeds is not merely ignorance, but rather stubbornness and malice; therefore, "when they are reminded—reminded of the proofs of the Resurrection and Divine punishment—they never take heed" and continue on their path (وَ إِذا ذُكِّرُوا لا يَذْكُرُونَ‌).

Even beyond this, "whenever they see a miracle among your miracles, they not only engage in mockery and ridicule but also incite others to mock!" (وَ إِذا رَأَوْا آيَةً يَسْتَسْخِرُونَ‌).

"And they say, 'This is nothing but manifest magic!'" (وَ قالُوا إِنْ هذا إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُبِينٌ‌). Their use of the word "hatha" (this) is intended to belittle and show the worthlessness of the miracles and Divine signs. Their use of the word "sihr" (magic) was because, on one hand, the extraordinary acts of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله could not be denied, and on the other hand, they did not want to submit to them as a miracle. The only word that could reflect their mischief and satisfy their whims was this word "magic," which simultaneously demonstrates the enemy's admission of the amazing and extraordinary influence of the Quran and the miracles of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Key Points

  1. According to a group of commentators, the phrase "yastaskhirun" is used in the sense of "yaskharun" (they mock), and there is no difference between these two expressions. However, others posit a different meaning for the two: they consider "yastaskhirun"—due to the concept inherent in the Istif'al form—to mean "inviting others to mock." This points to the fact that they not only ridiculed the Divine signs themselves but also exerted effort to make others do the same, so that the matter would become a general trend in society.

Some believe the difference between the two lies in the greater emphasis derived from the phrase "yastaskhirun."

Others have interpreted this phrase as "believing something to be a mockery"; that is, due to their severe deviation, they truly believed that these miracles were nothing more than a joke! However, the second meaning seems the most appropriate of all.

  1. Some commentators have also cited a "Reason for Revelation" (Shan al-Nuzul) for the above verse, the summary of which is: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله met one of the polytheists named "Rukanah" in one of the mountains surrounding Mecca while he was alone. Although Rukanah was among the strongest and most powerful people of Mecca, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله threw him to the ground to show him that he possessed miraculous power—since by ordinary means, the opponent's victory was certain. Then he showed him some of his other miracles, which alone were sufficient for his guidance. However, he not only failed to believe but came to Mecca and shouted: "O Banu Hashim! Your companion is so powerful in magic that you can confront all the magicians of the earth through him." The above verses were revealed concerning him and those like him [1].

[1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 23, p. 71.

Ayahs

أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ (١٦)
‘What! When we are dead and have become dust and bones, shall we be resurrected? (16)
أَوَءَابَآؤُنَا ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ (١٧)
And our forefathers too?!’ (17)
قُلْ نَعَمْ وَأَنتُمْ دَٰخِرُونَ (١٨)
Say, ‘Yes! And you will be utterly humble.’ (18)
فَإِنَّمَا هِىَ زَجْرَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ فَإِذَا هُمْ يَنظُرُونَ (١٩)
It will be only a single shout and, behold, they will look on, (19)
وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَا هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ (٢٠)
and say, ‘Woe to us! This is the Day of Retribution!’ (20)
هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ ٱلْفَصْلِ ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ (٢١)
‘This is the Day of Judgement that you used to deny!’ (21)
۞ ٱحْشُرُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ وَأَزْوَٰجَهُمْ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْبُدُونَ (٢٢)
‘Muster the wrongdoers and their mates and what they used to worship (22)
مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ فَٱهْدُوهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٢٣)
besides Allah, and show them the way to hell! (23)

Commentary

Do We and Our Forefathers Truly Get Resurrected?

These verses continue the dialogue of the deniers of the Resurrection and the responses given to them.

The first verse reflects the sense of improbability felt by the deniers of the Resurrection in this manner:

They used to say: "When we have died and become dust and bones, shall we indeed be resurrected?" (أَ إِذا مِتْنا وَ كُنَّا تُراباً وَ عِظاماً أَ إِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ 1)

And even more than this: "And our forefathers of old as well?" (أَ وَ آباؤُنَا الْأَوَّلُونَ). Those of whom nothing remains but a handful of decayed bones or scattered dust—who can gather these dispersed particles? And who can clothe them in the garment of life?

However, these blind-hearted people had forgotten that on the first day, they were all dust and were created from dust. If they doubted the power of Allah, they should know that Allah had already demonstrated His power to them once. And if they were hesitant about the potential of dust, that too—

1: This verse is in the form of a conditional sentence where "أَ إِذا مِتْنا..." forms the condition (shart), and its result (jaza) is omitted. The phrase "أَ إِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ" serves as an indicator for it, because according to grammatical rules, this phrase itself cannot be the result.

—had been proven once before. Furthermore, the creation of the heavens and the earth with all their grandeur leaves no room for anyone to doubt the infinite power of the Truth (Allah).

It is noteworthy that in their state of denial, they emphasized their statements with various forms of reinforcement (since the phrase "أَ إِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ" is a "nominal sentence" [jumlah ismiyyah], and both "inna" and the "lam" of emphasis are used within it). This was due to their ignorance and stubbornness.

Another point worthy of attention is that "turab" (dust) is mentioned before "izam" (bones) in the above verse. This may point to one of three nuances:

First, although a human after death initially becomes bones and then dust, since the return of dust to life is more wondrous, it is mentioned first.

Second, when the bodies of the dead decompose, the flesh first turns into dust and settles beside the bones; therefore, at any single moment, it is both dust and bones.

Third, "dust" refers to the bodies of distant ancestors, while "bones" refers to the bodies of fathers which have not yet completely turned into dust.

Then, the Quran provides the most crushing response to them, saying to the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Say to them: Yes, and you shall be brought forth while you are humiliated and belittled!" (قُلْ نَعَمْ وَ أَنْتُمْ داخِرُونَ 1)

Do you imagine that reviving you and all those who came before is a difficult task for the All-Powerful and Capable Allah, or a heavy and momentous undertaking? No, "it is but a single shout and a great cry from the agent of the Lord; suddenly they all rise from their graves, regain life, and with their own eyes, they behold the scene of the Gathering which they used to deny until that day!" (فَإِنَّما هِيَ زَجْرَةٌ واحِدَةٌ فَإِذا هُمْ يَنْظُرُونَ).

"Zajrah" is from the root "zajr." As we have previously mentioned, it sometimes means "to drive away" and sometimes means "a shout." Here, the second meaning is intended, referring to the second blast and the second shout of Israfil, the details of which will—Allah willing—come under the commentary of the verses of Surah Az-Zumar.

The phrase "yanzurun" (they look) refers to them staring wide-eyed at the arena of the Gathering, or looking in expectation of punishment. In any case, the meaning is that they not only come back to life but regain their perception and sight with that single shout! The expression "a single shout" (zajratun wahidah), considering the content of these two words, points to the speed and suddenness of the Resurrection and its ease before the power of Allah—that with one authoritative cry from the "Angel of Resurrection," everything is set in motion!

It is here that the wailing of these arrogant and headstrong polytheists—which is a sign of their weakness, abjectness, and misery—rises: "And they say: Woe to us! This is the Day of Recompense!" (وَ قالُوا يا وَيْلَنا هذا يَوْمُ الدِّينِ). Yes, when their eyes fall upon the court of Divine Justice, the witnesses and judges of this court, and the signs and tokens of punishment, they involuntarily break into wailing and shouting.

With their entire being, they confess to the truth of the Resurrection—a confession that can solve no problem for them, nor bring the slightest reduction in their punishment.

It is here that they are addressed by Allah or His angels: "Yes, today is the Day of Sorting which you used to deny"—the sorting of truth from falsehood, the separation of the ranks of the evildoers from the righteous, and the day of judgment of the Great Lord (هذا يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تُكَذِّبُونَ).

Similar expressions are seen in other verses of the Quran where the Day of Resurrection is referred to as "Yawm al-Fasl" or the Day of Sorting. What a wondrous, expressive, and terrifying description! 1 It is noteworthy that the disbelievers, when speaking about that day in the Hereafter, refer to it as the Day of Recompense (يا وَيْلَنا هذا يَوْمُ الدِّينِ).

However, Allah refers to it as the "Day of Sorting" (هذا يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ).

This difference in expression may be because the criminals think only of their retribution and punishment, but Allah points to a broader meaning of which punishment is only one dimension: that the Day of Resurrection is the day of separations. Yes, the day of separating the ranks of the wicked from the righteous, as stated in Surah Ya-Sin, verse 59: "And stand apart today, O you criminals!"—meaning: "O criminals, separate yourselves from others, for this is not the world where the evildoers can mingle themselves into the ranks of the servants of Allah." And how painful it is for them to witness their believing relatives and children being separated from them and taking the path to Paradise.

Furthermore, that day is the day of separating truth from falsehood. Conflicting schools of thought, opposing paths, and programs—

1: Ad-Dukhan: 40; Al-Mursalat: 13, 14, and 38; An-Naba: 17.

—true and false are not mixed together as they are in the world. Each must be placed in its proper station.

Above all, that day is the Day of Sorting in the sense of the Day of Judgment, and the All-Knowing and Just Allah, in the position of judgment, issues the most precise verdict regarding His servants. It is here that comprehensive disgrace is prepared for the polytheists.

In short, the nature of this world is the blending and mixing of truth and falsehood, whereas the nature of the Resurrection is the nature of separating these two from one another. For this reason, one of the names of the Resurrection in the Holy Quran, repeated many times, is "Yawm al-Fasl." Fundamentally, on a day when all secrets are made manifest, the separation of ranks is inevitable.

Then, Allah commands the angels commissioned to drive the criminals toward Hell: "Gather those who did wrong and their associates, and what they used to worship" (احْشُرُوا الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا وَ أَزْواجَهُمْ وَ ما كانُوا يَعْبُدُونَ).

Yes, "move whatever they worshipped besides Allah and guide them to the path of the Blazing Fire!" (مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ فَاهْدُوهُمْ إِلى‌ صِراطِ الْجَحِيمِ). "Uhshuru" is from the root "hashr," which according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat means removing a group from their place and dispatching them to a battlefield or the like.

This word is also used in many instances to mean "gathering."

In any case, this speech is either from Allah or from one group of angels to another group commissioned to gather and move the criminals toward Hell, and the result is the same.

"Azwaj" (associates/spouses) here either refers to their criminal and idol-worshipping spouses, or their like-minded peers, colleagues, and counterparts, as this word carries both meanings. As we read in Surah Al-Waqi'ah, verse 7: "And you become three kinds (azwajan)"—meaning: "On the Day of Resurrection, you will be divided into three groups."

Therefore, the polytheists will be dispatched toward Hell in ranks with other polytheists, and the evildoers and dark-hearted with their likes and counterparts.

Or it may refer to the devils who were similar to them in form and action.

At the same time, these three meanings are not mutually exclusive and may all be contained within the concept of the verse.

The phrase "what they used to worship" refers to the deities of the polytheists, including idols, devils, and tyrannical humans such as the Pharaohs and Nimrods. The use of "ma" (what/that which) in "what they used to worship" might be because most of their deities were inanimate and non-rational beings, and this expression is used by way of "taghlib" (predominance).

"Jahim," meaning Hell, is derived from the root "jahmah," meaning "the intensity of the flare of a fire."

Interestingly, He uses the expression "guide them to the path of the Blazing Fire." What a strange phrase! One day they were guided toward the "Straight Path" (Sirat al-Mustaqim) but did not accept it; today, however, they must be guided to the "Path of the Blazing Fire" (Sirat al-Jahim) and are forced to accept it! This is a heavy reproach that burns the depths of their souls.

Ayahs

وَقِفُوهُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُم مَّسْـُٔولُونَ (٢٤)
[But first] stop them! For they must be questioned.’ (24)
مَا لَكُمْ لَا تَنَاصَرُونَ (٢٥)
‘Why is it that you do not support one another [today]?’ (25)
بَلْ هُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ مُسْتَسْلِمُونَ (٢٦)
‘Indeed, they are [meek and] submissive today!’ (26)

They will say, ‘Indeed you used to accost us peremptorily.’ (28)
قَالُوا۟ بَل لَّمْ تَكُونُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ (٢٩)
They will answer, ‘No, you [yourselves] had no faith. (29)
وَمَا كَانَ لَنَا عَلَيْكُم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍۭ ۖ بَلْ كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا طَـٰغِينَ (٣٠)

فَحَقَّ عَلَيْنَا قَوْلُ رَبِّنَآ ۖ إِنَّا لَذَآئِقُونَ (٣١)

Commentary

The Dialogue Between Misguided Leaders and Followers in Hell

As we learned in the previous verses, the angels of punishment herd the oppressors and their like-minded associates, along with their idols and false deities, together and guide them toward the path of Hell.

Continuing this discourse, the Quran says: At this moment, a command is issued: "Stop them, for they must be questioned" (وَ قِفُوهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ مَسْؤُلُونَ) [^1]. Yes, they must be detained and answer various questions.

But what will they be questioned about?

Some have said: About the innovations (bid'ah) they established.

Others have said: About their evil deeds and errors.

[^1]: "Qifūhum" is derived from the root "waqf." Sometimes it is used transitively (to hold or detain) and sometimes intransitively (to stop or stand). The infinitive of the first is waqf and the second is wuqūf.

Some have added: About Monotheism (Tawhid) and "La ilaha illa Allah."

Some have said: About blessings—youth, health, lifespan, wealth, and the like.

And in a famous narration reported through both Sunni and Shi'a chains, it is stated that they will be questioned about "the Wilayah (guardianship) of Ali عليه السلام" [^1]. Of course, these interpretations do not contradict one another, for on that Day, everything will be questioned: beliefs, Monotheism, Wilayah, words, deeds, and the blessings and favors that Allah placed at man's disposal.

Here, a question arises: How is it that they are first taken toward the path of Hell and then stopped for questioning? Should the questioning and judgment not precede this action?

This question can be answered in two ways:

First, the fact that this group belongs in Hell is clear to everyone, even to themselves; the questioning and inquiry are meant to clarify the extent and degree of their crimes for them.

Second, these questions are not for the sake of passing judgment, but rather a form of rebuke and psychological punishment.

Of course, all of this applies if the questions relate to what we mentioned above. However, if they relate to the subsequent verse where they are asked, "Why do you not help one another?", then no difficulty remains in the verse. Yet, this interpretation is not consistent with the numerous narrations transmitted regarding this matter, unless this question

[^1]: This narration is recorded in Al-Sawa'iq from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and also by Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani in Shawahid al-Tanzil from him. It is also narrated in Uyun Akhbar al-Rida from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام.

is also considered one part of the various questions posed to them (reflect on this).

In any case, when these wretched denizens of Hell are guided toward the path of Jahannam, they find themselves cut off from all help. They are told: "You who used to seek refuge in one another during hardships in the world and sought help from each other, 'Why do you not help one another here?'" (ما لَكُمْ لا تَناصَرُونَ). Indeed, all the supports you imagined for yourselves in the world have crumbled here; you can neither help one another, nor will your deities rush to your aid, for they themselves are helpless and ensnared.

It is said that Abu Jahl shouted on the day of Badr: Nahnu jami'un muntasir ("We are all together victorious over the Muslims"), a statement the Holy Quran recounts in Surah Al-Qamar, verse 44: "أَمْ يَقُولُونَ نَحْنُ جَمِيعٌ مُنْتَصِرٌ". But on the Day of Resurrection, the Abu Jahls and those like him will be asked: "Why do you not rise to help one another?" They will have no answer to this question and will do nothing but maintain a humiliating silence.

In the next verse, it adds: "Nay, but that day they shall be in submission" (بَلْ هُمُ الْيَوْمَ مُسْتَسْلِمُونَ) [^1]. They are humble before the command of Allah and possess no power to assert themselves, let alone oppose Him.

It is here that they turn to rebuking one another, each insisting on

[^1]: "Istislam" is from the root "salamah." According to the form of Istif'al, it means seeking safety, which is usually accompanied by submission and humility when facing a great power.

throwing the burden of their sin onto the other. The followers blame their leaders and chiefs, and the leaders blame their followers, as the next verse says: "And they will turn to one another, questioning each other" (وَ أَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ).

The misguided followers say to their misguiding leaders: "You devilish ones used to come to us from the right, under the guise of advice, goodwill, and compassion, and in the name of guidance and direction" (قالُوا إِنَّكُمْ كُنْتُمْ تَأْتُونَنا عَنِ الْيَمِينِ). But there was nothing in your work but deceit and trickery! "We, who by the dictate of our primordial nature (fitrah) sought goodness, purity, and salvation, responded to your call, unaware of the demonic nature hidden behind that benevolent face that dragged us to the precipice of misery. Yes, all our sins are on your necks; we had no capital but good intentions and sincerity, while you lying demons had nothing in your store but deception and guile!"

The word "Yamin," which means "right hand" or "right side," is sometimes used among Arabs as a metaphor for goodness, blessing, and sincere advice. Originally, the Arabs took whatever came to them from the right side as a "good omen." Therefore, many commentators have interpreted the phrase "كُنْتُمْ تَأْتُونَنا عَنِ الْيَمِينِ" as an outward display of goodwill and advice, as mentioned above.

In any case, it is a general cultural concept to consider the right limb and the right side as noble, and the left as ignoble, and this has caused "Yamin" to be used for virtues and good things.

A group of commentators has mentioned another interpretation here, saying it means:

"You used to come to us relying on power," because usually the right side is stronger; for this reason, most people perform important tasks with the right hand, so this expression became a metaphor for "power."

Other interpretations have also been mentioned which revert to the two above, but undoubtedly the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

In any case, their leaders will not remain silent either, and in response, "They will say: Nay, you yourselves were not believers!" (قالُوا بَلْ لَمْ تَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ).

"If your temperament were not ready for deviation, if you yourselves were not seekers of evil and devilry, where would you have come to us? Why did you not respond to the call of the prophets and the righteous and pure? Yet as soon as we made a single gesture, you came running? Thus, it is clear the fault lies within yourselves; go and blame yourselves, and send whatever curses and imprecations you have upon yourselves!"

"Our proof is clear: 'We had no authority over you,' and there was no force or compulsion involved!" (وَ ما كانَ لَنا عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ). "Rather, you were a people who were rebellious and transgressive," and your own character of oppression caused your misery (بَلْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً طاغِينَ).

How painful it is for a person to see that the leader and guide to whom he had devoted his heart for a lifetime has brought about his misery, and then disavows him in this manner, throwing all the guilt upon him and completely exonerating himself! The truth is that each of these two groups is right in one respect; neither these are innocent, nor are those. From the leaders came seduction and devilry, and from the followers came receptivity to seduction

and submission!

Therefore, these dialogues lead nowhere, and finally, these misguided leaders confess to this reality and say: "So the word of our Lord has been realized against us; indeed, we are all about to taste [the punishment]" (فَحَقَّ عَلَيْنا قَوْلُ رَبِّنا إِنَّا لَذائِقُونَ). "You were rebellious, and such is the fate of the rebellious; and we were both misguided and misguiding."

"We led you astray, just as we ourselves were astray" (فأَغْوَيْناكُمْ إِنَّا كُنَّا غاوِينَ). Therefore, what cause for surprise is there that we are all partners in these calamities and punishments?

Key Points

1- The Wilayah of Ali عليه السلام Will Also Be Questioned

As we previously noted, numerous narrations have been recorded in both Shi'a and Sunni sources regarding the interpretation of the verse "وَ قِفُوهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ مَسْؤُلُونَ," indicating that among the matters the criminals will be questioned about that Day is the Wilayah of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام.

Sheikh Tusi in Al-Amali narrates from Anas ibn Malik from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

"When the Day of Resurrection comes and the Bridge (Sirat) is set over Hell, no one shall pass over it unless he possesses a permit containing the Wilayah of Ali ibn Abi Talib, and that is the word of Allah the Exalted: 'وَ قِفُوهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ مَسْؤُلُونَ,' meaning about the Wilayah of Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام." [^1]

In many Sunni books as well, the interpretation of this verse as being questioned about the Wilayah of Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام has been narrated from Ibn Abbas and Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. Among the scholars who have narrated this hadith are:

  • Ibn Hajar al-Haythami in Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah (page 147).
  • Abd al-Razzaq al-Hanbali (as cited in Kashf al-Ghummah, page 92).
  • Allamah Sibt ibn al-Jawzi in Tadhkirah (page 21).
  • Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani under the verse in question.
  • Abu Nu'aym al-Isfahani (as cited in Kifayat al-Khisal, page 360).
  • And a group of others. [^2]

Of course, as we have said many times, such narrations do not limit the broad meaning of the verses, but rather reflect the clearest instances of the verses. Therefore, there is no obstacle to being questioned about all beliefs, but since the issue of Wilayah holds a special position in the discussion of beliefs, it has been specifically emphasized.

It is also noteworthy that Wilayah does not mean a simple friendship or a dry belief; rather, the goal is the acceptance of the leadership of Ali عليه السلام in matters of belief, practice, ethics, and society after the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله—matters that are reflected in the eloquent sermons of Nahj al-Balagha and the words narrated from him عليه السلام.

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, page 401.

[^2]: For more information in this regard, refer to the precious book Ihqaq al-Haqq, Vol. 3 (new edition), page 104, and Al-Muraja'at, page 58 (Letter 12).

These are matters where faith in them and harmonizing one's actions with them are effective means for exiting the ranks of the denizens of Hell and being placed upon the Straight Path of the Lord.

2- Misguided Leaders and Followers!

In the above verses and other verses of the Holy Quran, there are meaningful references to the dispute between misguided leaders and followers on the Day of Resurrection or in Hell.

This is an instructive warning to all those who place their intellect and religion at the disposal of misguided leaders.

On that Day, although each will try to disavow the other and even throw their own sin onto them, nonetheless, neither is capable of proving their innocence.

In the verses above, we saw that the seductive leaders explicitly say to their followers: "The factor of our influence over you was your own rebellious spirit: 'بَلْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً طاغِينَ'."

"This rebelliousness provided the grounds for your susceptibility to our seduction, and we were able to transfer the deviations we had to you through this path: 'فَأَغْوَيْناكُمْ إِنَّا كُنَّا غاوِينَ'."

Attention to the precise meaning of "Ighwa" (seduction), which is from the root "Ghayy," makes the matter clearer. For "Ghayy," as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means ignorance that stems from a corrupt belief. These misguided leaders remained ignorant of the realities of existence and life, and they transferred this ignorance and corrupt belief to their followers who possessed a spirit of rebellion against Allah's command.

For this reason, they confess there that both they themselves deserve punishment and so do their followers: "فَحَقَّ عَلَيْنا قَوْلُ رَبِّنا إِنَّا لَذائِقُونَ." Especially the emphasis on the word

"Rabb" (Lord) is meaningful; it means that a person's state reaches a point where Allah—who is his Owner and Nurturer and desires nothing but his good and salvation—subjects him to His painful punishment. And of course, this too is among the affairs of His Lordship.

In any case, that Day is truly the "Day of Regret" (Yawm al-Hasrah); it is a day when both the misguiding leaders and their misguided followers will regret their programs, but what use is it when there is no way to return?

Indeed you will taste the painful punishment, (38)

Commentary

The Fate of These Leaders and Those Followers

Following the description of the confrontation between the misled followers and their leaders on the Day of Resurrection by the side of Hell—as mentioned in the previous verses—the verses under discussion present the fate of both groups together and explain the causes of their misery, serving as both a diagnosis of the ailment and a mention of the cure.

First, He says: "Indeed, that Day they—the followers and the followed, the leaders and the led—will be sharers in the divine punishment" (فَإِنَّهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ فِي الْعَذابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ‌).

Of course, their sharing in the essence of the punishment does not preclude differences and variations in their levels (darakat) within Hell and the divine torment; for certainly, one who has caused the deviation of thousands of humans will never face a punishment equal to that of an ordinary misled individual.

This verse is, in fact, similar to verse 48 of Surah Ghafir, where the arrogant ones say to those who were oppressed in their beliefs after their argument and dispute: "Indeed, we are all in it [Hell], for Allah has judged between His servants" (قالَ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا إِنَّا كُلٌّ فِيها إِنَّ اللَّهَ قَدْ حَكَمَ بَيْنَ الْعِبادِ). This does not contradict verse 13 of Surah Al-Ankabut, which states: وَ لَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقالَهُمْ وَ أَثْقالًا مَعَ أَثْقالِهِمْ‌: "They will surely carry their own heavy burdens on the Day of Resurrection, and other burdens along with their own heavy burdens"—burdens resulting from their seduction and misleading of others, and their encouragement of sin and innovation (bid'ah).

Then, for further emphasis, He adds: "Indeed, thus do We deal with the criminals" (إِنَّا كَذلِكَ نَفْعَلُ بِالْمُجْرِمِينَ‌).

This is Our perpetual Sunnah (way), a Sunnah that originates from the law of justice.

Next, He proceeds to explain the root cause of their misery, saying: "Indeed, they were such that when it was said to them, 'There is no god but Allah,' they were arrogant" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ‌). Yes, the root of all their deviations was arrogance and self-superiority, refusing to submit to the truth, and persisting with stubbornness in false traditions and vain imitations, looking down upon everything else with contempt.

The opposite of the spirit of arrogance is humility and submission before the truth, which is the essence of true Islam and nothing else. That arrogance is the source of wretchedness, while this humility and submission is the leaven of felicity.

It is interesting that in some verses of the Quran, divine punishment is directly linked to arrogance, as we read in verse 20 of Surah Al-Ahqaf: "So today you will be recompensed with the punishment of humiliation because you were arrogant upon the earth without right" (فَالْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ عَذابَ الْهُونِ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَسْتَكْبِرُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِ‌).

However, for this great sin of theirs, they offered an excuse worse than the sin itself, "and they would say: 'Are we to abandon our gods for a mad poet?'" (وَ يَقُولُونَ أَ إِنَّا لَتارِكُوا آلِهَتِنا لِشاعِرٍ مَجْنُونٍ‌). They called him a poet because his words penetrated hearts so deeply and carried human emotions with them as if he were composing the most rhythmic poetry, even though his speech was not poetry at all. And they called him mad because he did not take on the color of his environment and stood against the superstitious beliefs of a multitude of stubborn bigots—an act which, in the eyes of the misled masses, is a kind of insane suicide, whereas the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله greatest pride was that he did not surrender to these conditions!

Then, the Quran, to negate these baseless claims and defend the status of revelation and the messengership of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, adds: "Rather, he has come with the truth and confirmed the [previous] messengers" (بَلْ جاءَ بِالْحَقِّ وَ صَدَّقَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ‌). The content of his words on one hand, and their harmony with the call of the prophets on the other, is proof of the truthfulness of his speech.

"But you, O blind-hearted arrogant ones and foul-mouthed misled ones, will surely taste the painful punishment of Allah" (إِنَّكُمْ لَذائِقُوا الْعَذابِ الْأَلِيمِ‌).

But do not think that Allah is vengeful and seeks to take revenge for His Prophet upon you; it is not so: "And you will not be recompensed except for what you used to do" (وَ ما تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌). In reality, it is your own deeds that will be manifested before you, remain with you, and torture and torment you. Your punishment is your own action: that very arrogance, disbelief, and lack of faith; that very act of attributing poetry and madness to the divine verses and His Prophet; that very oppression, injustice, and wickedness.

In the final verse under discussion, which is actually an introduction to future discussions, He excludes one group, saying: "Except the chosen servants of Allah, who are far removed and exempt from all these punishments" (إِلَّا عِبادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ‌) [1]. The term "servants of Allah" (ibad Allah) alone is sufficient to express the connection of this group to God, but when "the chosen" (mukhlasin) is placed beside it, it gives it another depth and soul—and that too, "mukhlas" in the form of the passive participle (ism maf'ul): one whom Allah has purified and made sincere, pure from any kind of polytheism (shirk) or hypocrisy (riya), and from any satanic whispers or taints of carnal desires.

Yes, it is only this group who are not recompensed according to their deeds; rather, Allah treats them with grace and generosity, and they receive rewards without measure.

Key Point: The Reward of the Sincere (Mukhlasin)

A careful study of the Quranic verses shows that "mukhlis" (with a kasrah on the lam) is mostly used in cases where a person is in the stages of self-purification and has not yet reached the necessary perfection. However, "mukhlas" (with a fathah on the lam) refers to a stage where a person, after a period of struggle against the self (jihad al-nafs) and traversing the stages of gnosis and faith, reaches a station where they gain immunity from the influence of the whispers of the devils, as the Quran quotes Iblis saying:

"By Your might, I will surely mislead them all, except Your chosen servants among them!" (فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ‌) (Surah Sad, verses 82-83).

This phrase, which appears repeatedly in the verses of the Quran, clarifies the greatness of the station of the "mukhlasin." This is the station of the truthful Josephs after passing through that great field of trial: "Thus [it was] that We might avert from him evil and immorality. Indeed, he was of Our chosen servants" (كَذلِكَ لِنَصْرِفَ عَنْهُ السُّوءَ وَ الْفَحْشاءَ إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُخْلَصِينَ‌) (Surah Yusuf, verse 24).

This is the station of those who emerge victorious in the "Greater Jihad" (al-jihad al-akbar), and the hand of the Lord's grace removes all impurities from their existence. They melt in the furnace of events such that nothing remains in them but the pure gold of gnosis.

And it is here that their reward is not given by the measure of action, but rather their reward is by the measure of Allah's grace and mercy.

"Allamah Tabataba'i" has a statement here, the summary of which is:

"Allah says in the verse under discussion: All people will receive the reward of their deeds except the chosen servants of Allah.

Because, by virtue of their station of servitude (ubudiyyah), they do not consider themselves owners of anything; they do not intend except what Allah wills, and they do not perform except what He seeks.

And by virtue of being 'mukhlas' (chosen), Allah has selected them for Himself, and they have no attachment of the heart to anything other than His Pure Essence.

Neither the glitter of the world nor the blessings of the hereafter; in their hearts, there is nothing but Allah!

It is clear that one who possesses this characteristic, their pleasure, blessing, gift, and provision is something other than what others have, as it comes in the following verses: 'Those will have a known provision' (أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ رِزْقٌ مَعْلُومٌ‌)—they have a specific and special provision that is distinct from others.

It is true that they live in Paradise like other inhabitants of Paradise, but their portion bears no resemblance to the portions of others (they benefit from the manifestations of Allah's Pure Essence and His internal delights, and their hearts are overflowing from the cup of longing for Him, drowned in His love and union)." [2]

[1]: This phrase is a "disconnected exception" (istithna' munqati') from the pronoun in "tujzawn" or "la-dha'iqu." [2]: Al-Mizan, vol. 17, p. 141.

Ayahs

For such there is a known provision (41)

—fruits—and they will be held in honour, (42)
فِى جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٤٣)
in the gardens of bliss, (43)
عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مُّتَقَـٰبِلِينَ (٤٤)
[reclining] on couches, facing one another, (44)
يُطَافُ عَلَيْهِم بِكَأْسٍ مِّن مَّعِينٍۭ (٤٥)
served around with a cup, from a clear fountain, (45)
بَيْضَآءَ لَذَّةٍ لِّلشَّـٰرِبِينَ (٤٦)
