wherein there will be neither headache nor will it cause them stupefaction, (47)
وَعِندَهُمْ قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ عِينٌ (٤٨)
and with them will be maidens, of restrained glances with big [beautiful] eyes, (48)
كَأَنَّهُنَّ بَيْضٌ مَّكْنُونٌ (٤٩)
as if they were hidden ostrich eggs. (49)

Commentary

A Glimpse of Heavenly Bounties

In the final verse of the previous discussion, mention was made of the "sincere servants of Allah" (Ibad Allah al-Mukhlaseen). The verses under discussion describe the countless favors and blessings that Allah bestows upon them, which can be summarized into seven categories:

First, He says: "For them is a known and determined provision" (أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ رِزْقٌ مَعْلُومٌ).

Is this a summary of the same blessings detailed in the subsequent verses, expressed here in a concise manner? Or does it refer to unknown spiritual and indescribable blessings that stand at the pinnacle of heavenly bounties?

A group of commentators has interpreted it in the first sense, while others have taken the second view.

The context of the discussion and the comprehensiveness of the blessings are more consistent with the second meaning. Accordingly, the first of the seven blessings mentioned in these verses consists of spiritual favors, psychological pleasures, the perception of the manifestations of the Pure Essence of Truth, and becoming intoxicated by the pure wine of His love—a pleasure that no one can truly know until they experience it.

The reason why the material favors of Paradise are detailed in the verses of the Quran, while the spiritual favors and psychological pleasures are mentioned concisely, is that the former can be described, whereas the latter are beyond description!

Regarding the meaning of "known provision" (rizqun ma'lum), many other points have been raised: Is its timing known? Or its permanence and duration? Or its other characteristics? However, based on what we stated above, the word "known" is a concise expression for these indescribable favors.

Then, He proceeds to mention other blessings, naming heavenly favors before anything else—favors that are granted to the inhabitants of Paradise with the utmost respect. He says: "For them are various fruits" (فَواكِهُ). "And they are honored" (وَ هُمْ مُكْرَمُونَ).

They are not like animals before whom fodder is thrown; rather, they are treated like dear guests and are hosted with the highest degree of respect.

Moving past the blessing of various fruits and the honor and esteem, the discussion turns to their dwelling place. He says: "Their place is in the lush and bountiful gardens of Paradise" (فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ). Every blessing they desire is there, and whatever they intend is present before them.

Since one of the greatest human pleasures is enjoying the company of sincere and pure-hearted friends, He points to this blessing in the fourth stage: "The inhabitants of Paradise are on couches, facing one another" (عَلى‌ سُرُرٍ مُتَقابِلِينَ).

They converse about every topic—sometimes about their past in the world, sometimes about the great blessings of the Lord in the Hereafter, sometimes about the attributes of God's Beauty and Majesty, and sometimes about the stations of the Awliya (saints) and their miracles, and other matters that are incomprehensible to us, the prisoners of this world! "Surur" is the plural of "sarir," referring to thrones or couches used in gatherings of joy and intimacy, although it is sometimes used in a broader sense, even to refer to a deceased person's coffin, perhaps in the hope that it serves as a vessel of joy carrying them toward Divine forgiveness and His eternal Paradise.

In the fifth stage of describing the favors of the people of Paradise, He speaks of their pure drink and wine. He says: "Cups overflowing with pure wine are circulated around them," and whenever they wish, they are quenched by its measure and submerged in a world of vitality and spirituality (يُطافُ عَلَيْهِمْ بِكَأْسٍ مِنْ مَعِينٍ). These vessels are not placed in a corner for them to request a cup; rather, according to the expression "يُطافُ عَلَيْهِمْ" (circulated around them), they are brought to them! "Ka’s" (rhyming with ra’s) is defined by linguists as a vessel that is full and overflowing; if it is empty, it is usually called a "qadah." Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat:

"The ka’s is the vessel along with the drink inside it."

"Ma'in" is derived from the root "ma'n" (rhyming with sahn), meaning "flowing." This indicates that there are springs of pure wine flowing there, from which the cups are filled at every moment and circulated around the inhabitants of Paradise. It is not such that this pure wine would ever run out, nor does its preparation require any toil or pain, nor does it become stale, spoiled, or corrupted.

Then, describing the vessels of that pure wine, He says: "They are white and brilliant, and delicious for the drinkers" (بَيْضاءَ لَذَّةٍ لِلشَّارِبِينَ). Some commentators have taken "bayda" (white) as an adjective for the "vessels" of this wine, while others take it as a description of the "pure wine" itself—meaning this wine is not like the colored wines of the world; it is pure, free from satanic hues, white, and transparent. Certainly, the second meaning is more compatible with the phrase "لَذَّةٍ لِلشَّارِبِينَ" (delicious for the drinkers)!

Since the mention of wine, cups, and the like might evoke other concepts in the mind, He immediately washes these concepts from the listeners' minds in the next verse with a short and expressive phrase, saying: "That wine—the pure drink—is neither a cause of intellectual corruption, nor does it cause intoxication" (لا فِيها غَوْلٌ وَ لا هُمْ عَنْها يُنْزَفُونَ). There is nothing in it except alertness, vitality, and spiritual pleasure.

"Ghawl" (rhyming with qawl) originally means a corruption that secretly penetrates something. The fact that secret killings and assassinations are called "ghilah" in Arabic literature stems from this.

"Yunzafun" is originally from the root "nazf" (rhyming with hadhf), meaning to eliminate something gradually. When this word is used regarding well water, it means to draw the water gradually from the well until it is exhausted. The term "nazf al-dam" is also used for gradual bleeding that leads to the loss of all the body's blood.

In any case, what is meant in the verse under discussion is the gradual loss of intellect and reaching the state of intoxication, which absolutely does not exist regarding the pure wine of Paradise; it neither diminishes the intellect nor produces corruption.

These two expressions are implicitly a very subtle and precise description of worldly wines and alcoholic substances that penetrate the human being gradually and secretly, creating corruption and ruin. Not only do they destroy the intellect and the nervous system, but they also have an undeniable destructive effect on all organs of the human body—from the heart to the vessels, and from the stomach to the kidneys and liver—as if they "assassinate" and destroy the person. Furthermore, they gradually draw out the human's intellect and intelligence like well water until they leave it dry and empty! But the Divine pure wine in the Resurrection is free from all these qualities. [1]

Finally, in the sixth stage, He refers to the pure heavenly spouses, saying: "With them are spouses who love none but their husbands, who do not look at others, and who have large, beautiful eyes" (وَ عِنْدَهُمْ قاصِراتُ الطَّرْفِ عِينٌ).

"Tarf" originally means the eyelid, and since the eyelids move during the act of looking, this word is an allusion to "looking." Therefore, the expression "قاصِراتُ الطَّرْفِ" (those of modest gaze) means women who have a "shortened" gaze, and several possibilities have been suggested for its interpretation, which can all be true simultaneously:

First: They look only at their husbands, averting their eyes from everything else to gaze at them. Second: This expression is an allusion to the fact that they love only their husbands and hold no love for anyone else in their hearts—which is one of the greatest merits of a spouse: to think of no one but her husband and love no one but him. Another interpretation is that they have "languid" eyes—that specific state mentioned in many poems as a beautiful description of the eye. [2] Of course, the first and second meanings seem more appropriate, though there is no obstacle to combining the meanings.

The word "‘in" (rhyming with min) is the plural of "‘ayna’," meaning a woman with large eyes.

Lastly, the final verse under discussion provides another description for these heavenly spouses, expressing their purity and sanctity with this phrase: "Their bodies, due to extreme purity, delicacy, whiteness, and clarity, are like eggs that neither a human hand has touched nor dust has settled upon; rather, they have remained hidden and covered under the wings of a bird!" (كَأَنَّهُنَّ بَيْضٌ مَكْنُونٌ). "Bayd" is the plural of "baydah," meaning an egg (of any kind of bird), and "maknun" is derived from the root "kann" (rhyming with jann), meaning covered and hidden.

This Quranic simile becomes truly clear when one closely observes an egg at the moment it leaves the bird, before any human hand has reached it, while it is still under the bird's wing; it possesses a remarkable transparency and clarity.

Some commentators have taken "maknun" to mean the contents of the egg hidden under the shell, and in fact, the simile refers to when an egg is boiled and its shell is removed in one piece, in which state, besides whiteness and brilliance, it has a special delicacy and softness. In any case, the Quranic expressions in explaining truths are so deep and meaningful that with one short and elegant phrase, they reflect many points with a unique grace.

Key Points A Look at the Collection of Preceding Verses

The various favors mentioned regarding the inhabitants of Paradise in the preceding verses are a collection of material and spiritual bounties. As we said, the first favor—derived from the concise phrase "أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ رِزْقٌ مَعْلُومٌ"—pertains to spiritual and psychological favors that cannot be explained by any tongue.

As for the other six parts—heavenly fruits, pure drink, good spouses, sufficient respect, pure dwellings, and worthy companions—they describe different dimensions of heavenly blessings, which are often a blend of material and spiritual favors.

However, these are all words spoken in our language and can never reflect all aspects of heavenly blessings. Fundamentally, as we have said, another tongue, ear, perception, and vision are required, along with other words, phrasing, and speech, to be able to describe this event. In other words, the reality of heavenly blessings as they truly are will remain hidden from the people of this world, except that they go, see, and realize them! In any case, the "sincere servants" and those who have reached the stage of perfection in knowledge and faith are so dear in the presence of Allah that His boundless favors toward them defy description and are higher and greater than anything we can imagine.

[1]: The pronouns in "fiha" and "anha" both refer to "khamr" (wine), which is not explicitly mentioned in the text but is understood from the context. We know that khamr is a feminine noun in Arabic usage. The "an" in "anha" is to indicate cause—meaning they do not become intoxicated or lose their intellect because of this wine. It should be noted that the word khamr is a shared term that is sometimes applied to the corruptive wine that destroys the intellect, as in: "Indeed, intoxicants (khamr) and gambling..." (Al-Ma'idah: 90), and sometimes to the pure wine which is the portion of the sincere servants of Allah, as in: "...and rivers of wine (khamr) delicious to the drinkers" (Muhammad: 15), which appears in the description of Paradise. [2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 33, p. 81.

Ayahs

فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (٥٠)
Some of them will turn to others, questioning each other. (50)
قَالَ قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ إِنِّى كَانَ لِى قَرِينٌ (٥١)
One of them will say, ‘Indeed I had a companion (51)
يَقُولُ أَءِنَّكَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُصَدِّقِينَ (٥٢)
who used to say, ‘‘Are you really among those who affirm (52)
أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَءِنَّا لَمَدِينُونَ (٥٣)
that when we have died and become dust and bones, we shall be brought to retribution?’’ ’ (53)
قَالَ هَلْ أَنتُم مُّطَّلِعُونَ (٥٤)
He will say, ‘Will you have a look?’ (54)
فَٱطَّلَعَ فَرَءَاهُ فِى سَوَآءِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٥٥)
Then he will take a look and sight him in the middle of hell. (55)
قَالَ تَٱللَّهِ إِن كِدتَّ لَتُرْدِينِ (٥٦)
He will say, ‘By Allah, you had almost ruined me! (56)
وَلَوْلَا نِعْمَةُ رَبِّى لَكُنتُ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْضَرِينَ (٥٧)
Had it not been for my Lord’s blessing, I too would have been among those mustered [in hell]!’ (57)
أَفَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَيِّتِينَ (٥٨)
‘Is it [true] that we shall not die [anymore], (58)
إِلَّا مَوْتَتَنَا ٱلْأُولَىٰ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ (٥٩)
aside from our earlier death, and that we shall not be punished? (59)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٦٠)
This is indeed the supreme triumph!’ (60)
لِمِثْلِ هَـٰذَا فَلْيَعْمَلِ ٱلْعَـٰمِلُونَ (٦١)
Let all workers work for the like of this! (61)

Commentary

Searching for the Hellbound Friend!

The sincere servants of the Lord, who according to the previous verses are immersed in various spiritual and material blessings of Paradise—with types of heavenly fruits on one side and heavenly maidens (Huris) on the other, cups of pure wine circulating around them, reclining on heavenly thrones and engaged in intimate conversation with sincere friends—suddenly, some of them remember their past and their worldly friends. These are the friends who separated their paths, and whose places are empty among the gathering of the people of Paradise; they wish to know what became of their fate.

Yes, while "they are immersed in conversation, speaking of every matter, some turning to others asking questions and hearing answers" (فَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ‌).

Suddenly, "one of them has memories manifest before him; he turns to the others and says: I had a companion in the world!" (قالَ قائِلٌ مِنْهُمْ إِنِّي كانَ لِي قَرِينٌ‌).

Unfortunately, he was led astray and stood in the line of those who deny the Resurrection; "he used to constantly say to me: Do you really believe this word and testify to its truth?!" (يَقُولُ أَ إِنَّكَ لَمِنَ الْمُصَدِّقِينَ‌).

"That when we have died and become dust and bones, we shall (once again) be brought to life and come to the reckoning and accounting, and be punished or rewarded for our deeds?" I do not believe these words! (أَ إِذا مِتْنا وَ كُنَّا تُراباً وَ عِظاماً أَ إِنَّا لَمَدِينُونَ‌) [^1] O friends! I wish I knew where he is now? And in what condition? Ah, his place among us is empty!

Then he adds: O friends! Can you take a look and get some news of him? (قالَ هَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُطَّلِعُونَ‌) [^1]

"It is here that he also rises to search and casts a look toward Hell; suddenly he sees his friend in the midst of Hell!" (فَاطَّلَعَ فَرَآهُ فِي سَواءِ الْجَحِيمِ‌) [^2]

He addresses him and "calls out: By Allah, you had almost caused me to fall and led me to destruction!" (قالَ تَاللَّهِ إِنْ كِدْتَ لَتُرْدِينِ‌) [^3]

"It was close that your whisperings would affect my pure heart and lead me into the same deviant path you were on. Had it not been for the grace of my Lord and if the blessing of my Lord had not rushed to my aid, I too would have been among those brought [to the Fire] today!" (وَ لَوْ لا نِعْمَةُ رَبِّي لَكُنْتُ مِنَ الْمُحْضَرِينَ‌). It was this Divine success (Tawfiq) that became my traveling companion, and it was the hand of His guiding grace that caressed and led me.

At this point, he turns to his hellbound friend and mentions this statement as a reproach, saying: Was it not you who used to say in the world, "We shall never die"? (أَ فَما نَحْنُ بِمَيِّتِينَ‌).

[^1]: "Madinun" is from the root "Din" meaning recompense; i.e., shall we be recompensed?! [^1]: "Muttali'un" is from the root "Ittila'" meaning to peer, to search, to overlook something, and to gain knowledge of it. [^2]: "Sawa'" means the middle or center. [^3]: "Turdin" is from the root "Irda'" meaning to fall from a height, which is usually accompanied by destruction.

"Except for that first death in the world, and after that there is neither a renewed life nor shall we ever be punished!" (إِلَّا مَوْتَتَنَا الْأُولى‌ وَ ما نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ‌). Now look and see what a great mistake you made! After death, there was such a life and such reward and punishment; now all realities have become manifest to you, but what use is it when there is no way back! According to this commentary, these last two verses are from the speech of this person of Paradise to his companion in Hell, reminding him of his words regarding the denial of the Resurrection.

However, a group of commentators have suggested another possibility in interpreting these two verses: that the conversation of the person of Paradise with the hellbound companion has ended, and the heavenly friends are speaking with one another again. One of them, out of extreme joy, cries out: "Is it true that we shall not die again and that we have eternal life here? Will there be no death other than the first death? And will this Divine grace remain eternal for us and we shall never be punished?"

Of course, these words are not out of doubt or hesitation, but out of extreme ecstasy and joy. It is just as when a person, after a long period of longing and waiting, reaches a spacious and comfortable home and says in amazement: "Is this mine? O my God! What a blessing! Will it not be taken from me?"

In any case, he ends this conversation with a meaningful, highly emotional sentence emphasized with various types of stress, saying: "Truly, this is the great success and victory" (إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ‌). What victory and success is greater than for a human to be immersed in eternal blessing and everlasting life, and to be subject to various Divine favors? What can be imagined greater and higher than this?

Finally, the Great Allah concludes this discussion with a short, awakening, and meaningful sentence, saying: "For the like of this let the workers work" and for the sake of these bounties let the strivers strive (لِمِثْلِ هذا فَلْيَعْمَلِ الْعامِلُونَ‌). The possibility suggested by some commentators that this last verse is also from the speech of the people of Paradise seems very unlikely, because on that day there is no longer any "action" (Amal) to be done; in other words, on that day there is no program of action for which they would want to encourage people with this expression. Meanwhile, the appearance of the verse shows that the goal is to draw a conclusion from all the previous verses by mentioning this sentence and to drive people toward faith and action. Therefore, it is appropriate that this be the word of Allah at the end of this discussion.

Key Points

1- The Connection Between the People of Paradise and the People of Hell

It appears from the above verses that sometimes a type of connection is established between the people of Paradise and the people of Hell. It is as if the people of Paradise, who are situated above, look down upon the people of Hell, who are below, and see their condition (this meaning is derived from the expression fa-ttala'a, which means overlooking from above).

Of course, this is not evidence that the distance between Paradise and Hell is small; rather, in those conditions, an extraordinary power of vision is given to them such that the issue of distance and space is irrelevant before it.

In some words of the commentators, it is mentioned that there is an opening in Paradise from which one can see Hell! From the verses of Surah Al-A'raf, this connection is also clearly evident, where it says: "The inhabitants of Paradise will call out to the inhabitants of the Fire, 'We have indeed found what our Lord promised us to be true. Have you also found what your Lord promised to be true?' They will say, 'Yes.' Then an announcer will proclaim among them, 'The curse of Allah is upon the wrongdoers!'" (وَنادى‌ أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ أَصْحابَ النَّارِ أَنْ قَدْ وَجَدْنا ما وَعَدَنا رَبُّنا حَقًّا فَهَلْ وَجَدْتُمْ ما وَعَدَ رَبُّكُمْ حَقًّا قالُوا نَعَمْ فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ بَيْنَهُمْ أَنْ لَعْنَةُ اللَّهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ‌) (Al-A'raf 44).

From verse 46 of the same Surah Al-A'raf, it is also understood that "between the two there is a partition" (وَ بَيْنَهُما حِجابٌ‌).

The expression "Nada" (called out), which is usually used for speaking from a distance, is an indication of the spatial or positional distance of these two groups. However, as we have said many times, the conditions and states of the Day of Resurrection are very different from the situation of this world, and we cannot evaluate them by the standards of this world.

2- Regarding Whom Were These Verses Revealed?

Some commentators have narrated occasions of revelation for the above verses, according to which these verses refer to those two individuals mentioned as an example in Surah Al-Kahf, where He says: "And present to them an example of two men: We granted to one of them two gardens of grapevines, and We bordered them with palm trees and placed between them [fields of] crops..." (Verses 32 to 43 of Surah Al-Kahf).

In those verses, it is mentioned that one of the two was a very selfish, arrogant, and shallow individual who denied the Resurrection, while the other was a believer who believed in the Day of Judgment. Eventually, that faithless, arrogant man was caught by Divine punishment even in this world, and all his wealth and property were lost. [^1] However, the tone of the verses under discussion is not very harmonious with the verses of Surah Al-Kahf and suggests two separate stories.

Other commentators consider it to refer to two partners or friends who had great wealth; one gave much in charity, and the other, who did not believe in these matters, abstained. After a while, the one who gave charity became needy and was reproached by his friend, who mockingly said to him: "Are you among those who believe (al-musaddiqin)?"

"Do you spend in the way of Allah?" [^2] However, this occasion of revelation depends on reading "musaddiqin" in the verses under discussion with a shaddah on the letter Sad, which would relate to giving charity (Sadaqah).

While in the famous recitation, "musaddiqin" is without a shaddah on the Sad; therefore, the mentioned occasion of revelation is not consistent with the famous recitation.

3- For Such Bounties One Must Strive

Is it worthy for a human to spend the precious capital of life and God-given creative talents on matters that are unstable like a bubble on water? A commodity that is low in value and non-durable, a commodity full of pests and trouble.

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Vol. 26, p. 139. [^2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 23, p. 83.

Or should he employ these valuable forces in a path whose result is eternal life, endless bounties, and the pleasure of the Lord?

What a beautiful expression the Quran has in the above verses; it says the strivers should strive for such a goal—for a Paradise full of spiritual pleasures and physical blessings, whose pure wine immerses man in a celestial ecstasy, and the companionship of its sincere friends leaves no grief in the heart. There is neither limitation nor prohibition in it; neither the sorrow of decline enters it, nor the trouble of preservation and maintenance.

Yes, for the like of this, one must endeavor and act.

Ayahs

أَذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ نُّزُلًا أَمْ شَجَرَةُ ٱلزَّقُّومِ (٦٢)
Is this a better reception, or the Zaqqum tree? (62)
إِنَّا جَعَلْنَـٰهَا فِتْنَةً لِّلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٦٣)
Indeed We have made it a punishment for the wrongdoers. (63)
إِنَّهَا شَجَرَةٌ تَخْرُجُ فِىٓ أَصْلِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٦٤)
It is a tree that rises from the depths of hell. (64)
طَلْعُهَا كَأَنَّهُۥ رُءُوسُ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ (٦٥)
Its spathes are as if they were devils’ heads. (65)
فَإِنَّهُمْ لَـَٔاكِلُونَ مِنْهَا فَمَالِـُٔونَ مِنْهَا ٱلْبُطُونَ (٦٦)
They will eat from it and gorge with it their bellies. (66)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ لَهُمْ عَلَيْهَا لَشَوْبًا مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ (٦٧)
On top of that they will take a solution of scalding water. (67)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ مَرْجِعَهُمْ لَإِلَى ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٦٨)
Then their retreat will be toward hell. (68)
إِنَّهُمْ أَلْفَوْا۟ ءَابَآءَهُمْ ضَآلِّينَ (٦٩)
They had found their fathers astray, (69)
فَهُمْ عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِمْ يُهْرَعُونَ (٧٠)
yet they press onwards in their footsteps. (70)

Commentary

A Glimpse into the Agonizing Torments of the People of Hell

After describing the soul-refreshing and precious blessings of Paradise, the verses under discussion proceed to describe the painful and sorrowful torments of Hell. It paints such a picture that, when compared to the preceding blessings, it deeply affects receptive souls and restrains them from every form of ugliness and impurity.

First, It says: "Is this [eternal and delightful] hospitality better, or the repulsive tree of Zaqqum?" (أَ ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ نُزُلًا أَمْ شَجَرَةُ الزَّقُّومِ).

The expression "Nuzul," considering its meaning, refers to that which is prepared for the reception of a guest. Some have said it is the very first thing with which a newly arrived guest is welcomed. This indicates that the inhabitants of Paradise are received like dear and honored guests.

The Quran asks: Is this better or the tree of "Zaqqum"? The use of the word "better" (khayr) does not imply that the tree of Zaqqum is a good thing and the blessings of Paradise are merely better than it; rather, such expressions in the Arabic language are sometimes used in cases where one side possesses no goodness at all. However, it is also possible that this is a form of irony. It is exactly like saying to someone who has become utterly disgraced among the people due to various sins: "Is this disgrace better, or honor and dignity?"

As for "Zaqqum," according to linguists, it is the name of a plant that is bitter, foul-smelling, and bad-tasting [1]. According to some commentators, it is a plant with small, bitter, and foul-smelling leaves that grows in the land of "Tihamah," and the polytheists were familiar with it [2]. The commentary Ruh al-Ma'ani adds that this plant has a sap which, when it touches a human body, causes it to swell [3]. Raghib in Al-Mufradat states: "Zaqqum is any kind of repulsive food for the people of Hell."

Lisan al-Arab states: This root originally means "to swallow."

It then adds that when the verse regarding "Zaqqum" was revealed, Abu Jahl said: "Such a tree does not grow in our land; who among you knows the meaning of Zaqqum?"

A man from Africa was there and said: "Zaqqum in the language of Africans means 'butter and dates'!" Abu Jahl, by way of mockery, called out: "O slave girl! Bring some dates and butter so we may 'Zaqqum'!" They ate and mocked, saying: "Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) threatens us with this in the Hereafter!" The Quran was revealed and gave them a crushing response, which we will read in the following verses.

In any case, the word "Shajarah" does not always mean "tree"; sometimes it refers to a "plant," and the context indicates that what is meant here is a "plant."

[1]: Majma' al-Bahrayn, root "Zaqam." [2]: Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 7, p. 464. [3]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 23, p. 85.

Then the Quran addresses some of the characteristics of this plant, saying: "Indeed, We have made it a source of torment and suffering for the wrongdoers" (إِنَّا جَعَلْناها فِتْنَةً لِلظَّالِمِينَ). "Fitnah" may mean suffering and torment, or it may mean a trial, as it most commonly appears in the Quran with this meaning. This refers to the fact that when they heard the name "Zaqqum," they began to mock and ridicule it; thus, it became a means of testing these oppressors.

It then adds: "Indeed, it is a tree that grows from the bottom of Hell" (إِنَّها شَجَرَةٌ تَخْرُجُ فِي أَصْلِ الْجَحِيمِ). But these arrogant wrongdoers continued their mockery, saying: "Is it possible for a plant or a tree to grow from the bottom of Hell? Where is fire, and where is a tree or plant?" Therefore, hearing the name of this plant and its descriptions was a trial for them in this world, and the plant itself will be a source of pain and suffering for them in the Hereafter.

It seems they were oblivious to the fact that the principles governing life in that world (the Hereafter) are very different from this world. A plant or tree that grows from the bottom of Hell is of the color of Hell and is nurtured under the conditions of Hell; it is not a plant like those that grow in the gardens of this world. Perhaps they were not unaware of this point, but rather their only goal was mockery and ridicule.

It then adds: "Its blossoms are like the heads of devils" (طَلْعُها كَأَنَّهُ رُؤُسُ الشَّياطِينِ). "Tal'" usually refers to the blossom of the date palm, which has a green skin and inside it are white strands that later turn into a cluster of dates. The word "Tal'" is from the root "Tulu'" (to rise/appear) because it is the first fruit that appears and "rises" on the tree.

Here, the question arises: Had the people seen the heads of devils that the Quran compares the blossoms of "Zaqqum" to them?

Commentators have mentioned several answers here:

Some have said: One of the meanings of "Shaytan" is a type of ugly-looking serpent to which the blossom of Zaqqum is compared.

Others have said: It is a type of ugly-looking plant, as mentioned in the book Muntaha al-Arab: "Ra's al-Shaytan" or "Ru'us al-Shayatin" is a plant.

However, what seems more correct is that this comparison is to express its ultimate ugliness and repulsive appearance. This is because when a human being detests something, they depict an ugly and terrifying face for it in their mind, and for whatever they love, they depict a beautiful and lovely face.

Therefore, in the pictures people draw of angels, they depict the most beautiful faces, and conversely, for devils and demons, they depict the worst faces, even though they have seen neither an angel nor a demon.

In everyday expressions, it is often seen that people say: "So-and-so is like an efreet," or "He has the face of a demon!" These are all comparisons based on the mental reflections of humans regarding various concepts—comparisons that are subtle and expressive.

Finally, the Quran says: "These arrogant wrongdoers will surely eat from it and fill their bellies with it" (فَإِنَّهُمْ لَآكِلُونَ مِنْها فَمالِؤُنَ مِنْهَا الْبُطُونَ).

This is the very "Fitnah" and torment referred to in the previous verses. Eating from this hellish plant with its foul smell and bitter taste, with that sap whose contact with the body causes burning and swelling—and eating it in large quantities—is a painful torment [1].

It is obvious that eating this unpleasant and bitter food causes thirst. But what do they drink when they become thirsty? The Quran says: "Then indeed, for them after this [Zaqqum] will be a mixture of boiling, filthy water" (ثُمَّ إِنَّ لَهُمْ عَلَيْها لَشَوْباً مِنْ حَمِيمٍ). "Shawb" means something that is mixed with another thing, and "Hamim" means hot and scalding water. Therefore, even the hot water they drink is not pure but contaminated.

That is the food of the people of Hell, and this is their drink. But where do they go after this reception? The Quran says: "Then indeed, their return is to Hell" (ثُمَّ إِنَّ مَرْجِعَهُمْ لَإِلَى الْجَحِيمِ). Some commentators have inferred from this expression that this contaminated hot water is from a spring outside of Hell; the people of Hell are first called there to drink it, like animals led to a watering hole, and after drinking from it, their return is once again toward Hell.

Others have said this refers to the different stations and positions within Hell, where the wrongdoers and criminals are taken from one area to another to drink that scalding water and then returned to their original place. However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

As we have pointed out before, it is neither possible for us in this world to depict the blessings of Paradise as they truly are, nor the torments of the people of Hell. Only a distant shadow of it is depicted in our minds through short phrases (O Lord, protect us from these torments under the shelter of Your grace).

In the final verses under discussion, the Quran states the main reason for the people of Hell being caught in the clutches of these painful punishments in two short and meaningful sentences, saying: "Indeed, they found their fathers astray" (إِنَّهُمْ أَلْفَوْا آباءَهُمْ ضالِّينَ).

"Yet they rush headlong in their footsteps" (فَهُمْ عَلى‌ آثارِهِمْ يُهرَعُونَ). Interestingly, here "Yuhra'un" is used in the passive form from the root "Ihra'", which means to run with speed. This indicates that they have so lost their hearts and faith to the imitation of their ancestors that it is as if they are being made to run after them speedily and involuntarily, as if they have no will of their own. This points to the ultimate prejudice and infatuation they have for the superstitions of their ancestors.

[1]: The pronoun in "minha" refers back to "Shajarah," and this itself is an indication that "Shajarah" here means a plant, not a tree, because one eats a plant, not a tree.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ضَلَّ قَبْلَهُمْ أَكْثَرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٧١)
Most of the former peoples went astray before them, (71)
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا فِيهِم مُّنذِرِينَ (٧٢)
and We had certainly sent warners among them. (72)
فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ (٧٣)
So observe how was the fate of those who were warned (73)
إِلَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (٧٤)
—[all] except Allah’s exclusive servants! (74)

Commentary

Misguided Nations of the Past

Since the past issues regarding criminals and oppressors are not limited to a specific point in time or space, the Quran proceeds to generalize and expand upon them in the verses under discussion. Within a few short and concise verses, it prepares the ground for explaining the circumstances of many past nations—whose history serves as a living testament to the preceding discussions—nations such as the people of Noah, Abraham, Moses, Aaron, Lot, Jonah, and others like them.

First, He says: "And indeed, most of the former peoples went astray before them" (وَ لَقَدْ ضَلَّ قَبْلَهُمْ أَكْثَرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

It is not only the polytheists of Mecca who are trapped in deep misguidance by imitating their ancestors; rather, before them, most of the past nations met such a fate, and the believers among them were few compared to the misguided. This serves as a consolation for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the few early believers at that time in Mecca, who were besieged by enemies from every side.

He then adds that their misguidance was not due to a lack of leaders or guides: "And We had certainly sent warners among them" (وَ لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا فِيهِمْ مُنْذِرِينَ). Prophets who warned them against polytheism, disbelief, oppression, injustice, and blind imitation of others, and who made them aware of their responsibilities.

It is true that the Prophets held a message of warning in one hand and a message of glad tidings in the other; however, since the greatest pillar of their mission—especially regarding such misguided and rebellious nations—was warning, it is the only aspect emphasized here.

Then, in a short and meaningful sentence, He says: "So see what was the end of those who were warned" (فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُنْذَرِينَ). The addressee in the phrase "So see" (فانظر) may be the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself or any rational and awakened individual.

In reality, this sentence refers to the end of the nations whose stories will be detailed in the subsequent verses.

And in the final verse, as an exception, He says: "Except the chosen servants of Allah" (إِلَّا عِبادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ).

In fact, this sentence indicates: look at the end of these nations, how We afflicted them with painful punishment and destroyed them, except for the faithful and sincere servants who escaped this peril safely. [1] It is noteworthy that in this Surah, the "chosen" (mukhlasin) servants of Allah are emphasized five times in different verses, which is a sign of the greatness of their station. As we have previously mentioned, they are those who have been so successful in knowledge, faith, and the struggle against the self (jihad al-nafs) that Allah has chosen them and purified them; for this reason, they have gained immunity against deviations and slips.

Satan is powerless and desperate to influence them, and from the very first day, he laid down his shield before them and admitted his helplessness.

The clamor of the environment, the whispers of tempters, the imitation of ancestors, and false or tyrannical cultures can never divert them from their path.

This is, in truth, an inspiring message for the steadfast believers of that day in Mecca and for us Muslims in today's tumultuous world: that we should not fear the multitude of enemies and should strive to take our place in the ranks of the "chosen servants of Allah."

[1]: This sentence is an exception to an omitted part understood from the context, and the implied meaning is: "So see what was the end of those who were warned, for We destroyed them all except the chosen servants of Allah."

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ نَادَىٰنَا نُوحٌ فَلَنِعْمَ ٱلْمُجِيبُونَ (٧٥)
Certainly Noah called out to Us, and how well did We respond! (75)
وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْكَرْبِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٧٦)
We delivered him and his family from their great distress, (76)
وَجَعَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُۥ هُمُ ٱلْبَاقِينَ (٧٧)
and made his descendants the survivors, (77)
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٧٨)
and left for him a good name among posterity: (78)
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰ نُوحٍ فِى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٧٩)
‘Peace to Noah, throughout the nations!’ (79)
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٨٠)
Thus do We reward the virtuous. (80)
إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٨١)
He is indeed one of Our faithful servants. (81)
ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ (٨٢)
Then We drowned the rest. (82)

Commentary

A Glimpse into the Story of Noah

From here begins the detailed account of the stories of nine great Prophets of Allah, which were briefly alluded to in the preceding verses. It starts first with "Noah," the Shaykh al-Anbiya (the Elder of the Prophets) and the first of the Ulul 'Azm (Arch-Prophets). Before anything else, it refers to his fervent prayer when he despaired of his people's guidance, stating: "Noah called upon Us, and how excellent are We to respond!" (وَلَقَدْ نَادَانَا نُوحٌ فَلَنِعْمَ الْمُجِيبُونَ) [^1]. This prayer may refer to what is mentioned in Surah Nuh: "And Noah said, 'My Lord, do not leave upon the earth from among the disbelievers a single inhabitant. Indeed, if You leave them, they will mislead Your servants and not beget except [every] wicked disbeliever'" (They are corrupt themselves, and so will be their future generations) (Nuh, verses 26 and 27).

Or it may refer to the prayer he offered to the presence of Allah upon embarking on the Ark: "My Lord, let me land at a blessed landing place, and You are the best of those who bring to land" (Al-Mu'minun, verse 29).

Or the prayer mentioned in Surah Al-Qamar, verse 10: "So he invoked his Lord, 'Indeed, I am overpowered, so help [me]!'"

Certainly, there is no obstacle to the verse under discussion referring to all these prayers, and Allah responded to all of them in the best possible manner.

Consequently, in the following verse, He immediately says: "And We saved him and his family from the great distress" (وَنَجَّيْنَاهُ وَأَهْلَهُ مِنَ الْكَرْبِ الْعَظِيمِ) [^1]. What was this "great distress" that caused Noah such severe suffering? It may refer to the mockery of the disbelieving and arrogant people, their verbal abuse, their insolence and insults toward him and his followers, or it may refer to the continuous denials by this stubborn people.

Sometimes they would say: "And we do not see you followed except by those who are the lowest of us" (Hud, 27).

And sometimes they said: "O Noah, you have disputed with us and been frequent in dispute with us. So bring us what you threaten us with, if you should be of the truthful" (Hud, 32).

And at other times, as the Quran states: "And he was constructing the ship, and whenever a group of the eminent of his people passed by him, they mocked him" (And they would say, 'This man has gone mad!') (Hud, 38).

They troubled this patient Prophet so much, behaved so insolently, and attributed madness to him that he pleaded: "My Lord, support me because they have denied me" (Al-Mu'minun, 26).

In any case, the collection of these unfortunate events and sharp verbal wounds heavily weighed upon his pure heart, until the Flood arrived, and Allah delivered him from the clutches of these oppressive people and from that karb 'azim (great distress) and profound sorrow.

Some commentators have suggested that "great distress" refers to the Flood itself, from which none but Noah and his companions were saved, but this interpretation seems unlikely.

Then He adds: "And We made his descendants the survivors [on the earth]" (وَجَعَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُ هُمُ الْباقِينَ). Are all humans currently living on earth truly the descendants of Noah? Does the above verse say exactly this? Or did a great group of Prophets, saints, and righteous people remain from his lineage, even if not all people are from his progeny?

We have a discussion on this matter which, God willing, will come in the Key Points.

Furthermore, "And We left for him [a goodly mention] among later generations" (وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ). They remember him as a resilient, brave, patient, compassionate, and kind Prophet, and they call him "Shaykh al-Anbiya."

His history is a legend of resistance and steadfastness, and his path is an inspiration for all wayfarers of the path of Truth against the harsh obstructions and follies of the enemies.

"Peace be upon Noah among the worlds" (سَلَامٌ عَلَى نُوحٍ فِي الْعَالَمِينَ).

What honor could be greater or higher than Allah sending peace upon him—a peace that remains throughout the world and among the inhabitants of the worlds, extending until the horizon of Resurrection; the peace of Allah accompanied by the beautiful praise and good mention of His servants.

It is noteworthy that rarely is a salutation seen in the Quran with such breadth and scope regarding anyone, especially since "Al-'Alamin" (by virtue of being plural and accompanied by the definite article 'al-') has such a vast meaning that it may encompass not only all humans but also the realms of angels and the celestial beings.

And so that this program may become an inspiration for others, He adds: "Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good" (إِنَّا كَذَلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

"Indeed, he was of Our believing servants" (إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ). In reality, the station of servitude and devotion, as well as faith coupled with ihsan (excellence/good-doing) mentioned in the last two verses, were the primary reasons for Allah's grace toward Noah, his rescue from the great distress, and the divine peace and salutation upon him. If this path is followed by others, they too shall be included in the same mercy and grace that Noah was, for the criteria of the Lord's favors are infallible and are not based on personal or private bias.

In the final verse under discussion, with a short and striking sentence, He describes the fate of those oppressive, wicked, and spiteful people, saying: "Then We drowned the others" (ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا الْآخَرِينَ). Floods came from the sky, and water gushed from the earth, and the entire globe was transformed into a turbulent ocean! It crushed the palaces of the tyrants, and their lifeless bodies remained upon the surface of the water! It is interesting that He describes His favors toward Noah across several verses, but mentions the punishment of those rebellious people in a single short sentence accompanied by contempt and indifference; for the context of describing the honors and victories of the believers and Allah's aid to them is a place for elaboration, while the context of describing the state of the rebels is a place for indifference.

Key Point: Are all people on earth from the lineage of Noah?

A group of great commentators have inferred from "And We made his descendants the survivors" (وَجَعَلْنَا ذُرِّيَّتَهُ هُمُ الْباقِينَ) that the entire human race after Noah originated from his lineage, and currently everyone is a descendant of Noah.

Many historians have narrated this, stating that three sons remained from Noah named: "Shem," "Ham," and "Japheth," and all current races of the earth trace back to them. They consider the Arab, Persian, and Roman races to be of the Semitic (Shem) race; the Turks and another group from the descendants of Japheth; and the races of Sudan, Sindh, India, Nubia, Abyssinia, Copt, and Berber from the descendants of Ham.

The discussion is not about which race belongs to which son of Noah, as various expressions are seen among historians and commentators on this matter; the discussion is whether all human races trace back to these three.

Here the question arises: did other believers not board the Ark with Noah? What was their fate? Did they all pass away without leaving any offspring? Or if they had children, were they daughters who married the children of Noah? This issue is not very clear historically; rather, from some narrations and certain indications in the verses of the Quran, it may be inferred that descendants of them also remained on earth, and there are nations from their progeny.

In a tradition narrated in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام explaining the above verse, we read:

"The Truth, Prophethood, the Book, and Faith remained in his progeny, but not everyone on earth among the children of Adam is from the offspring of Noah (ع). Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, said in His Book: 'Carry therein two of every kind and your family, except those against whom the word has preceded'—among them—'and those who believed. And none had believed with him, except a few.' And Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, also said: 'O descendants of those We carried with Noah...'" [^1]

Accordingly, what is famous regarding all races on earth tracing back to the sons of Noah is not proven.

[^1]: "Mujibun" (Respondents) is in the plural form while it refers to Allah who responded to Noah's prayer. This is because the plural form is sometimes used to express greatness, just as the first-person plural pronoun in "Nadanā" (called Us) is for the same purpose.

[^1]: "Karb" according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat is intense sorrow, and the description "Azim" (Great) here is a further emphasis on this meaning.

[^1]: This tradition is found in Volume 4 of Nur al-Thaqalayn, page 405, and also in Tafsir al-Safi under the verses discussed.

Ayahs

۞ وَإِنَّ مِن شِيعَتِهِۦ لَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ (٨٣)
Indeed Abraham was among his followers, (83)
إِذْ جَآءَ رَبَّهُۥ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ (٨٤)
when he came to his Lord with a sound heart [untainted by sin]. (84)
إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦ مَاذَا تَعْبُدُونَ (٨٥)
When he said to his father and his people, ‘What is it that you are worshiping? (85)
أَئِفْكًا ءَالِهَةً دُونَ ٱللَّهِ تُرِيدُونَ (٨٦)
Is it a lie, gods other than Allah, that you desire? (86)
فَمَا ظَنُّكُم بِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٧)
Then what is your idea about the Lord of all the worlds?’ (87)
فَنَظَرَ نَظْرَةً فِى ٱلنُّجُومِ (٨٨)
Then he made an observation of the stars (88)
فَقَالَ إِنِّى سَقِيمٌ (٨٩)
and said, ‘Indeed I am sick!’ (89)
فَتَوَلَّوْا۟ عَنْهُ مُدْبِرِينَ (٩٠)
So they went away leaving him behind. (90)
فَرَاغَ إِلَىٰٓ ءَالِهَتِهِمْ فَقَالَ أَلَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٩١)
Then he stole away to their gods and said, ‘Will you not eat? (91)
مَا لَكُمْ لَا تَنطِقُونَ (٩٢)
Why do you not speak?’ (92)
فَرَاغَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضَرْبًۢا بِٱلْيَمِينِ (٩٣)
Then he attacked them, striking forcefully. (93)
فَأَقْبَلُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ يَزِفُّونَ (٩٤)
They came running towards him. (94)

Commentary

Abraham’s Ingenious Plan for Idol-Breaking

In these verses, a significant portion of the life of Abraham, the hero of idol-breaking (peace be upon him), follows the glimpses of the eventful history of Noah (peace be upon him).

Here, it first speaks of the incident of Abraham’s idol-breaking and the idolaters' severe confrontation with him. In another part, it discusses the greatest scene of sacrifice by Abraham, the Friend of Allah (Khalil), and the issue of sacrificing his son—a section that is mentioned exclusively here in the Holy Quran.

The first verse links Abraham’s story to Noah’s story in this manner, stating: "And indeed, among his kind [followers] was Abraham" (وَ إِنَّ مِنْ شِيعَتِهِ لَإِبْراهِيمَ).

He walked the same line of monotheism and justice, the same path of piety and sincerity that was the tradition (Sunnah) of Noah. Indeed, all Prophets are heralds of one school of thought and teachers of one university; each continues and completes the program of the other.

What a beautiful expression! Abraham was among the "Shi'a" (followers/kindred) of Noah, even though a vast span of time separated them (about 2,600 years according to some commentators). However, we know that in a shared school of thought, time has the least impact [1].

After this brief mention, it proceeds to the details, stating: "Remember when he came to his Lord with a sound heart" (إِذْ جاءَ رَبَّهُ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ). Commentators have provided several interpretations for "a sound heart" (Qalb Salim), each pointing to one dimension of this matter:

  • A heart that is pure from polytheism (Shirk).
  • A heart that is sincere and free from sins, malice, and hypocrisy.
  • A heart that is empty of the love of the world, for the love of the world is the root of all errors.
  • And finally, a heart in which there is nothing but Allah!

The truth is that "Salim" is derived from the root "Salamah" (health/soundness). When soundness is mentioned in an absolute sense, it encompasses health from every type of moral and doctrinal disease.

Regarding the hypocrites, the Holy Quran says: "In their hearts is a disease, so Allah has increased their disease" (فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ فَزادَهُمُ اللَّهُ مَرَضاً) [Al-Baqarah: 10]—due to their stubbornness and sin.

The most beautiful interpretation of "a sound heart" was expressed by Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, where we read: "The sound heart is the one that meets its Lord while there is no one in it besides Him!" [1] This expression encompasses all the previous descriptions.

Also, in another narration from the same Imam عليه السلام, it is stated: "The possessor of a truthful intention is the possessor of a sound heart, because the soundness of the heart from the aforementioned distractions makes the intention sincere for Allah in all matters." [2]

Regarding the importance of the sound heart, it suffices that the Holy Quran considers it the only asset for salvation on the Day of Resurrection. As we read in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verses 88 and 89, from the tongue of this great Prophet Abraham عليه السلام: "The Day when neither wealth nor children will benefit [anyone], except one who comes to Allah with a sound heart" (يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ مالٌ وَ لا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ). [1]

Indeed, Abraham, with a sound heart, a pure soul, a powerful will, and firm resolve, was commissioned to fight the idolaters. He began with his father (uncle) and his people, as the Quran says: "Remember when he said to his father and his people: 'What is it that you worship?'" (إِذْ قالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَ قَوْمِهِ ما ذا تَعْبُدُونَ). Is it not a pity for a human being, with such inherent nobility and intellect, to bow down before a handful of worthless stones and wood? Where is your reason?!

Then, he completed this expression—which was accompanied by an obvious belittling of the idols—with another sentence, saying: "Is it a lie—gods besides Allah—that you desire?" (أَ إِفْكاً آلِهَةً دُونَ اللَّهِ تُرِيدُونَ). [2] Considering that "Ifk" means a great lie or the ugliest of lies, the decisiveness of Abraham’s words regarding the idols becomes clearer.

Finally, he concluded his speech at this stage with another crushing sentence, saying: "Then what is your thought about the Lord of the worlds?" (فَما ظَنُّكُمْ بِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ). You eat His provision, His blessings surround your entire existence, yet you place worthless beings on par with Him? Despite this, do you still expect Him to have mercy on you and not punish you with the severest penalty? What a great mistake! What a dangerous deviation!

The expression "Lord of the worlds" (Rabb al-Alamin) points to the fact that the entire universe is managed under His Lordship. You have abandoned Him and gone after a handful of fantasies and delusions that have no effect.

It is mentioned in histories and commentaries that the idolaters of Babylon had a special festival ceremony every year. They would prepare food in the temple and arrange it there, under the delusion that the food would become blessed. Then they would go out of the city en masse and return at the end of the day to the temple for worship and to consume the food. That day, the city became empty, and a good opportunity arose for Abraham to smash the idols—an opportunity Abraham had long awaited and did not wish to let slip away easily.

Therefore, when they invited him in the evening to participate in these ceremonies, "He cast a glance at the stars" (فَنَظَرَ نَظْرَةً فِي النُّجُومِ).

"And said, 'Indeed, I am sick'" (فَقالَ إِنِّي سَقِيمٌ). And in this way, he requested his excuse!

"So they turned away from him, departing" and hurried toward their ceremonies (فَتَوَلَّوْا عَنْهُ مُدْبِرِينَ).

Here, two questions arise: First: Why did Abraham look at the stars? What was his purpose in this look? Second: Was he truly sick when he said "I am sick"? What illness did he have?

The answer to the first question is clear considering the beliefs of the people of Babylon and their customs. They had studies in astronomy, and it is even said that their idols were "talismans" (hayakil) of the stars; they respected them because they were symbols of the stars. Of course, alongside astronomical knowledge, many superstitions were also prevalent among them, such as believing that stars influenced their destiny, seeking blessings from them, and deducing future events from their positions.

Abraham عليه السلام, to convince them according to their customs, cast a glance at the stars in the sky so they would imagine he had predicted his illness from studying the celestial bodies and be satisfied! Some great commentators have also suggested the possibility that he wanted to determine the exact time of his illness from the movement of the stars, as a type of illness like fever would come to him at specific intervals. However, given the state of mind of the Babylonian people, the first possibility is more appropriate. Some have also suggested that his look at the sky was actually a look of contemplation into the secrets of creation, even though they perceived his look as that of an astrologer wanting to predict future events from the stars.

Regarding the second question, several answers have been given: One is that he was truly sick, although even if he were healthy, he would never have participated in the idol festival. However, his illness was a good excuse for not participating and for using the golden opportunity to smash the idols. There is no reason for us to say he used "Tawriyah" (equivocation) here, as Tawriyah is not suitable for Prophets.

Others have said that Abraham had no physical illness, but his soul was sickened by the unseemly actions of this population—their disbelief, polytheism, oppression, and corruption. Therefore, he stated a reality, even though they thought otherwise and assumed he was physically ill.

It is also possible that he used Tawriyah in this statement. For example, if someone comes to the door and asks if so-and-so is at home, they might reply: "He is not here," meaning "not right here behind the door," not the entire house, while the listener does not understand it that way. (Such expressions, which are not lies but whose appearance is something else, are called Tawriyah in jurisprudence). Abraham’s intention with this statement was that "I might become sick in the future," so they would leave him alone and go about their business. However, the first and second interpretations seem more appropriate.

Thus, Abraham عليه السلام remained alone in the city, and the idolaters emptied the city and went out. Abraham looked around; the spark of enthusiasm appeared in his eyes. The moments he had long awaited had arrived. He must rise alone and go to war against the idols, dealing a heavy blow to their bodies—a blow that would shake and awaken the dormant minds of the idolaters. The Quran says: "Then he turned to their gods and said [mockingly], 'Will you not eat?'" (فَراغَ إِلى‌ آلِهَتِهِمْ فَقالَ أَ لا تَأْكُلُونَ). [1] Your worshippers have prepared these foods; they are diverse, rich, and colorful. Why do you not partake?!

Then he added: "What is [the matter] with you that you do not speak?" Why are you mute and silent?! (ما لَكُمْ لا تَنْطِقُونَ). In this way, he ridiculed all their superstitious beliefs. Undoubtedly, he knew well that they neither eat nor speak; they are nothing more than inanimate objects. But in reality, he wanted to present the reason for his idol-breaking program in this beautiful and subtle manner.

Then he rolled up his sleeves, took the axe, moved with power, and "turned upon them, striking with the right hand!" (فَراغَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضَرْباً بِالْيَمِينِ). "The right hand" (Yamin) either literally means the right hand, with which a person performs most tasks, or it is a metaphor for power and strength (both meanings can also be combined). In any case, it was not long before he turned that flourishing and beautiful temple into a terrifying ruin. The idols were all smashed to pieces, and with broken limbs, each fell into a corner. Truly, for the idolaters, it became a heartbreaking, appalling, and tragic sight. Abraham did his work and came out of the temple confident and calm, heading to his home while preparing himself for future events.

He knew he had caused a massive explosion in the city, rather in the entire country of Babylon, the sound of which would soon be heard! A storm of rage and anger would be unleashed, and he would be alone in the midst of that storm. But he had Allah, and He is sufficient for him.

The idolaters returned to the city and came to the temple. What a terrifying and staggering sight! It was as if they were frozen in their tracks. For a few moments, they lost their train of thought and stared blankly and bewildered at that ruin, seeing the idols they thought were their refuge in times of helplessness lying there helpless. Then silence gave way to roaring, shouting, and screaming... Who did this? Which oppressor?! It was not long before they remembered there was a monotheistic youth in this city named Abraham who used to ridicule the idols and had threatened, "I have a dangerous plan for your idols!" It became clear that the act was his. "Then the people came toward him, hastening [in anger]" (فَأَقْبَلُوا إِلَيْهِ يَزِفُّونَ). "Yaziffun" is from the root "Zaff," originally used for the blowing of the wind or the rapid movement of an ostrich, which is a mixture of walking and leaping. Later, this word was used metaphorically for the "Zifaf" of a bride, meaning taking the bride to the groom's house. In any case, the meaning is that the idolaters came toward Abraham with speed, the continuation of which we will read in the following verses.

Key Points

Do Prophets also use Tawriyah?

First, it is necessary to know what "Tawriyah" is. "Tawriyah" (equivocation), which is sometimes referred to as "Ma'aridh," is to say something that has an apparent meaning, but the speaker intends something else, even though the listener's attention is directed toward that apparent meaning. For example, someone asks another: "When did you come from your journey?" He says: "Before sunset," while he actually arrived before noon. The listener understands from the appearance of this speech that it was shortly before sunset, while the speaker intended before noon, because that is also "before sunset"! Or someone asks another: "Have you eaten?" He says: "Yes." The listener understands from this that he ate today, whereas his intention is that he ate yesterday.

This point is discussed in books of jurisprudence: is Tawriyah considered a lie or not? A group of great jurists, including Sheikh Ansari (may Allah be pleased with him), believe that Tawriyah is not part of lying. Neither is it linguistically defined as a lie, nor is its inclusion in lying deduced from Islamic narrations; rather, in some narrations, the label of lying is officially negated from it. In a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "A man seeks permission to enter a house, and the master of the house (who has a reason not to receive him) says to the maidservant: 'Say: He is not here' (meaning, for example, right here behind the door)." The Imam عليه السلام said: "There is no harm in it; it is not a lie." [1]

But the truth is that a distinction must be made here, and as a general rule, it can be said: whenever a word, in terms of its linguistic and customary concept, is capable of two meanings, but the listener's mind applies it to a specific meaning while the speaker intends another, such Tawriyah is not a lie. For example, using a homonym where the listener's mind goes to one meaning while the speaker intends another. For instance, in the accounts of Sa'id ibn Jubayr, it is mentioned that Hajjaj asked him: "What is your opinion of me?" He said: "In my opinion, you are 'Adil' (just/swerving)!" Those around were happy, but Hajjaj said: "With this word, he has pronounced my disbelief, because one meaning of 'Adil' is one who swerves (ya'dilu) from truth to falsehood!" However, if a word has only one linguistic and customary meaning and the speaker abandons it for a metaphorical meaning without mentioning a metaphorical clue (qarinah), such Tawriyah is undoubtedly forbidden. With this distinction, different views of jurists can be reconciled.

But it must be noted that even in cases where Tawriyah is not an instance of lying, it sometimes carries its harms, leading to "misleading into ignorance" and causing people to fall into error. From this perspective, it may reach the level of being forbidden. But whenever it has no such harm and is not an instance of lying, we have no reason for its prohibition, and the narration of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام is of this kind. Therefore, merely not being a lie is not enough for using Tawriyah; rather, other harms must also not be present in it. And of course, in cases where necessity requires a person to tell a lie, as long as Tawriyah is possible, one must use Tawriyah so that the speech is not an instance of lying.

As for whether Tawriyah is permissible for Prophets, it must be said: if it causes a loss of public trust, it is not permissible, because the asset of the Prophets in the path of propagation is the public trust of the people. But in cases like what appeared in the story of Abraham عليه السلام in the above verses—where he expressed illness or looked at the stars like astrologers, and there was an important goal in this act without shaking the foundations of the trust of truth-seekers—there is no problem whatsoever.

Abraham and the "Sound Heart"

We know that "Heart" (Qalb) in Quranic terminology means the soul and the intellect. Therefore, a "Sound Heart" (Qalb Salim) refers to a pure and healthy soul that is free from any polytheism, doubt, or corruption. The Holy Quran has described some hearts as "Qasiyah" (hardened) [Al-Ma'idah: 13]. Sometimes it introduces hearts as "unclean" [Al-Ma'idah: 41]. It introduces some hearts as "diseased" [Al-Baqarah: 10]. And some hearts as "sealed" and closed [At-Tawbah: 87]. In contrast to these, it presents the "Sound Heart," which has none of these defects; it is both pure and soft and full of compassion, both healthy and flexible toward the truth.

This is the same heart described as the "Sanctuary of Allah" in narrations, as we read in a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "The heart is the sanctuary of Allah, so do not settle anyone other than Allah in the sanctuary of Allah!" [1]

This is the same heart that can see the realities of the unseen and look into the kingdom (Malakut) of the higher world, as stated in a tradition from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "If it were not that devils hover over the hearts of the children of Adam, they would surely look into the Malakut!" [1] In any case, the best asset for salvation in the Hereafter is this "Sound Heart," and it was with this that Abraham عليه السلام hastened to the presence of his Lord and received the command of messengership.

We conclude this discussion with another tradition. It is stated in a narration: "Indeed, Allah has vessels among His servants, and that is the heart. The most beloved of them to Him are the 'clearest,' the 'firmest,' and the 'thinnest': the firmest in the religion of Allah, the clearest from sins, and the thinnest (most tender) toward brothers!" [2]

Perhaps they imagined that the term "Shi'a" is evidence of Noah's superiority over Abraham, while the Quran grants Abraham a higher status. However, this expression has no such implication; rather, it means the continuity of the intellectual and school-of-thought line, just as the superiority of the Prophet of Islam over all Prophets does not contradict his following the monotheistic school of Abraham. Commentary

The Plots of the Polytheists Fail

Ultimately, following the incident of idol-breaking, Ibrahim was brought to court on that very charge.

He was questioned and demanded to explain who had carried out the horrific act in the temple. The Quran describes this event in Surah Al-Anbiya, while in the verses under discussion, it contents itself with only one sensitive passage: Ibrahim’s final words to them regarding the falsehood of idol worship. It says:

"He said: 'Do you worship that which you [yourselves] carve?'" (قَالَ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مَا تَنْحِتُونَ).

Does any rational person worship their own handiwork? Does any sentient being kneel before their own creation? What intellect or logic has granted you such permission?!

The deity must be the Creator of man, not his creation. Now look closely and find the true Deity: "While Allah created you and that which you make" (وَاللَّهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَمَا تَعْمَلُونَ). The heavens and the earth are all His creation, and time and space all belong to Him; one must prostrate at the threshold of such a Creator and worship and pray to Him.

This is a very powerful and crushing argument against which they had no answer.

The word "ma" (ما) in the phrase "ma ta’malun" (مَا تَعْمَلُونَ) is what is grammatically termed ma mawsulah (the relative pronoun "that which"), not ma masdariyah (the infinitive "your doing"). It intends to say that Allah created both you and your handiworks. If the term "man-made" or "man-wrought" is applied to idols, it is because of the form man gives them; otherwise, their raw material was also created by Allah. This is exactly like saying this carpet, that house, or that car is man-made. Certainly, it does not mean that man created their materials; rather, their form is shaped by human hands.

However, if we take "ma" as masdariyah, the meaning would be that Allah created both you and your actions. Of course, this meaning is not incorrect and, contrary to the imagination of some, does not lead to fatalism (determinism). For although our actions are performed by our will, the will and the power to decide, as well as the other forces with which we perform our deeds, all originate from Allah. Nevertheless, the verse is not focused on this meaning; rather, it refers to the idols, saying: "Allah is the Creator of both you and the idols you have fashioned and prepared." The beauty of the speech lies in this, as the discussion was about idols, not human actions.

In fact, this verse is similar to what is mentioned in the story of Musa and the sorcerers: "And behold, it swallowed up what they had falsely devised" (فَإِذَا هِيَ تَلْقَفُ مَا يَأْفِكُونَ) — meaning Musa released the staff, it became a great serpent and swallowed what they had falsely made (referring to the sorcerers' artificial snakes) (Al-A'raf: 117).

But we know that tyrants and bullies have never been acquainted with logic and reasoning. For this reason, this eloquent and powerful proof of Ibrahim عليه السلام had no effect on the hearts of the leaders of the tyrannical Babylonian system. Although it awakened a group of the oppressed masses, the arrogant ones—who saw the progress of this monotheistic logic as a threat to their interests—entered the field with the logic of force, bayonets, and fire; a logic they understand better than any other. They relied on their power "and shouted: 'Build for him a structure and cast him into the burning fire!'" (قَالُوا ابْنُوا لَهُ بُنْيَانًا فَأَلْقُوهُ فِي الْجَحِيمِ).

From this expression, it is understood that they were previously ordered to build a large four-walled enclosure, then they lit a fire inside it. Perhaps this was to contain the fire from spreading and prevent potential hazards, and also to practically create the "hell" with which Ibrahim had threatened the idolaters! It is true that a small pile of firewood would have sufficed to burn a man like Ibrahim, but to quench the burning of their own hearts over the breaking of the idols, and to take their revenge to the utmost degree—while also bestowing a grandeur upon the idols to perhaps restore their lost reputation and to intimidate all their opponents so that such an incident would never be repeated in the history of Babylon—they created this sea of fire (note that "Jahim" linguistically refers to fires piled on top of one another).

Some have interpreted "bunyan" (structure) here as a "catapult," which was a device for throwing heavy objects from a distance, but most commentators have chosen the first interpretation: that "bunyan" refers to a building or a large four-walled structure.

Here, the Quran does not mention the subtleties and details of this matter which appear in Surah Al-Anbiya; rather, in a concise and beautiful summary, it describes the end of this event as follows: "So they intended a plot against him, but We made them the lowest" (فَأَرَادُوا بِهِ كَيْدًا فَجَعَلْنَاهُمُ الْأَسْفَلِينَ). "Kayd" (plot) originally means any kind of "contriving a solution," whether in a right or wrong way, though it is mostly used in blameworthy contexts. Given that it appears here in the "indefinite" form (nakirah)—an indefiniteness that denotes magnitude and importance—it refers to the vast and extensive plan they had devised to destroy Ibrahim and eradicate the effects of his verbal and practical preaching.

Yes, Allah placed them as "asfal" (the lowest), and Ibrahim in the "highest rank." Just as his logic was superior, Allah made him superior in the incident of the fire as well, and brought his powerful enemies to a fall. He made the fire cool and safe for him, and he emerged from that sea of fire healthy without even a single strand of his hair being burnt! One day He saves Nuh from "drowning" (gharq), and another day He saves Ibrahim from "burning" (harq), to make it clear that "water" and "fire" are under His command and "they do what Allah commands."

Ibrahim عليه السلام emerged safe and proud from this horrific incident and the dangerous conspiracy the enemy had plotted for him. Since he saw his mission in Babylon as completed, he decided to migrate to the holy lands of "Sham" (the Levant) "and said: 'Indeed, I am going to my Lord; He will guide me'" (وَقَالَ إِنِّي ذَاهِبٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ).

It is obvious that Allah does not occupy a physical space, but migrating from a polluted environment to a pure environment is a migration toward Allah. Migrating to the land of the Prophets, the Saints, and the centers of Divine revelation is a migration toward Allah, just as the journey to Mecca is called a "journey to Allah" (safar ila-Allah). Furthermore, migrating to fulfill a Divine duty and mission is considered a journey toward the Beloved, and in this journey, the Guide and Leader everywhere is Allah.

And here, his first request from Allah, reflected in the above verses, was the request for a righteous child—a child who could continue his line of mission and complete his unfinished programs. It was here that he pleaded: "My Lord! Grant me [a child] from among the righteous" (رَبِّ هَبْ لِي مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ).

What a beautiful expression: "a righteous and worthy child"—worthy in terms of belief and faith, worthy in speech and action, and worthy in all aspects. It is noteworthy that in one place Ibrahim himself asks to be among the righteous, as the Quran quotes him: "My Lord! Grant me authority and join me with the righteous" (رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ) (Ash-Shu'ara: 83). And here he requests that righteous children be granted to him, because "righteous" (salih) is a comprehensive description in which all the merits of a perfect human being are gathered.

Allah also answered this prayer and granted him righteous children such as "Isma'il" and "Ishaq," as we read in the subsequent verses of this Surah: "And We gave him good tidings of Ishaq, a prophet from among the righteous" (وَبَشَّرْنَاهُ بِإِسْحَاقَ نَبِيًّا مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ). And regarding Isma'il, He says: "And [remember] Isma'il and Idris and Dhul-Kifl; all were of the patient. And We admitted them into Our mercy. Indeed, they were of the righteous" (Al-Anbiya: 85-86).

Key Points

1- He is the Creator of All Things

In the verses under discussion, we read: "While Allah created you and that which you make." Ibrahim says to the idolaters: "Both you yourselves are created by Allah, and so are your fabricated idols."

Some have imagined the above verse to be a justification for the corrupt school of fatalism (jabr) (by taking "ma" in the phrase "ma ta’malun" as ma masdariyah and saying: the meaning of the sentence becomes that Allah created you and your actions, and when our actions are created by Allah, then we have no choice of our own).

This claim is baseless for several reasons: Firstly, as we said, the meaning of "ma ta’malun" here refers to the idols they made with their own hands, not human actions; and undoubtedly, they took these materials from the world of creation but gave them shape (therefore, "ma" is ma mawsulah). Secondly, if the meaning of the verse were what they imagined, it would become an argument in favor of the idolaters, not against them, for they could then say: "Since Allah created our act of idol-making and idol-worship, we are blameless in this matter!" Thirdly, even assuming the meaning of the verse were such, it still would not be proof of fatalism. For despite the freedom of will and choice, in one sense Allah is still the Creator of our actions, because who gave us this freedom of will, the power to decide, and the physical, intellectual, material, and spiritual forces with which we perform our deeds, if not Allah? Thus, He is the Creator, even though the act is our voluntary act.

2- The Migration of Ibrahim (peace be upon him)

Many Prophets throughout their lives undertook migration to fulfill their mission, among them Ibrahim, whose migration is emphasized in various verses of the Quran. For instance, in Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 26, we read: "And he said: 'Indeed, I am a migrant to my Lord. Indeed, He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise.'" The Quran mentions this statement there after the incident of Ibrahim’s fire.

The truth is that Divine leaders, when they completed their mission in one location or found the environment unready for the expansion of their call, would resort to migration so that their mission would not come to a halt. These migrations became the source of many blessings throughout the history of religions, to the extent that the history of Islam, both outwardly and inwardly, revolves around the axis of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله migration. If it were not for the migration (Hijrah), Islam would have been swallowed forever in the quagmire of the polytheists of Mecca. It was migration that gave new life to Islam and the Muslims, changed everything in their favor, and placed humanity on a new path.

Rather, in one sense, migration is a general program for every single believer; whenever they find the environment unsuitable for their sacred goals during their life and find it like a foul quagmire where everything rots, they are duty-bound to migrate. They must pack their bags and move to a more prepared land, for Allah’s kingdom is not limited.

However, before migration has an outward aspect, it has an inward aspect. First, within the heart and soul, "a migration must be made": a migration from "impurities" toward "purity," a migration from "polytheism" to "faith," and a migration from "sin" to obedience to the Great Lord. This internal migration will be the starting point for the transformation of the individual and society, and a prelude to outward migration. In the fourth volume of Tafsir Nemouneh, we have provided a detailed discussion on "Islam and Migration" under verse 100 of Surah An-Nisa.
