So when they had both surrendered [to Allah’s will], and he had laid him down on his forehead, (103)
وَنَـٰدَيْنَـٰهُ أَن يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ (١٠٤)
We called out to him, ‘O Abraham! (104)
قَدْ صَدَّقْتَ ٱلرُّءْيَآ ۚ إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٠٥)
You have indeed fulfilled your vision! Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous! (105)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْبَلَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٠٦)
This was indeed a manifest test.’ (106)
وَفَدَيْنَـٰهُ بِذِبْحٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٠٧)
Then We ransomed him with a great sacrifice, (107)
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١٠٨)
and left for him a good name in posterity: (108)
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ (١٠٩)
‘Peace be to Abraham!’ (109)
كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١١٠)
Thus do We reward the virtuous. (110)

Commentary

Abraham at the Place of Sacrifice

In the preceding verses, we reached the point where Abraham, after fulfilling his mission in Babylon, migrated from there. His first request to his Lord was to grant him a righteous child, as he had not been blessed with offspring until that day.

The first verse under discussion speaks of the fulfillment of this prayer of Abraham, stating: "So We gave him the good news of a forbearing boy" (فَبَشَّرْناهُ بِغُلامٍ حَلِيمٍ‌).

In fact, three glad tidings are gathered in this single sentence: the news of the birth of a child who is a son, the news of him reaching the age of adolescence, and the news of the sublime quality of hilm (forbearance).

In the commentary of "halim," it is said to be one who, despite having the power, does not hasten in any matter before its proper time and does not rush to punish offenders. He possesses a great soul and is in control of his emotions.

"Raghib" in Al-Mufradat says: Hilm means self-restraint during the excitement of anger. Since this state arises from intellect and wisdom, it is sometimes used to mean intellect as well; otherwise, the literal meaning of hilm is what was stated first. Furthermore, it is inferred from this description that Allah gave the glad tidings of this child’s survival until he reached an age where he could be described as forbearing. As we will see in the subsequent verses, he demonstrated his status of being halim during the event of the "Sacrifice," just as Abraham manifested his own forbearance both at that time and during the ordeal of the fire.

It is noteworthy that the word "halim" is repeated fifteen times in the Holy Quran, and it is mostly an attribute for Allah, except in two instances where it appears as a description for Abraham and his son in the word of God, and in one instance where it is a description for Shu'ayb (peace be upon him) spoken by others.

The word "ghulam" (boy/youth), according to some, refers to any child before reaching the age of young adulthood, while others apply it to a child who has passed ten years of age but has not yet reached puberty. From the various expressions used in the Arabic language, it can be inferred that "ghulam" is the stage between "tifl" (infant/child) and "shabb" (young man), which we refer to in Persian as "nojavan" (adolescent/teenager).

Finally, Abraham’s promised son was born according to the divine glad tidings, brightening the heart of the father who had waited years for a righteous child. He passed through the period of childhood and reached the age of adolescence.

Here, the Quran says: "And when he reached [the age of] exertion with him" (فَلَمَّا بَلَغَ مَعَهُ السَّعْيَ‌).

This means he reached a stage where he could strive and work alongside his father in various life matters and assist him. Some have interpreted "sa'y" (exertion/striving) here to mean worship and working for the sake of God. While sa'y has a broad meaning that includes this, it is not limited to it. The expression "ma'ahu" (with him/with his father) indicates that the intent is assisting the father in the affairs of life.

In any case, according to a group of commentators, the son was thirteen years old at that time when Abraham saw a strange and amazing dream, which signaled the start of another great trial for this magnificent Prophet. In the dream, he saw that he was commanded by Allah to sacrifice and slaughter his only son with his own hands.

Abraham woke up startled. He knew that the dreams of Prophets are reality and are far from satanic whisperings. Nevertheless, the same dream was repeated for two more nights, which was an emphasis on the necessity of this command and its urgency.

It is said that he first saw this dream on the night of Tarwiyah (the 8th night of Dhu al-Hijjah), and the dream was repeated on the nights of Arafah and the night of Eid al-Adha (the 9th and 10th of Dhu al-Hijjah). Therefore, not the slightest doubt remained for him that this was a definitive command from God.

Abraham, who had emerged victorious from the hot furnace of divine trials many times before, had to once again take the plunge and submit to the command of the Truth. He had to slaughter with his own hands the child he had waited a lifetime for, who was now a robust youth! But before anything else, he had to prepare the son for this task. He turned to him and "said: O my son, indeed I have seen in a dream that I [must] sacrifice you, so see what you think" (قالَ يا بُنَيَّ إِنِّي أَرى‌ فِي الْمَنامِ أَنِّي أَذْبَحُكَ فَانْظُرْ ما ذا تَرى‌).

The son, who was a copy of his self-sacrificing father’s existence and had studied the lesson of patience, endurance, and faith in his short life in his father's school, welcomed this divine command with open arms and a willing heart. With clarity and decisiveness, "he said: O my father, do as you are commanded" (قالَ يا أَبَتِ افْعَلْ ما تُؤْمَرُ).

And as for me, let your mind be at ease, for "you will find me, if Allah wills, of the steadfast" (سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ‌).

How meaningful are these expressions of the father and the son, and what subtleties are hidden within them!

On one hand, the father explicitly presents the issue of sacrifice to his 13-year-old son and asks for his opinion, granting him an independent personality and freedom of will. He never wishes to deceive his son or invite him blindly to this great field of trial. He wants the son to participate in this great struggle against the self and to taste the sweetness of submission and pleasure just as the father does!

On the other hand, the son also wants the father to be firm in his resolve and decision. He does not say "sacrifice me," but rather says "do whatever mission You have; I am submissive to His order and command." He specifically addresses his father with "Ya abati!" (O my father!), to show that this matter does not diminish the father-son affection by a single atom, for the command of God reigns over everything.

And thirdly, he maintains the levels of etiquette before the presence of the Lord in the most sublime manner. He never relies on the strength of his own faith, will, or resolve; rather, He relies on the will of Allah and His volition, and with this phrase, he seeks from Him the success of steadfastness and endurance.

In this way, both the father and the son pass the first stage of this great trial with complete victory.

What happened in the meantime? The Quran refrains from detailing it and only points to the sensitive moments of this extraordinary event.

Some have written: To help his father in fulfilling this mission and to reduce the mother's grief, when Abraham brought him to the place of sacrifice amidst the dry and scorching mountains of the land of "Mina," the son said to the father: "O my father! Tie the rope tightly so that I do not struggle while the divine command is being executed; I fear my reward might be diminished! Dear father, sharpen the knife and pass it quickly over my throat so that enduring it may be easier for me (and for you)! My father, take off my shirt first so it does not get stained with blood, for I fear that when my mother sees it, she will lose her patience."

Then he added: "Convey my greetings to my mother, and if you see no obstacle, take my shirt to her, as it will be a source of comfort and a soothing of her pains, for she will find the scent of her son from it, and whenever she feels distressed, she will press it to her chest to alleviate her inner burning."

The sensitive moments arrived; the divine command had to be executed. When Abraham saw the son's state of submission, he embraced him and kissed his cheeks, and both began to weep at that moment—a weeping that expressed deep emotions and was a prelude to the longing for meeting God.

The Quran says this much in a short and meaningful phrase: "And when they had both submitted and he put him down upon his forehead..." (فَلَمَّا أَسْلَما وَ تَلَّهُ لِلْجَبِينِ‌) ^1. Again, the Quran passes over this briefly, allowing the listener to follow the story with the waves of their own emotions.

Some have said the meaning of the phrase "tallahu lil-jabin" was that he placed the boy's forehead on the ground at the boy's own suggestion, lest his eyes fall upon his son's face and fatherly emotions be stirred, preventing the execution of God's command! In any case, Abraham placed his son's face on the ground and moved the knife, placing it with speed and strength upon his son's throat while his soul was immersed in agitation, and only the love of God moved him forward without hesitation.

But the sharp knife had not the slightest effect on the son's tender throat!...

Abraham was plunged into astonishment. He moved the knife again, but again it was ineffective. Yes, Abraham the "Khalil" (Friend) says: "Cut!" But the Lord of the Khalil commands: "Do not cut!" and the knife only obeys His command.

It is here that the Quran ends all anticipation with one short and meaningful sentence, saying: "And We called out to him, 'O Abraham!'" (وَ نادَيْناهُ أَنْ يا إِبْراهِيمُ‌).

"You have fulfilled the vision [mission]" (قَدْ صَدَّقْتَ الرُّؤْيا). "Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good" (إِنَّا كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌).

We grant them both the success of victory in the trial and do not allow their beloved child to be lost. Indeed, one who is entirely submissive to Our command and has brought goodness to its peak shall have no reward but this.

Then He adds: "Indeed, this was the manifest trial" (إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ الْبَلاءُ الْمُبِينُ‌). Sacrificing a child with one's own hands—and a robust, worthy child at that—for a father who has waited a lifetime for such a child, is no simple or easy task. How can one tear their heart away from such a son? And even more so, to hasten to comply with this command with ultimate submission and pleasure without even a frown, and to perform all the preliminaries until the final stage, such that nothing was neglected in terms of psychological and practical readiness?

And even more amazing was the absolute submission of this youth before this command, who hastened to welcome the sacrifice with open arms and a heart confident in the grace of the Lord and submission to His will.

Therefore, it is mentioned in some narrations that when this act was performed, Gabriel (out of admiration) called out: "Allahu Akbar! Allahu Akbar!"...

And the son of Abraham called out: "La ilaha illa Allah, wa Allahu Akbar!"... and the heroic, self-sacrificing father also said: "Allahu Akbar wa lillahi al-hamd" ^1. This is similar to the takbirat we recite on the day of Eid al-Adha.

However, so that Abraham’s program would not remain incomplete, and so that he would have performed a sacrifice in the presence of God and his wish would be fulfilled, Allah sent a great ram to be sacrificed in place of the son, establishing a tradition for future generations in the "Hajj" rituals and the land of "Mina," as the Quran says: "And We ransomed him with a great sacrifice" (وَ فَدَيْناهُ بِذِبْحٍ عَظِيمٍ‌).

Regarding the nature of the greatness of this sacrifice—was it in terms of physical appearance? Or because it was a ransom for Abraham's son? Or because it was for God and in the way of God? Or because this sacrifice was sent by God for Abraham?

Commentators have many discussions, but there is no obstacle to all these aspects being gathered in the "great sacrifice," making it great from various perspectives. One sign of the greatness of this sacrifice is that with the passage of time, it has expanded year by year, and now every year more than a million people sacrifice in memory of that great sacrifice, keeping its memory alive.

"Fadaynahu" is from the root "fada," which originally means placing something as a shield against calamity or to ward off harm from a person or another thing. Therefore, the wealth given to free a captive is called "fidyah," and the expiation that some sick people give instead of fasting is also called by this name.

^1: Tafsir al-Qurtubi and Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan.

Regarding how this great ram was given to Abraham (peace be upon him), many believe Gabriel brought it; some have also said it descended from the slopes of the mountains of "Mina." Whatever it was, it was by the command of Allah and by His will.

Not only did Allah praise Abraham's victory in this great trial on that day, but He also made its memory eternal, as He says in the next verse: "And We left for him [a goodly name] among later generations" (وَ تَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ‌).

"He became an 'exemplar' for all who come after, and a 'model' for all the pure-hearted and devoted lovers of the Friend's path. We made his program eternal in the form of the Hajj tradition throughout the coming ages and centuries until the end of the world." He was the father of the great Prophets; he was the father of the Islamic Ummah and the Prophet of Islam.

"Peace be upon Abraham" (that sincere and devoted servant) (سَلامٌ عَلى‌ إِبْراهِيمَ‌).

Yes, "Thus do We reward the doers of good" (كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌). A reward as great as the world, an eternal reward throughout all time, a reward worthy of the peace and salutations of the Great God! It is interesting to note that the phrase "Thus do We reward the doers of good" is mentioned once here and once a few verses prior; this repetition certainly has a point.

The reason might be that in the first stage, Allah confirms Abraham's victory in his great trial and signs his "passing grade" report. This itself is a great reward, and it was the most important news God gave to Abraham. Then, He mentions the issue of "ransoming with a great sacrifice," "the eternity of his name and tradition," and "God sending peace upon him"—which are three other great blessings—and introduces them as the reward for the doers of good.

Key Points

1- Who is the "Dhabih Allah" (Sacrifice of Allah)?

There is intense debate among commentators as to which of Abraham's sons (Ishmael or Isaac) was taken to the place of sacrifice and earned the title Dhabih Allah. A group considers "Isaac" to be the Dhabih, while another group identifies "Ishmael." The first view is held by many Sunni commentators, while the second is chosen by Shia commentators.

However, what aligns with the apparent meanings of various Quranic verses is that the Dhabih was "Ishmael," because:

Firstly: In one place we read: "And We gave him the good news of Isaac, a prophet from among the righteous" (As-Saaffat: 112). This expression clearly shows that Allah gave the glad tidings of Isaac's birth after this event and because of Abraham's sacrifices; therefore, the event of the sacrifice did not pertain to him. Additionally, when Allah gives glad tidings of someone's prophethood, it implies they will live to adulthood, which is inconsistent with the issue of being sacrificed in childhood.

Secondly: In verse 71 of Surah Hud, we read: "So We gave her good news of Isaac and after Isaac, Jacob." This verse shows that Abraham was certain that Isaac would remain and that a child like Jacob would be born from him; therefore, there would be no occasion for sacrifice. Those who consider Isaac to be the Dhabih have essentially ignored these verses.

Thirdly: Many narrations in Islamic sources indicate that the Dhabih was "Ishmael." For example: In a reliable tradition narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam, we read: "I am the son of the two sacrificed ones (al-dhabihayn)." The "two sacrificed ones" refers to his father "Abdullah," whom "Abdul Muttalib" (the Prophet's grandfather) had vowed to sacrifice for God and then ransomed with a hundred camels by God's command (a famous story), and the other was "Ishmael," as it is certain that the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) is from the descendants of Ishmael, not Isaac ^1. In a prayer narrated by Ali (peace be upon him) from the Noble Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), we read: "O He who provided a ransom for the sacrifice of Ishmael" [^2]. In traditions narrated from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon them), we read: "When they were asked who the Dhabih was, they said: 'Ishmael'."

In a tradition narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Ridha (peace be upon him), we also read: "If Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, knew of anything more honorable than a ram, He would have ransomed Ishmael with it" [^3]. In short, the narrations in this regard are numerous; if we were to cite them all, the discourse would become lengthy ^1.

^1: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 421. [^3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 422.

Against these numerous narrations that harmonize with the apparent meaning of the Quranic verses, there are rare (shadh) narrations indicating Isaac was the Dhabih, which cannot stand against the first group of narrations, nor do they harmonize with the apparent meaning of the Quran.

Beyond all this, it is a settled matter that the child whom Abraham brought to Mecca with his mother by God's command and left there, and with whose help he later built the Kaaba and performed Tawaf and Sa'y, was Ishmael. This indicates that the Dhabih was also Ishmael, as the program of sacrifice was considered a completion of the aforementioned programs.

Of course, what appears from the books of the "Old Testament" (the current Torah) is that the Dhabih was Isaac [^2]. From this, it seems that some of the less-known Islamic narrations identifying Isaac as the Dhabih are influenced by "Isra'iliyyat" (Judeo-Christian traditions) and are likely Jewish fabrications. Since the Jews were from the lineage of "Isaac," they wished to record this honor for themselves and strip it from the Muslims whose Prophet was a descendant of Ishmael, even if it meant denying the facts! In any case, what is most firm for us is the apparent meaning of the Quranic verses, which clearly show that the Dhabih was Ishmael. Although for us, it makes no difference whether the Dhabih was Ishmael or Isaac—both were sons of Abraham and great Prophets of God—the goal is to clarify this historical event.

^1: For more information on these narrations, refer to Tafsir al-Burhan (Vol. 4, p. 28 onwards) and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn (Vol. 4, p. 420 onwards). [^2]: Torah, Genesis, Chapter 22.

2- Was Abraham Commanded to Actually Slaughter the Child?

Another important question raised by commentators in this discussion is: was Abraham truly commanded to slaughter the child, or was he commanded to perform the preliminaries?

If he was commanded to slaughter, how was this divine decree abrogated before its execution? Abrogation before action is not permissible, as established in the science of Usul al-Fiqh (Principles of Jurisprudence). And if he was only commanded to perform the preliminaries of slaughter, it would not be a significant honor. The suggestion by some that the importance arose because Abraham expected a command for the actual slaughter after the preliminaries—and that this was his great trial—does not seem compelling.

In our view, these debates arise from failing to distinguish between "trial commands" and "non-trial commands." The command given to Abraham was a trial command. We know that in trial commands, the serious will is not directed toward the actual deed itself, but rather the goal is to clarify to what extent the person being tested is ready to obey the command. This occurs when the person being tested is unaware of the secrets behind the scenes. Thus, no abrogation occurred here that would require debate over its validity before action.

When we see that Allah says to Abraham after this event: "You have fulfilled the vision," it is because he did everything in his power regarding the sacrifice of his beloved son, proved his spiritual readiness in every respect, and emerged successfully from this trial.

3- How Could Abraham's Dream Be a Proof?

There is much to say regarding "dreams" and "dreaming," of which we provided a detailed explanation in the commentary of Surah Yusuf under verse 4 ^1. What must be noted here is: how did Abraham consider the dream a proof (hujjah) and make it the criterion for his action? In response to this question, it is sometimes said that the dreams of Prophets are never satanic or the product of the power of imagination; rather, they are a part of the program of their prophethood and revelation.

In other words, the connection of Prophets with the Source of Revelation is sometimes through inspiration to the heart, sometimes through seeing the Angel of Revelation, sometimes through hearing sound waves created by God's command, and sometimes through dreams. Thus, no error or mistake occurs in their dreams, and what they see in a dream is exactly like what they see while awake.

It is also said that Abraham (peace be upon him) was informed through revelation while awake that he must act upon the dream he would see regarding the "sacrifice." Alternatively, it is said that the various clues in this dream—including its identical repetition for three consecutive nights—created for him knowledge and certainty that this was a divine mission and nothing else. In any case, all these interpretations may be correct, they do not contradict each other, and they do not oppose the apparent meaning of the verses.

^1: Vol. 9, p. 311 onwards.

4- Satan's Whispers Did Not Affect Abraham's Great Soul

Since Abraham's trial was one of the greatest trials in history—a trial whose goal was to empty his heart of affection and love for other than God and to let divine love radiate throughout his heart—according to some narrations, Satan exerted every effort to prevent Abraham from emerging victorious. Sometimes he went to his mother "Hagar" and said to her: "Do you know what Abraham intends? He wants to slaughter his son today!" Hagar said: "Go, do not speak of the impossible, for he is more merciful than to kill his own son. Is there even a human in the world who would slaughter his own child with his own hands?"

Satan continued his whispering and said: "He claims God commanded him." Hagar replied: "If God commanded him, then he must obey, and there is no way but pleasure and submission!!" Sometimes he went to the "son" and engaged in whispering to him, but achieved no result there either, as he found Ishmael to be entirely submission and pleasure.

Finally, he went to the "father" and said to him: "Abraham! The dream you saw is a satanic dream! Do not obey Satan!" Abraham, who recognized him in the light of faith and prophethood, shouted at him: "Begone, O enemy of God!" ^1 In another tradition, it is said: Abraham first came to "Mash'ar al-Haram" to sacrifice the boy; Satan hastened after him. He came to the site of the "first Jamrah," and Satan followed him; Abraham threw seven stones at him. When he reached the "second Jamrah," he saw Satan again and threw seven more stones at him, until he came to the "Jamrah al-Aqabah" and threw seven more stones at him (despairing him forever) ^1. This shows that the whisperings of devils in the great fields of trial come not from one side but from various directions, every time in a different color and through a different path. Men of God must, like Abraham, recognize devils in all their faces, and from whichever path they enter, they must block the way and stone them. And what a great lesson!

5- The Philosophy of Takbirat in "Mina"

We know that among the instructions regarding Eid al-Adha in Islamic narrations are specific takbirat that all Muslims—both those participating in the Hajj rituals in Mina and those in other places—recite after prayers. (However, those in Mina repeat them after 15 prayers, the first being the Dhuhr prayer of the day of Eid, and those elsewhere repeat them after 10 prayers). The form of the takbirat is as follows:

Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar, La ilaha illa Allah, wa Allahu Akbar, Allahu Akbar, wa lillahi al-hamd, Allahu Akbar 'ala ma hadana.

When we compare this instruction with the tradition we cited earlier, we see that these takbirat are in fact a collection of the takbirat of "Gabriel," "Ishmael," and his father "Abraham," and something added to it. In other words, these expressions keep the memory of the victory of "Abraham" and "Ishmael" in that great field of trial alive in minds and inspire all Muslims, whether in Mina or elsewhere.

Additionally, it is known from Islamic narrations that the naming of the land of "Mina" with this name is because when Abraham reached this land and succeeded in the trial, Gabriel said to him: "Ask your Lord for whatever you wish (tamanna)." He made a "wish" (tamanna) to God that He command a ram to be sacrificed as a ransom for his son Ishmael, and this wish of his was fulfilled ^1.

6- "Hajj": An Important Character-Building Worship

The journey of Hajj is in truth a great migration, a divine journey, and a vast field of self-building and the "Greater Jihad" (Jihad al-Akbar). The Hajj rituals actually represent a worship that is deeply intertwined with the memory of the struggles of Abraham, his son Ishmael, and his wife Hagar. If we neglect this point in our studies of the secrets of Hajj, many of its rituals will appear as enigmas. Yes, the key to solving this enigma is paying attention to this deep intertwining.

When we come to the place of sacrifice in the land of Mina, we wonder: what is all this sacrifice for? Can the slaughter of an animal essentially be a link in the set of a worship? But when we remember the issue of Abraham's sacrifice—how he offered the dearest of his loved ones and the sweetest fruit of his life in this field in the way of God, and later a tradition was established as sacrifice in Mina—we realize the philosophy of this act.

Sacrificing is a symbol of giving up everything in the way of the Beloved. Sacrificing is a manifestation of emptying the heart of everything except the remembrance of God. One can only gain sufficient educational benefit from these rites when the entire scene of Ishmael's sacrifice and the states of mind of this father and son during the sacrifice are visualized, and those states of mind radiate within the human being ^1.

When we go to the Jamarat (the three specific stone pillars that pilgrims pelt with stones during Hajj rituals, hitting them with seven stones each time with specific ceremonies), this enigma presents itself: what meaning can the throwing of all these stones at a soulless pillar have? And what problem does it solve? But when we remember that these are reminders of the memory of the struggle of Abraham, the hero of monotheism, against the whisperings of Satan—who appeared in his path three times and intended to trap him in weakness and doubt in this field of "Greater Jihad," but each time the hero Abraham drove him away with stones—the content of these ceremonies becomes clearer.

The meaning of these ceremonies is that all of you, throughout your lives, face the whisperings of devils in the field of the Greater Jihad, and until you stone them and drive them away, you will not be victorious. If you expect the Great God to look upon you with grace and mercy, just as He sent peace upon Abraham and made his school and memory eternal, you must continue his path.

Or when we come to "Safa" and "Marwah" and see groups of people going from this small hill to that smaller hill, and returning from there to here, repeating this act without having gained anything—sometimes running and sometimes walking—we certainly wonder what kind of work this is and what meaning it could have?! But when we look back and remember the story of the striving and effort of that woman of faith, "Hagar," to save the life of her nursing infant Ishmael in that dry and scorching desert—how after this striving and effort, God brought her to her goal and the spring of Zamzam began to gush from under her infant's feet—suddenly the wheel of time turns back, the curtains are pulled aside, and we see ourselves at that moment beside "Hagar," and we become her companions in her striving and effort: that in the way of God, without striving and effort, no one reaches anywhere!

It is easy to conclude from what we have said that "Hajj" must be taught with these secrets, and the memories of Abraham, his son, and his wife must be visualized step by step, so that both its philosophy is understood and the deep moral effects of Hajj radiate within the souls of the pilgrims; for without those effects, it is nothing but a shell.
