إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١١١)
He is indeed one of Our faithful servants. (111)
وَبَشَّرْنَـٰهُ بِإِسْحَـٰقَ نَبِيًّا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١١٢)
And We gave him the good news of [the birth of] Isaac, a prophet, one of the righteous. (112)
وَبَـٰرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ وَعَلَىٰٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ ۚ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِمَا مُحْسِنٌ وَظَالِمٌ لِّنَفْسِهِۦ مُبِينٌ (١١٣)
And We blessed him and Isaac. Among their descendants [some] are virtuous, and [some] who manifestly wrong themselves. (113)

Commentary

Abraham, the Faithful Servant of Allah

The three verses above are the final verses that follow and complete the story of Abraham and his sons. In reality, they serve as both an explanation of the reasons for what transpired and a conclusion to it.

First, it says: "Indeed, he (Abraham) was of Our believing servants" (إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

In truth, this sentence provides the reason for what preceded it; it expresses the reality that if Abraham placed his entire existence and being, and even his beloved son, all at once on the platter of sincerity and sacrificed them in the path of his Deity, it was because of his deep and powerful faith.

Yes, all of these are manifestations of faith, and what wondrous manifestations faith has! Furthermore, this expression expands and generalizes the story of Abraham and his son, taking it out of the form of a personal and private event, and showing that wherever there is faith, there is altruism, love, sacrifice, and devotion. Abraham desired what Allah desired and wanted what Allah wanted, and every believer can be like this.

Then, it speaks of another of Allah's blessings upon Abraham, stating:

"And We gave him the good news of Isaac, a prophet from among the righteous" (وَ بَشَّرْناهُ بِإِسْحاقَ نَبِيًّا مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ‌).

Considering the verse: "So We gave him the good news of a forbearing boy" (فَبَشَّرْناهُ بِغُلامٍ حَلِيمٍ‌), which was mentioned at the beginning of this account, it becomes clearly evident that these two glad tidings refer to two different sons. If the latter glad tiding, according to the explicit text of the verse under discussion, refers to "Isaac," then the "forbearing boy" (the patient and tolerant youth) must certainly be "Ishmael." Those who insist on identifying the "Sacrificed One" (Dhabih) as "Isaac" have considered both verses to refer to the same subject, with the difference that they view the first verse as the announcement of the son's birth and the second verse as the glad tiding of his prophethood. However, this interpretation is very far-fetched, and the above verses clearly state that these two glad tidings were related to two different sons (reflect carefully).

Furthermore, the glad tiding of prophethood indicates that Isaac must remain alive to carry out the duties of prophethood, and this is inconsistent with the matter of the sacrifice (Dhabh).

It is interesting that here we once again encounter the greatness of the status of the righteous (Salihin), as He says in describing Isaac: "he was to be a prophet and among the righteous." How exalted is the station of the righteous in the presence of the Great Allah!

In the final verse, there is mention of a blessing that Allah bestowed upon Abraham and his son Isaac. He says: "And We blessed him and Isaac" (وَ بارَكْنا عَلَيْهِ وَ عَلى‌ إِسْحاقَ‌).

But blessing in what? No specific explanation is given for it, and we know that usually, when a verb appears in an absolute form without any conditions or restrictions, it conveys a general meaning. Therefore, it includes blessing in everything: in life and longevity, in future generations, in history and school of thought, and in all things.

Fundamentally, "Barakah" (blessing) originates from "Bark" (rhyming with Dark), meaning the chest of a camel. When a camel rests its chest upon the ground, this root is used for it (Baraka al-ba'ir).

Gradually, this root came to be used in the sense of stability and permanence of something. A pool of water is called a "Birka" because the water remains stable in it, and a "Mubarak" (blessed) person or thing is called so because its goodness remains and persists.

From this, it becomes clear that the verse under discussion refers to the stability and permanence of Divine favors upon Abraham and Isaac (and their families). One of the blessings that Allah gave to Abraham and Isaac was that all the prophets of the Children of Israel came from the lineage of Isaac, while the Great Prophet of Islam is from the lineage of Ishmael.

However, so that it is not mistakenly thought that this blessing in the family of Abraham is merely a matter of lineage and tribe, but rather is connected to religion, school of thought, and faith, He adds at the end of the verse:

"And among their offspring are the doers of good and those who clearly wrong themselves" (مِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِهِما مُحْسِنٌ وَ ظالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ مُبِينٌ‌).

"Muhsin" (doer of good) here means a believer who is obedient to Allah's command, and what excellence or goodness can be imagined greater than this? And "Zalim" (wrongdoer) means a disbeliever and a sinner. The expression "to himself" (li-nafsihi) indicates that disbelief and sin are, in the first instance, an injustice against one's own self—and a clear and manifest injustice at that.

In this way, the above verse responds to a group of Jews and Christians who took pride in being the children of prophets, stating that the bond of kinship alone is not a source of pride unless it exists under the shadow of an intellectual and ideological bond.

A witness to this statement is a Hadith narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, who addressed the "Bani Hashim" saying:

"Let it not be that people come to me on the Day of Resurrection with their deeds, while you come to me with your lineages!" (Meaning they have an ideological bond while you have only a physical bond). [^1]

[^1]: "Ruh al-Bayan," Vol. 7, p. 479.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ (١١٤)
Certainly We favoured Moses and Aaron, (114)
وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُمَا وَقَوْمَهُمَا مِنَ ٱلْكَرْبِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (١١٥)
and delivered them and their people from their great distress, (115)
وَنَصَرْنَـٰهُمْ فَكَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ (١١٦)
and We helped them so that they became the victors. (116)
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ٱلْمُسْتَبِينَ (١١٧)
We gave them the illuminating scripture (117)
وَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمَا ٱلصِّرَٰطَ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمَ (١١٨)
and guided them to the straight path, (118)
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِمَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١١٩)
and left for them a good name in posterity. (119)
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ (١٢٠)
‘Peace be to Moses and Aaron!’ (120)
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٢١)
Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous. (121)
إِنَّهُمَا مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٢٢)
They are indeed among Our faithful servants. (122)

Commentary

Divine Favors Upon Moses and Aaron

In these verses, a portion of the Divine favors toward "Moses" and his brother "Aaron" is mentioned. The discussions are harmonized with what preceded regarding "Noah" and "Abraham" in the previous verses; the content of the verses is similar to one another, and the wording is also coordinated in certain aspects, in order to implement a cohesive educational program for the believers.

Once again, these verses utilize the method of "brevity followed by detail," which is the Quranic method in narrating many events:

First, It says: "We bestowed favor upon Moses and Aaron" and made them indebted to Our blessings (وَ لَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلى‌ مُوسى‌ وَ هارُونَ‌).

"Mann" (favor/grace), as we have mentioned previously, originally stems from "man," meaning a stone used for weighing. Subsequently, it was applied to great and "heavy" blessings. If this favor is practical in nature, it is beautiful and praiseworthy; but if it is expressed through words and speech [as a reminder of obligation], it is ugly and unsightly. Although in everyday usage, "minnat" is mostly used in the second sense—which causes an undesirable association when studying verses like the ones under discussion—it must be noted that "minnat" in the literal sense and in Quranic usage has a broad meaning that includes the first concept (bestowing heavy blessings) as well as the second.

In any case, in this verse, Allah briefly announces the weighty blessings He granted to these two brothers, and in the following verses, He explains seven instances of these blessings, each more precious than the last.

In the first stage, He says: "We delivered them and their people from the great distress" (وَ نَجَّيْناهُما وَ قَوْمَهُما مِنَ الْكَرْبِ الْعَظِيمِ‌). What distress could be greater than the Children of Israel being trapped in the clutches of the tyrannical and bloodthirsty Pharaohs? They would slaughter their sons, take their women into domestic service, and force their men into slavery and hard labor.

Yes, the loss of liberty and freedom, and being caught in the grip of a ruthless ruler who showed mercy to neither the young nor the old, and even toyed with the honor of a nation, was a karb-e-azim (great distress) and a massive sorrow. This was the first favor that Allah bestowed upon the people of the Children of Israel.

In the second stage, He says: "And We helped them so they were the victors" (وَ نَصَرْناهُمْ فَكانُوا هُمُ الْغالِبِينَ‌). On that day when the bloodthirsty army of Pharaoh set out with immense power and force, with Pharaoh himself at their head, the Children of Israel were a weak and helpless people, lacking fighting men and sufficient weapons. However, the hand of Allah’s grace came to their aid; He buried the Pharaohs amidst the waves, delivered the Israelites from the abyss, and handed over the palaces, wealth, gardens, and treasures of the Pharaohs to them.

In the third stage, referring to the spiritual gifts that Allah granted to this liberated people, He says: "And We gave them the explicit Book" (وَ آتَيْناهُمَا الْكِتابَ الْمُسْتَبِينَ‌).

Yes, the "Torah" was a mustabin book, meaning a clarifying book, and it answered all the religious and worldly needs of the Children of Israel at that time, just as we read in verse 44 of Surah Al-Ma'idah: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْراةَ فِيها هُدىً وَّ نُورٌ "Indeed, We sent down the Torah, in which was guidance and light."

In the fourth stage, He points to another spiritual gift—the gift of guidance to the Straight Path—saying: "And We guided them to the straight path" (وَ هَدَيْناهُمَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ‌). The very path that is straight and free from any crookedness or deviation, which is the path of the Prophets and the Friends of Allah, where the danger of deviation, misguidance, and fall does not exist.

It is interesting that in Surah Al-Hamd, which we recite in all prayers, when we ask Allah for guidance to the "Straight Path," we say: the path of those upon whom You have bestowed Your favor, not the path of those who have evoked [Your] anger or of those who are astray. This is the path of the Prophets and the Friends of Allah.

In the fifth stage, moving toward the continuity of their school of thought and the preservation of their good name, He says: "And We left for them [a goodly mention] among later generations" (so that they may be known as two role models, and the people of the world may take inspiration from their method and history) (وَ تَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِما فِي الْآخِرِينَ‌).

[Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 19, p. 135]

This is the same expression used in the previous verses regarding "Abraham" and "Noah." Fundamentally, all men of God and the great wayfarers of the path of Truth have eternal histories and names. It must be so, for they do not belong to a single tribe or nation, but rather belong to the entire world of humanity.

In the sixth stage, the discourse is about Allah's peace and salutations upon Moses and Aaron; He says: "Peace be upon Moses and Aaron" (سَلامٌ عَلى‌ مُوسى‌ وَ هارُونَ‌). A peace from the Great and Merciful Lord.

A peace that is a symbol of soundness in religion and faith, in belief and school of thought, and in path and creed. A peace that signifies salvation and security from punishment and torment in this world and the next.

In the seventh and final stage, He addresses His great reward and recompense to them, saying: Yes, "Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good" (إِنَّا كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌). If they attained these honors, it was not without reason; they were muhsinin (doers of good)—believing, sincere, self-sacrificing, and virtuous. Such individuals must be included in such vast rewards.

It is noteworthy that this exact phrase "إِنَّا كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌" appears in this same Surah regarding Noah, Abraham, Moses, Aaron, and Elias. A similar expression is also seen regarding Joseph (Joseph, verse 22) and a group of other Prophets (Al-An'am, verse 84). All of this testifies that to benefit from Divine favors, one must first be among the "Muhsinin," after which Divine blessings are certain (reflect on this).

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, He points to the same reason that appeared in the stories of Abraham and Noah before it, saying: "Indeed, they were both (Moses and Aaron) among Our believing servants" (إِنَّهُما مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

It is faith that makes the human soul so bright and powerful that it pursues excellence, goodness, purity, and piety—excellence that opens the doors of Divine mercy to man and sends down His various blessings upon him.

Ayahs

وَإِنَّ إِلْيَاسَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٢٣)
Indeed Ilyas was one of the apostles. (123)
إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٢٤)
When he said to his people, ‘Will you not be Godwary? (124)
أَتَدْعُونَ بَعْلًا وَتَذَرُونَ أَحْسَنَ ٱلْخَـٰلِقِينَ (١٢٥)
Do you invoke Baal and abandon the best of creators, (125)
ٱللَّهَ رَبَّكُمْ وَرَبَّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٢٦)
Allah, your Lord and Lord of your forefathers?,’ (126)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ (١٢٧)
they impugned him. So they will indeed be mustered [in hell] (127)
إِلَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (١٢٨)
—[all] except Allah’s exclusive servants. (128)
وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١٢٩)
We left for him a good name in posterity. (129)
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰٓ إِلْ يَاسِينَ (١٣٠)
‘Peace be to Ilyas!’ (130)
إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٣١)
Thus indeed do We reward the virtuous. (131)
إِنَّهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١٣٢)
He is indeed one of Our faithful servants. (132)

Commentary

The Prophet of Allah, Ilyas, Confronting the Idolaters

The fourth account of the previous prophets mentioned in this Surah is the concise story of "Ilyas." He says: "Indeed, Ilyas was among the messengers" (وَ إِنَّ إِلْياسَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ).

Regarding "Ilyas" and the specifics of his lineage and life, there are discussions that will follow in the final points of these verses, Allah willing.

Then, to elaborate on this brief mention, He provides details, saying: "Remember when he warned his people and said: 'Will you not practice piety?'" (إِذْ قالَ لِقَوْمِهِ أَ لا تَتَّقُونَ).

Piety towards Allah and abstaining from polytheism and idol-worship, from sin, injustice, and corruption, and from everything that leads humanity to ruin.

In the next verse, He speaks of this matter with greater clarity: "Do you call upon 'Ba'l' and forsake the Best of Creators?!" (أَ تَدْعُونَ بَعْلًا وَ تَذَرُونَ أَحْسَنَ الْخالِقِينَ). From here, it becomes clear that they had a famous idol named "Ba'l" which they prostrated before. Ilyas (peace be upon him) forbade them from this ugly act and invited them toward the Great Creator of the universe and pure monotheism (Tawhid).

It is for this reason that a group believes the center of Ilyas's activity was the city of "Baalbek," one of the cities of the Levant [^1], because "Ba'l" was the name of that specific idol and "Bek" meant city; from the combination of these two, "Baalbek" was formed. It is said that this golden idol was so large that its length reached 20 cubits! It had four faces, and its attendants numbered over four hundred! [^2] However, some do not consider "Ba'l" to be the name of a specific idol but rather take it to mean "idol" in a general sense, while others consider it to mean "Lord and deity."

"Raghib" says in Al-Mufradat: "Ba'l" originally means husband, but the Arabs used to call the deities through which they sought proximity to Allah "Ba'l."

The expression "Best of Creators"—despite the fact that there is no real creator in the universe except Allah—apparently refers to the manufactured goods that humans create by changing the form of natural materials.

[^1]: Today, "Baalbek" is part of Lebanon, located near the Syrian border.

[^2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, under the verses being discussed.

From this perspective, the term "creator" is applied to man, even if he is a metaphorical creator.

In any case, Ilyas severely rebuked this idol-worshipping people and continued: "[Will you forsake] Allah, your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers?" (اللَّهَ رَبَّكُمْ وَ رَبَّ آبائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ). He has been and is the Owner and Nurturer of you all; every blessing you have is from Him, and the solution to every problem is possible through His hand of power. Other than Him, there is neither a source of good and blessing nor a repeller of evil and calamity.

It seems the idolaters of Ilyas's time, like the idolaters of the era of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), relied on the tradition of their ancestors and predecessors to justify their actions, to which Ilyas responds: "Allah is your Lord and the Lord of your fathers."

The expression "Rabb" (Owner and Nurturer) is the best stimulus for reflection and thought, because the most important issue in human life is to know by whom he was created. And today, who is his Master, Nurturer, and Benefactor?

But this headstrong and selfish people did not listen to the reasoned advice and clear guidance of this great Prophet of Allah, "and they denied him" (فَكَذَّبُوهُ). Allah also states their punishment in one short sentence, saying:

"They will surely be brought [for punishment]" (فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ) in the court of Divine Justice and in the torment of His Hell.

And they will receive the retribution for their ugly and ominous deeds.

However, it appears that a small group of the pure, the good, and the sincere believed in Ilyas.

So that their right is not forgotten, He immediately says: "Except the sincere servants of Allah" (إِلَّا عِبادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ) [^1].

In the final verses of this story, the same four matters that appeared in the accounts of other prophets (regarding Moses, Aaron, Abraham, and Noah) are repeated because of their importance: First, He says: "And We left for him [a goodly remembrance] among later generations" (وَ تَرَكْنا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ).

Other nations will never forget the efforts of these great prophets who exerted their utmost endeavor in guarding the line of monotheism and watering the seeds of faith. As long as the world stands, the memory and the school of these great and self-sacrificing men remain alive and eternal.

In the second stage, He adds: "Peace be upon Ilyasin" (سَلامٌ عَلى‌ إِلْ‌ياسِينَ).

The expression "Ilyasin" instead of "Ilyas" is either because "Ilyasin" was a linguistic variation of the word "Ilyas," both having the same meaning, or it refers to Ilyas and his followers, mentioned in a collective form [^2].

In the third stage, He says: "Thus do We reward the doers of good" (إِنَّا كَذلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

[^1]: According to what we said above, this exception is a "connected exception" (istithna' muttasil) from the "waw" in "kadhdhabuhu," meaning the entire people denied him and all will be caught in Divine punishment except the sincere servants of Allah.

[^2]: First, "Ilyas" was attributed and became "Ilyasi," then it was made plural and became "Ilyasiyyin," and then it was shortened to "Ilyasin." (Note carefully).

The intention behind "doing good" (ihsan) is in the broad sense of the word, which includes acting upon all His laws and commands, and then struggling against every kind of polytheism, deviation, sin, and corruption.

And in the fourth stage, He presents the primary root of all these, which is "faith," saying: "Indeed, he (Ilyas) was of Our believing servants" (إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبادَنَا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ). "Faith" and "servitude" are the source of ihsan, and ihsan is the factor for being placed in the ranks of the sincere (mukhlasin) and being included in the peace of Allah.

Key Points

1- Who is Ilyas?

There is no doubt that "Ilyas" is one of the great prophets of Allah, and the verses under discussion state this matter explicitly where they say: "Indeed, Ilyas was among the messengers."

The name of this prophet appears in two verses of the Holy Quran: one in this Surah "As-Saffat" and the other in Surah "Al-An'am" along with another group of prophets where He says: "And Zechariah and John and Jesus and Elias. Each was of the righteous" (Al-An'am 85).

But as to whether Ilyas is another name for one of the prophets whose names are mentioned in the Quran, or if it is the name of a prophet independently, and what his characteristics are, commentators have various views:

A: Some believe "Ilyas" is the same as "Idris" (because Idris was also pronounced Idras, and with a slight change became Ilyas).

B: Ilyas is one of the prophets of the Children of Israel, the son of "Yasin" from the descendants of Aaron, the brother of Moses (peace be upon him).

C: Ilyas is the same as Khidr, while others believe Ilyas is a friend of Khidr, and both are alive, with the difference that Ilyas has a mission on land, while Khidr is in the islands and seas. Others consider Ilyas's mission to be in the deserts and Khidr's mission in the mountains, and they believe in eternal life for both. Some also considered Ilyas to be the son of "Elisha" (Al-Yasa').

D: Ilyas is the same as "Elijah," the prophet of the Children of Israel contemporary with "Ahab," the king of the Children of Israel, whom Allah sent to warn and guide this tyrannical king.

Some have also considered him to be "John" the Baptist.

However, what is consistent with the apparent meaning of the Quranic verses is that this word is independently the name of one of the prophets, other than those whose names are mentioned in the Quran, who was commissioned to guide an idol-worshipping people, and the majority of that people rose to deny him, but a group of sincere believers followed him.

And as we pointed out before, some—noting that the name of the great idol of this people was "Ba'l"—believe that this prophet arose from the land of "Levant" (Sham), and they consider the center of his activity to be the city of "Baalbek," which today is part of Lebanon and located on the Syrian border.

In any case, various stories about this prophet have appeared in books, and since they were not reliable or certain, we refrain from quoting them [^1].

2- Who are the "Ilyasin"?

Commentators and historians have different views regarding "Ilyasin":

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir al-Mizan, Ruh al-Bayan, Fakhr Razi, Fi Zilal, A'lam al-Quran, and Dehkhoda Encyclopedia.

A: Some consider it a linguistic variation of Ilyas; just as, for example, "Mikan" and "Mikail" are two expressions for that specific angel, and "Sina" and "Sinin" are both names for a famous land, "Ilyas" and "Ilyasin" are also two expressions for this great prophet [^1].

B: Others consider it a plural, in the sense that "Ilyas" was joined with the "ya" of attribution (nisbah) and became "Ilyasi," then it was made plural with "ya" and "nun" and became "Ilyasiyyin," and after shortening became "Ilyasin." Therefore, its meaning is all those who were related to Ilyas and followed his school [^2].

C: "Al-Yasin" with a long Alif is a combination of the word "Al" (family/folk) and "Yasin." Yasin, according to one report, is the name of Ilyas's father, and according to another report, it is one of the names of the noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family). Therefore, "Al-Yasin" means the family of the noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) or the family of Yasin, the father of Ilyas.

There are clear indications within the Quran itself that support the first meaning—that "Ilyasin" refers to "Ilyas" himself. Because after the verse "Peace be upon Ilyasin," at an interval of one verse, it says: "Indeed, he was of Our believing servants" (إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبادَنَا الْمُؤْمِنِينَ). The return of the singular pronoun to "Ilyasin" is proof that he was no more than one person, namely Ilyas.

Another reason is that these four verses at the end of the story of Ilyas are exactly the same verses that were at the end of the stories of Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Aaron. When we place these verses side by side, we see that the peace mentioned by Allah in these verses is directed toward the same prophet mentioned at the beginning of the discourse (Peace be upon Noah among the worlds—Peace be upon Abraham—Peace be upon Moses and Aaron).

[^1] and [^2]: Al-Bayan fi Gharib I'rab al-Quran, Vol. 2, p. 308.

Therefore, here too, "Peace be upon Ilyasin" will be peace upon Ilyas (Note carefully).

A point that must be noted here is that in many hadith-based commentaries, a report is narrated that traces back to Ibn Abbas, and he says: "The meaning of 'Al-Yasin' is the Family of Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) because 'Yasin' is among the names of the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family)."

In Ma'ani al-Akhbar by Saduq, in the chapter he mentioned for the interpretation of "Al-Yasin," five hadiths are narrated in this regard, none of which reach the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt—except for one hadith—and the narrator of that hadith is a person named "Kadah" or "Qadah" [^1] who is not mentioned in the books of Rijal.

Since these reports are based on the assumption that the above verse is recited as "Peace be upon Al-Yasin" (Family of Yasin), and ignore the harmony of the verses, and the chains of these narrations are also debatable as we have seen, it is better to refrain from judging these narrations and entrust their knowledge to those qualified.

[^1]: Ma'ani al-Akhbar, p. 122 (Published by Jami'at al-Mudarrisin, Qom).

Ayahs

وَإِنَّ لُوطًا لَّمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٣٣)
Indeed Lot was one of the apostles. (133)
إِذْ نَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٣٤)
When We delivered him and all his family, (134)
إِلَّا عَجُوزًا فِى ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (١٣٥)
excepting an old woman among those who remained behind, (135)
ثُمَّ دَمَّرْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ (١٣٦)
We destroyed the rest. (136)
وَإِنَّكُمْ لَتَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهِم مُّصْبِحِينَ (١٣٧)
Indeed you pass by them at dawn (137)
وَبِٱلَّيْلِ ۗ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (١٣٨)
and at night. Do you not exercise your reason? (138)

Commentary

The Afflicted Land of This People is Before You!

The fifth prophet mentioned in this Surah, and in this series of verses, whose history is briefly recounted as an instructive lesson, is Lut عليه السلام. According to the explicit text of the Quran, he was a contemporary of Ibrahim عليه السلام and was among the great prophets of Allah (Surah Al-Ankabut, Verse 26 and Surah Hud, Verse 74).

The name "Lut" عليه السلام appears in many verses of the Quran, and he and his people are frequently discussed. In particular, the painful fate of this deviant people has been explained in the clearest manner (Surah Ash-Shu'ara, Verses 167 to 173; Surah Hud, Verses 70 to 83; Surah An-Naml, Verses 54 to 58, and others).

First, it states: "And indeed, Lut was among the messengers" (وَ إِنَّ لُوطاً لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ‌).

After this brief statement, following the Quranic method of brevity followed by detail, it proceeds to explain part of his story, saying: "Remember when We saved him and his family, all of them" (إِذْ نَجَّيْناهُ وَ أَهْلَهُ أَجْمَعِينَ‌).

"Except an old woman who remained among those who stayed behind" (إِلَّا عَجُوزاً فِي الْغابِرِينَ‌) [1]

"Then We utterly destroyed the others" (ثُمَّ دَمَّرْنَا الْآخَرِينَ‌). The above short sentences are references to the eventful history of this people, the details of which were previously mentioned in Surahs "Hud," "Ash-Shu'ara," and "Al-Ankabut."

Lut عليه السلام, like other prophets, began his call with Monotheism (Tawhid).

[^1]: "Ghabir" (غابر), as we have mentioned before, is derived from the root "Ghubur" (غبور) on the weight of "Ubur" (عبور), meaning the remainder of something. Whenever a group moves from a place and someone stays behind, they are called "Ghabir." For this same reason, the remaining dust is called "Ghubar" (غبار), and the remaining milk in the udder is called "Ghubrah" (غبرة).

He then engaged in a fierce struggle against the corruptions of the environment, especially that well-known moral deviation and homosexuality, the scandal of which is reflected in all histories.

This great prophet endured many bitterness and suffered great heartaches. He exerted all his power to reform this deviant people—who were ugly in character and conduct—and to prevent them from their shameful acts, but he achieved no result. If a few individuals believed in him, they soon saved themselves from that polluted environment.

Finally, Lut عليه السلام despaired of them and turned to prayer, asking Allah for the salvation of himself and his family. Allah answered his prayer and saved that small group entirely, except for his wife—that old woman who not only refused to follow his teachings but sometimes even assisted his enemies.

Allah ordained the severest punishment for this people: first, He turned their cities upside down, and then He rained down a shower of dense stones upon them, such that they were all destroyed and even their corpses were obliterated!

Since all of this serves as a prelude to awakening the arrogant and heedless, at the end of this discourse, He adds: "And indeed, you pass by [the ruins of] them in the morning" (وَ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهِمْ مُصْبِحِينَ‌).

"And at night. Will you not then understand?" (وَ بِاللَّيْلِ أَ فَلا تَعْقِلُونَ‌). This expression is because the cities of the people of Lut were located on the route of the caravans of the people of Hijaz toward Sham (Syria), and they would pass by them during their day and night journeys. If they had ears to hear with their souls, they would have heard the agonizing and soul-crushing cries of this sinful and afflicted people, for the ruins of their cities teach a lesson in a silent language to all passersby and warn them against falling into the clutches of similar fates.

Yes, the lessons of warning are many, but those who take heed are few: "How many are the lessons, and how few are those who take heed!" [1] A similar meaning appears in Verse 76 of Surah Al-Hijr after recounting the story of the people of Lut: "And indeed, they [the ruins] are on an established road" (وَ إِنَّها لَبِسَبِيلٍ مُقِيمٍ‌).

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, this sentence has been interpreted in another way. When one of his companions asked about the interpretation of the verses "And indeed, you pass by them in the morning and at night. Will you not then understand?", he said:

"You pass by them in the Quran; when you recite the Quran, read what Allah has recounted to you of their news." [2]

This interpretation may refer to the secondary meaning of the verse and its inner depths (batn). In any case, there is no obstacle to combining the two interpretations, as both the physical remains of the people of Lut were before their eyes externally, and their news is present in the Holy Quran.

[^1]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Short Sayings, Saying 297.

[^2]: "Rawdat al-Kafi," as cited in "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 4, p. 432.

Ayahs

وَإِنَّ يُونُسَ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٣٩)
Indeed Jonah was one of the apostles. (139)
إِذْ أَبَقَ إِلَى ٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلْمَشْحُونِ (١٤٠)
When he absconded toward the laden ship, (140)
فَسَاهَمَ فَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُدْحَضِينَ (١٤١)
he drew lots with them and was the one to be condemned [as one to be thrown overboard]. (141)
فَٱلْتَقَمَهُ ٱلْحُوتُ وَهُوَ مُلِيمٌ (١٤٢)
Then the fish swallowed him while he was blameworthy. (142)
فَلَوْلَآ أَنَّهُۥ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُسَبِّحِينَ (١٤٣)
Had he not been one of those who celebrate Allah’s glory, (143)
لَلَبِثَ فِى بَطْنِهِۦٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ (١٤٤)
he would have surely remained in its belly till the day they will be resurrected. (144)
۞ فَنَبَذْنَـٰهُ بِٱلْعَرَآءِ وَهُوَ سَقِيمٌ (١٤٥)
Then We cast him on a bare shore, and he was sick. (145)
وَأَنۢبَتْنَا عَلَيْهِ شَجَرَةً مِّن يَقْطِينٍ (١٤٦)
So We made a gourd plant grow above him. (146)
وَأَرْسَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَىٰ مِا۟ئَةِ أَلْفٍ أَوْ يَزِيدُونَ (١٤٧)
We sent him to a [community of] hundred thousand or more, (147)
فَـَٔامَنُوا۟ فَمَتَّعْنَـٰهُمْ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ (١٤٨)
and they believed [in him]. So We provided for them for a while. (148)

Commentary

Yunus in the Crucible of Trial

This is the sixth and final account of the prophets and past nations mentioned in this Surah: the story of "Yunus" and his repentant people. Interestingly, in the five previous accounts—discussing the peoples of "Nuh," "Ibrahim," "Musa," "Harun," "Ilyas," and "Lut"—all concluded with the fact that those nations never awakened and were seized by divine punishment, while Allah saved those great prophets from among them.

However, in this story, the conclusion is the opposite. Upon witnessing a sign of divine punishment, the disbelieving people of Yunus awakened and repented. Consequently, Allah encompassed them with His grace and granted them material and spiritual blessings. He even subjected Yunus to difficulties and distress because of a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla) he committed by hastening his migration from among these people. In his case, He even used the term "absconded" (abaqa), which is typically used for a runaway slave!

This is an indication to you, the polytheists of Arabia, and to all humans throughout history: do you wish to be like those five nations or like the people of Yunus? Do you seek that grim and painful end, or this conclusion of goodness and prosperity? It depends on your own decision! In any case, this great prophet and his story are mentioned in several Surahs of the Holy Quran (including Surah Al-Anbiya, Yunus, Al-Qalam, and this Surah As-Saffat), with each reflecting a portion of his circumstances. In Surah As-Saffat, the primary emphasis is on the issue of Yunus’s flight, his entrapment, and his rescue.

First, as with the previous stories, it mentions his station of messengership, saying: "And indeed, Yunus was among the messengers" (وَ إِنَّ يُونُسَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ‌).

Yunus عليه السلام, like other prophets, began his call with monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against idolatry, and then proceeded to combat the corruptions prevalent in that environment. However, those prejudiced people, who blindly followed their ancestors, did not submit to his call.

Yunus عليه السلام continued to advise those misguided people with compassion and goodwill, like a kind father, but in response to this wise logic, he heard nothing from his enemies but fallacy and sophistry. Only a small group, perhaps not exceeding two people (a "worshiper" and a "scholar"!), believed in him.

Yunus preached to the extent that he nearly despaired of them. Some narrations state that at the suggestion of the worshiper (and considering the state of the misguided people), he decided to invoke a curse upon them [1]. This plan was carried out, and Yunus cursed them. Revelation came to him that at a certain time, divine punishment would descend. When the appointed time for the punishment drew near, Yunus left from among them along with the worshiper while he was angry. He reached the seashore, where he saw a ship full of people and cargo, and requested that they take him with them.

This is what the Quran refers to in the next verse, saying: "Remember when he fled to the laden ship" (إِذْ أَبَقَ إِلَى الْفُلْكِ الْمَشْحُونِ‌). The use of the term "absconded" (abaqa), from the root ibaq meaning a slave fleeing from his master, is a remarkable expression here. It shows to what extent a very minor "leaving of the better" by high-ranking prophets is met with strictness and reproach from Allah, to the point that He calls His prophet a runaway slave! Undoubtedly, Yunus was an infallible (ma'sum) prophet and never committed a sin, but it would have been better for him to exercise more patience and remain among his people until the final moments before the descent of punishment; perhaps they would have awakened.

It is true that according to some narrations he preached for forty years, but it would still have been better to add a few more days or hours to that. Because he did not do so, he was likened to a runaway slave.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 35.

In any case, Yunus boarded the ship. According to narrations, a massive fish blocked the ship's path, opening its mouth as if seeking food. The passengers said: "It seems there is a sinner among us! (who must become prey for this fish, and there is no choice but to use lots)." They cast lots, and the lot fell upon Yunus! According to one narration, they repeated the draw three times, and each time it came up in Yunus's name. Inevitably, they took Yunus and threw him into the mouth of the massive fish! The Quran refers to this event in the verses under discussion with a short sentence: "He cast lots with them and was among the losers" (فَساهَمَ فَكانَ مِنَ الْمُدْحَضِينَ‌).

Sahama is from the root sahm, which originally means an arrow, and sahama means drawing lots, because during the draw, names were written on arrows and mixed together, then one arrow was pulled out, and whoever's name it hit was the one chosen by the lot.

Mudhad is from the root idhad, meaning to invalidate, nullify, or defeat; here it means the lot fell against him.

Another interpretation suggests that the sea became stormy and the ship was heavily laden, with the danger of sinking threatening the passengers at every moment. There was no choice but to throw some individuals into the sea to lighten the ship. The lot fell on Yunus; they threw him into the sea, and exactly at that moment, a whale arrived and swallowed him.

In any case, the Quran says: "Then the massive fish swallowed him while he was blameworthy" (فَالْتَقَمَهُ الْحُوتُ وَ هُوَ مُلِيمٌ‌).

Altaqamahu is from the root iltiqam, meaning "to swallow." Mulim is originally from the root lawm, meaning blame (and when it moves to the if'al form, it gives the meaning of deserving blame).

It is certain that this blame and reproach were not for committing a major or minor sin, but rather the cause was solely the "leaving of the better" that he committed—namely, his haste in leaving his people and migrating from them.

However, the God who keeps fire safe in the heart of water and glass safe next to stone gave a creative command to this massive animal not to cause the slightest harm to His servant Yunus. He had to undergo an unprecedented period of imprisonment to realize his "leaving of the better" and to seek to make amends.

It is mentioned in a narration: "Allah revealed to the fish: 'Do not break a bone of his, nor sever a joint of his!'" [1]

Yunus very quickly realized the situation and turned toward Allah's threshold with his entire being, seeking forgiveness for his "leaving of the better" and requesting pardon from His holy presence. Here, a famous and profound litany is quoted from Yunus, which appears in verse 87 of Surah Al-Anbiya and is known among the people of gnosis as the "Dhikr Yunusiyyah":

فَنادى‌ فِي الظُّلُماتِ أَنْ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ‌: "And he called out within the depths of darkness: 'There is no deity except You; exalted are You. Indeed, I have been of the wrongdoers!'"

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 26, p. 165; the same meaning with slight variation appears in Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 37.

"I have wronged myself and fallen far from Your presence, and I have been caught by Your reproach and rebuke, which is a burning fire for me."

This sincere confession and this glorification coupled with remorse did its work, and as stated in verse 88 of Surah Al-Anbiya: فَاسْتَجَبْنا لَهُ وَ نَجَّيْناهُ مِنَ الْغَمِّ وَ كَذلِكَ نُنْجِي الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌: "So We responded to him and saved him from the distress. And thus do We save the believers."

Now let us see what the verses under discussion say in this regard. In a short sentence, it says: "Had he not been of those who glorify [Allah]..." (فَلَوْ لا أَنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الْمُسَبِّحِينَ‌).

"He would certainly have remained in its belly until the Day they are resurrected" (لَلَبِثَ فِي بَطْنِهِ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ‌). And this temporary prison would have turned into a permanent one, and that permanent prison would have become his graveyard! Regarding whether Yunus remaining in the belly of the fish until the Day of Resurrection (assuming he had abandoned glorification and repentance) would have been in a living or dead state, some commentators have mentioned several possibilities:

First: That both would remain alive, and Yunus would be confined as a prisoner in the belly of the fish until the Day of Judgment. Second: That Yunus would pass away and the fish would remain alive as his mobile graveyard. Third: That both Yunus and the fish would die, and the belly of the fish would be Yunus's grave, and the earth would be the fish's grave; he would be buried in the heart of the fish and the fish in the heart of the earth until the Day of Resurrection.

The verse under discussion cannot be definitive proof for any of these views, but several verses stating that everyone will die at the end of the world show that the continued life of Yunus or the fish until the Day of Judgment is not possible. Therefore, among these three interpretations, the third seems closer to the truth [1]. There is also the possibility that this expression is a metaphor for a very long duration—meaning he would remain in this prison for a long time—just as this expression is used in similar cases, such as saying "you must wait until Judgment Day for such-and-forth."

But let us not forget that all of this would have occurred only if he had abandoned glorification and repentance; however, that did not happen. In the shadow of glorifying his Lord, he was encompassed by His special pardon.

Then, as the Quran says: "But We cast him onto a barren shore while he was ill" (فَنَبَذْناهُ بِالْعَراءِ وَ هُوَ سَقِيمٌ‌). The massive fish came to a dry and vegetation-less shore and, by Allah's command, cast out the morsel that was too great for it. But it is evident that this strange prison had disrupted Yunus's physical health; he was released from this prison ill and weak.

We do not know exactly how long Yunus was in the belly of the fish, but it is certain that however long it was, he could not remain unaffected by its consequences. It is true that a divine command had been issued that Yunus should not be digested or absorbed by the fish's body, but this did not mean he would not carry traces of this imprisonment. Thus, a group of commentators have written that he emerged from the belly of the fish like a newborn chick, weak and without feathers, such that he had no power to move.

[^1]: It is noteworthy that the great commentator, the late Tabarsi, who usually collects various views under the verses, contents himself here with only this one possibility and says: "The belly of the fish would have become a grave for him until the Day of Resurrection."

Once again, divine grace came to his aid, for his body was ill and pained, and his limbs were tired and weak. The sun of the shore was hurting him; a delicate covering was needed for his body to rest under. The Quran says here: "And We caused to grow over him a gourd vine" so that he might rest in the shade of its broad, moist leaves (وَ أَنْبَتْنا عَلَيْهِ شَجَرَةً مِنْ يَقْطِينٍ‌).

Yaqtin, as many linguists and commentators have said, refers to any plant that has no trunk and possesses broad leaves, such as melon, gourd, cucumber, watermelon, and the like. However, many commentators and narrators of Hadith have specified here that it refers particularly to the gourd vine. (It should be noted that the word shajarah in the Arabic language is used for plants that have trunks and branches as well as those without, or in other words, it is more general than "tree" and "plant"). There is even a Hadith narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that someone said to him: "Do you love the gourd?" He replied: "Yes, it is the plant of my brother Yunus" [1]. They say the gourd vine, in addition to having broad, water-filled leaves that can provide good shade, does not attract flies to its leaves. Because of his stay in the fish's belly, Yunus's skin had become so thin and sensitive that he suffered from insects landing on it; he covered his body with this gourd vine to be safe from both the sun's sting and from insects.

[^1]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 7, p. 489.

Perhaps Allah wanted to complete the lesson He gave to Yunus in the belly of the fish at this stage; he had to feel the sun's rays and its heat on his thin skin so that in the future, in his position of leadership, he would exert more effort and struggle to save his nation from the burning fire of Hell. This same theme has been mentioned in some Islamic narrations [1].

We leave Yunus here and go to his people. When Yunus left the people in a state of anger and wrath, and the precursors of divine wrath appeared to them, they were severely shaken and came to their senses. They gathered around a scholar and wise man who was among them, and under his leadership, they turned to repentance.

In some narrations, it is stated that they moved collectively toward the desert and separated mothers from children, and animals from their young. Then they broke into weeping and raised their voices in lamentation, sincerely repenting for their sins and the shortcomings they had shown toward Allah's prophet, Yunus.

At this point, the veils of punishment were removed, and the event was diverted to the mountains, and the believing, repentant population was saved by divine grace [2]. After this event, Yunus came to his people to see what the punishment had done to them.

When he arrived, he was filled with wonder: how was it that on the day of his migration they were all idolaters, but now they had all become monotheists and worshipers of God?

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 436, Hadith 116. [^2]: In Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 35, this Hadith is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام.

The Quran says here: "And We sent him to [a population of] a hundred thousand or more" (وَ أَرْسَلْناهُ إِلى‌ مِائَةِ أَلْفٍ أَوْ يَزِيدُونَ‌).

"And they believed, so We gave them enjoyment [of life] for a time" (فَآمَنُوا فَمَتَّعْناهُمْ إِلى‌ حِينٍ‌). Of course, their general faith and repentance had occurred previously, but their detailed faith in Allah, His prophet Yunus, and his teachings and commands took place when Yunus returned among them.

It is noteworthy that it can be inferred from the Quranic verses that this renewed mission was toward the same previous people. The idea held by some that it was a new mission to a new people is not consistent with the apparent meaning of the verses.

For on one hand, we read here: "And they believed, so We gave them enjoyment for a time," meaning this people whom Yunus was commissioned to guide believed, and We granted them enjoyment until a specified time.

On the other hand, this same expression appears in Surah Yunus regarding the same former people: "Then has there not been a [single] city that believed so its faith benefited it except the people of Yunus? When they believed, We removed from them the punishment of disgrace in worldly life and gave them enjoyment for a time" (Yunus: 98).

Furthermore, it becomes clear from here that the meaning of "until a time" (ila hin) is the end of their lives and their natural term. Regarding why the above verses say "a hundred thousand, or more," and what is meant by "more," commentators have mentioned various interpretations. However, it appears that such expressions are for emphasis and the greatness of something, not because of doubt or uncertainty on the part of the Speaker [1].

Key Points

1. A Brief History of the Life of Yunus عليه السلام "Yunus," the son of "Matta," whose title is "Dhu al-Nun" (The Companion of the Fish), is so titled because his story is intertwined with the account of the fish, as we have mentioned. He is among the famous prophets who apparently stepped onto the stage of existence after Musa and Harun.

Some consider him a descendant of "Hud" and his mission to be the guidance of the remnants of the people of Thamud. The land of his appearance was a region of Iraq called "Nineveh" [2]. Some have written that his appearance was around 825 years before the birth of Christ عليه السلام, and currently, near Kufa by the river, there is a famous tomb named "Yunus."

In some books, it is stated he was a prophet from the Children of Israel who was sent to the people of Nineveh after Sulayman.

[^1]: Therefore, "or" (aw) here means "rather" (bal). [^2]: "Nineveh" is the name of several places: first, a city near Mosul; second, a region around Kufa in the direction of Karbala; and a city in Asia Minor, the capital of the Assyrian kingdom located on the Tigris (Dehkhoda Encyclopedia). Others have written that Nineveh was the largest city of the Assyrian country, built on the eastern bank of the Tigris opposite Mosul (Farhang-e Qisas-e Quran).

In the "Book of Jonah" from the Old Testament (Torah), there are detailed discussions about Yunus under the name "Jonah son of Amittai."

According to this account, he was commissioned to go to the great city of Nineveh and rise against the wickedness of the people. It then mentions other events that bear a great resemblance to what is in the Quran, with the difference that according to Islamic narrations, Yunus rose to call his people and fulfilled his duty in this regard, and after the people rejected his call, he cursed them and left from among them, and the incident of the ship and the fish occurred. However, the Torah contains a jarring statement, specifying that before performing his mission, he wanted to resign! Therefore, he rose and fled, and the incident of the ship and the fish occurred.

Even more strange is that the "Torah" says when Allah removed the punishment from his people because of their repentance, Yunus was very upset and his anger was kindled! [1] From the chapters of the Torah, it is inferred that Yunus was commissioned twice: in the first mission he refused and was afflicted with that painful fate; the second time he was commissioned to go to the same city of Nineveh. The people of Nineveh awakened, believed in God, turned to repentance for their sins, and were encompassed by divine pardon—and this was the very pardon that Yunus did not like!

From a comparison of what is in the Quran and Islamic narrations with what is in the current Torah, it becomes clear to what extent the "distorted Torah" has lowered the status of this great prophet—sometimes attributing to him the refusal of the mission of messengership, and sometimes becoming angry at the inclusion of a repentant people in the pardon and mercy of the Lord. These are the things that show the current Torah is by no means a reliable book. In any case, he is among the great prophets whom the Quran mentions with greatness.

2. How Did Yunus Stay Alive in the Belly of the Fish? We said there is no clear evidence as to how long Yunus stayed in the belly of the fish. Nine hours, three days, or several weeks—some narrations say nine hours, some three days, some more, and even up to forty days—but there is no definitive document for any of these views. Only in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام, is Yunus's stay in the fish's belly mentioned as 9 hours [1]. Some Sunni commentators have even mentioned the duration as one hour [2].

But whatever it was, undoubtedly this stay is not an ordinary matter. A human cannot stay alive for more than a few minutes in an environment where there is no air. If we see that a fetus stays alive in the mother's womb for months, it is because its respiratory system has not yet started working and it receives the necessary oxygen only through the mother's blood.

Therefore, the story of Yunus is undoubtedly a miracle, and this is not the first miracle we encounter in the Quran. The same God who kept Ibrahim عليه السلام safe in the heart of the fire, and saved Musa and the Children of Israel from drowning by creating dry paths in the middle of the sea, and delivered Nuh عليه السلام from that massive and widespread flood by means of a simple ship and brought him safely to land—that same God has the power to keep one of His special servants safe for a time in the belly of a massive fish.

Of course, the existence of such large fish in the past and today is not a strange thing. Currently, there are massive fish called "whales" that are more than 30 meters long and are the largest animals on earth, and their liver alone weighs over a ton! In this very Surah, we read the stories of previous prophets who were saved from the clutches of calamities in miraculous ways, and Yunus is the last of them in this series.

3. Great Lessons in a Small Story! We know that the presentation of these accounts in the Holy Quran is all for educational purposes, for the Quran is not a book of stories; it is a book of human-building and education. Great lessons can be taken from this amazing account:

A: A transgression, even in the form of a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla), by a great prophet is significant in the presence of Allah and carries a penalty. Of course, because the station of the prophets is very high, a small oversight on their part is sometimes equivalent to a major sin committed by others. For this reason, we saw that in this story, Allah called him a runaway slave, and in narrations, it was said that the passengers of the ship said: "There is a disobedient person among us!" Finally, Allah trapped him in a terrifying prison, and after repentance and returning to Allah, he was released from that prison with a pained and ill body. This is so everyone may know that transgression is not accepted from anyone. The greatness of the station of the prophets and the saints of Allah also lies in the fact that they are servants obedient to His command; otherwise, no one has a kinship relationship with Allah. Of course, this is a sign of the greatness of this great prophet's station that Allah exercises such strictness regarding him.

B: In this same story (in the part that appears in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 87), the way of salvation for believers from grief, sorrow, entrapment, and problems is introduced as the same path that Yunus عليه السلام traversed: and that is the confession of error in the presence of the Truth, and glorification, sanctification, repentance, and returning to Him.

C: This event shows how a sinful people deserving of punishment can change the course of their history in the final moments, return to the embrace of divine love and mercy, and be saved—provided they realize before the opportunity is lost and, if they can, choose a "scholar" to lead them.

D: This event shows that faith in Allah and repentance from sin, in addition to spiritual effects and blessings, also bring worldly outward favors to man; it creates prosperity and development and becomes a cause for long life and benefiting from the blessings of life. A similar meaning appears in the story of Nuh عليه السلام, the explanation of which we will read, God willing, in the commentary of Surah "Nuh."

E: The power of Allah is so vast and extensive that nothing is difficult for it, to the extent that He can keep a human safe in the mouth and belly of a massive and terrifying creature and send him out safely. These show that all the causes of this world are tools of His will and all are subject to His command.

4. Answer to a Question A question arises here: in the accounts of other nations in the verses of the Quran, it is stated that at the time of the descent of punishment (the punishment of eradication that descended to destroy rebellious nations), repentance and supplication were ineffective. How was this matter an exception in the case of the people of Yunus?

Two answers can be given to this question: First: The punishment had not yet descended; only minor signs, which are of the nature of warnings, were seen, and they utilized these warnings in time and repented and believed before the descent of the punishment. Second: This punishment was not the "punishment of eradication" (adhab al-isti'sal), but was of the nature of the reprimands He gave to various nations before the descent of the foundational punishment so they might awaken before the opportunity passed and adopt the path of piety—like the various punishments of the people of Pharaoh before the drowning.

5. "The Lot" (Qur'ah) and Its Legitimacy in Islam In the narrations concerning the lot and its legitimacy, we read that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said: "What judgment is more just than the lot when the matter is delegated to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic? He says: 'He cast lots and was among the losers'" [1]. This indicates that the lot, when matters reach a dead end and there is no other solution and the matter is delegated to Allah, is truly a way-opener, just as in the story of Yunus it coincided exactly with reality.

This meaning is narrated with more clarity in another Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, who said: "No people engaged in a dispute (or casting lots) and then delegated their affair to Allah except that the lot of the one in the right came out" [1]. We have provided more explanation regarding this issue in the book Al-Qawa'id al-Fiqhiyyah.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 37 (Hadith 6). [^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Kitab al-Qada, Vol. 18, Chapter on Judging by the Lot in Difficult Cases (Chapter 13), Hadith 5.

Commentary

Ugly and Scandalous Accusations

After mentioning six stories from the history of previous Prophets and the instructive lessons hidden within each, the discourse shifts to another subject closely related to the Arab polytheists. It addresses various forms of their polytheism, brings them to a rigorous trial, and crushes their superstitious thoughts with diverse arguments.

The issue is that a group of Arab polytheists, due to intellectual decline and a total lack of knowledge, compared Allah to themselves, attributing children and sometimes a spouse to Him.

Among them, the tribes of "Juhaynah," "Sulaym," "Khuza'ah," and "Bani Malih" believed that the "angels" were the daughters of Allah! Many Arab polytheists also imagined the "Jinn" to be His children, or some even attributed a spouse from the "Jinn" to the Lord! These baseless and superstitious fancies had completely diverted them from the path of truth, to the extent that the traces of Monotheism and the Oneness of God had vanished from among them.

It is mentioned in a Hadith that "the ant imagines its Lord has two antennae like itself!"

Indeed, narrow-mindedness leads man to comparison—comparing the Creator to the created—and this comparison is the worst factor of deviation in the recognition of Allah.

In any case, the Quran first addresses those who imagined the angels to be the daughters of Allah and responds to them through three methods: "empirical," "rational," and "narrative":

First, It says: "Ask them: Does your Lord have daughters while they have sons?!" (فَاسْتَفْتِهِمْ أَ لِرَبِّكَ الْبَناتُ وَ لَهُمُ الْبَنُونَ) [^1] How is it that you attribute to Allah what you do not like for yourselves? (This statement is according to their false belief, as they intensely disliked daughters and were deeply fond of sons, because sons played an effective role in their lives, wars, and raids, while daughters provided them no such help).

[^1]: "Istaftehim" is from the root "Istifta," which originally comes from "Fatwa," meaning the answer to difficult problems.

Undoubtedly, sons and daughters are equal in terms of humanity and value before Allah, and the criterion for the personality of both is purity and piety. However, the Quran's argument here is what is termed "citing the opponent's premises"—taking the opponent's own claims and turning them back upon them.

Similar meanings appear in other Surahs of the Quran; for instance, in Surah An-Najm, verse 22, we read: أَ لَكُمُ الذَّكَرُ وَ لَهُ الْأُنْثى‌ تِلْكَ إِذاً قِسْمَةٌ ضِيزى‌: "Is the male for you and the female for Him? That, then, is an unfair division!"

Then, addressing the sensory aspect of the issue, again through a rhetorical question, It says: "Or did We create the angels as females while they were witnesses?" (أَمْ خَلَقْنَا الْمَلائِكَةَ إِناثاً وَ هُمْ شاهِدُونَ).

Undoubtedly, their answer in this regard was negative, as none of them could claim to have been present during the creation of the angels.

Once again, It returns to a rational argument derived from their own mental premises and says: "Behold, it is out of their [gross] falsehood that they say..." (أَلا إِنَّهُمْ مِنْ إِفْكِهِمْ لَيَقُولُونَ).

"'Allah has begotten [children],' and indeed, they are liars!" (وَلَدَ اللَّهُ وَ إِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ).

"Has He chosen daughters over sons?!" (أَصْطَفَى الْبَناتِ عَلَى الْبَنِينَ).

"What is [the matter] with you? How do you judge?!" Do you even understand what you are saying? (ما لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ).

"Has the time not come for you to abandon these scandalous and ugly absurdities and superstitions? 'Will you not then take heed?'" (أَ فَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ). These statements are so void and baseless that if a person possesses even a shred of intellect and discernment and reflects upon them, they would realize their falsehood.

After refuting their superstitious claim with a sensory argument and a rational argument, It proceeds to the third argument, which is the narrative proof, saying: If what you say were true, there should be a trace of it in the previous scriptures. "Or do you have a clear authority [in this regard]?" (أَمْ لَكُمْ سُلْطانٌ مُبِينٌ).

If you do, "Then bring your Book, if you are truthful!" (فَأْتُوا بِكِتابِكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ). In which book? In which writing? In which heavenly revelation has such a thing appeared, and to which Prophet was it revealed?! This statement is similar to another dialogue the Quran has with the idolaters. After stating that they have made the angels—who are servants of Allah—into daughters, and they claim that if Allah had not willed it, we would not have worshipped them, It says: أَمْ آتَيْناهُمْ كِتاباً مِنْ قَبْلِهِ فَهُمْ بِهِ مُسْتَمْسِكُونَ: "Or have We given them a book before this, to which they are adhering?" (Az-Zukhruf: 21).

No, these are not the essence of heavenly books; these are superstitions passed down from one generation to another and from one ignorant person to another, having no rational basis or source. As is also pointed out in the conclusion of the same verse in Surah Az-Zukhruf.

In the next verse, It addresses another superstition of the Arab polytheists: the relationship they claimed between "Allah" and the "Jinn"! The discourse shifts from "direct address" to the "third person," as if they are so worthless that they no longer deserve to be addressed directly. It says: "And they have claimed a kinship between Him and the Jinn!" (وَ جَعَلُوا بَيْنَهُ وَ بَيْنَ الْجِنَّةِ نَسَباً). What was this kinship they claimed between Allah and the Jinn? Various interpretations have been mentioned in response to this question.

Some have said: They were dualists and believed (we seek refuge in Allah) that God and Satan are brothers! God is the creator of good and Satan is the creator of evil.

This interpretation seems unlikely, as dualists or Manichaeans were not well-known among the Arabs; this superstition existed in regions like Iran during the Sassanid era.

Others have considered "Jinn" and "Angels" to be the same in meaning, because Jinn originally means a being that is covered and hidden from sight, and since angels are not seen with the eye, this word was applied to them. Therefore, they say the kinship refers to the same relationship the pre-Islamic Arabs attributed to them, calling them the daughters of Allah.

This interpretation also seems difficult because the apparent meaning of the verses under discussion is that they pursue two separate points. Furthermore, applying the term "Jinn" to angels is not common or familiar, especially in the Holy Quran.

A third interpretation some have given for this verse is that they imagined the "Jinn" to be the spouses of Allah and the angels to be His daughters!! This also seems unlikely, as applying the word "kinship" (nasab) to "marriage" is also far-fetched.

The most appropriate interpretation is that "kinship" (nasab) refers to any kind of relation or connection, even if it does not involve blood relation. We know that a group of Arab polytheists worshipped the Jinn and considered them partners to Allah, and in this way, they established a connection between them and Allah.

In any case, the Holy Quran strongly denies this superstitious belief and says:

"The Jinn, whom the superstitious idolaters imagine as their deities or for whom they claim a kinship with Allah—yes, those very Jinn—well knew that these superstitious idolaters will be brought [for judgment] in the court of Divine Justice for accounting and punishment." (وَ لَقَدْ عَلِمَتِ الْجِنَّةُ إِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ).

Some have mentioned another possibility in interpreting this verse: that it means "The seductive Jinn know that they themselves will be brought to Allah's court for accounting and punishment," but the first interpretation seems more appropriate. [^1]

Then It adds: "Exalted is Allah above what they describe (this ignorant and misguided group)!" (سُبْحانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ).

[^1]: In the first case, the pronoun "hum" (they) refers to the "polytheists," and in the second case, it refers to the "Jinn."

"Except for the description that the sincere servants of Allah (who have awareness and knowledge regarding Him) provide, no description is worthy of His Holy Essence." (إِلَّا عِبادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ).

Thus, any description people make of Allah is incorrect, and Allah is pure and exalted above it, except for the description provided by His sincere servants—servants who are free from any polytheism, caprice, ignorance, and deviation, and who do not describe Allah except by what He Himself has permitted. [^1] Regarding "the sincere servants of Allah," we had a discussion under verse 128 of this same Surah.

Indeed, to know Allah, one must not follow the superstitions left behind by ignorant peoples, which one is ashamed to even mention. One must go to the sincere servants whose words make the human soul fly to the heights of the heavens and dissolve it in the light of His Oneness, washing away any taint of polytheism from the heart and erasing any "anthropomorphism" or "comparison" from the mind.

One must seek the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the sermons of Nahj al-Balagha by Ali عليه السلام, and the profound supplications of Imam Sajjad عليه السلام in Sahifa Sajjadiya, and recognize Allah in the light of the descriptions of these servants of Allah.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says in one place:

"He has not informed the intellects of the limits of His attributes, yet He has not veiled them from the necessary recognition of Him. He is the One for whom the signs of existence testify, even to the heart of the denier. Exalted is Allah far above what those who liken Him to creation or those who deny Him say." [^1] Elsewhere, in describing the Lord, he says:

"Imaginations cannot reach Him to estimate Him, and intellects cannot conceive Him to picture Him. Senses cannot perceive Him to feel Him, and hands cannot touch Him. He does not change with any state, nor does He transform through conditions. Nights and days do not wear Him out, and light and darkness do not change Him. He is not described by parts, nor by limbs and organs, nor by any accident, nor by otherness or portions. It cannot be said He has a limit or an end, nor a cessation or a goal." [^2] And in another place, he says:

"And whoever says 'In what is He?' has contained Him. And whoever says 'Upon what is He?' has vacated other places of Him. He is a Being not from a beginning, and Existent not from non-existence. He is with everything, but not by association, and different from everything, but not by separation." [^1]

[^1]: According to this interpretation, the phrase "except the servants of Allah" is an exception from the pronoun in "they describe," but some have considered it an exception from the pronoun in "brought [for judgment]" and have various interpretations for it; however, the first interpretation seems more appropriate, and in any case, it is a "disjointed" (munqati') exception.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 49. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 186.

And Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn, the Master of Prostrators عليه السلام, says in Sahifa Sajjadiya:

"Praise be to Allah, the First without a first before Him, and the Last without a last after Him. He whom the eyes of onlookers fail to see and the imaginations of describers fail to depict." [^2]

Indeed, the recognition of Allah must be learned from the great school of these "righteous servants of Allah," and the lesson of theology must be studied in this academy.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 1. [^2]: Sahifa Sajjadiya, First Supplication.

Commentary

False Claims!

In the preceding verses, the various deities of the polytheists were discussed; the verses under consideration follow the same theme, presenting a point regarding this matter every few verses.

First, it brings up the discussion that the whisperings of you idolaters have no effect on the hearts of the pure and the righteous; it is only your own contaminated hearts and hellish souls, inclined toward corruption, that submit to these whisperings. It says: "Indeed, you and what you worship..." (فَإِنَّكُمْ وَ ما تَعْبُدُونَ‌).

"You cannot (with it) mislead anyone and divert them from Allah through fitnah and corruption" (ما أَنْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ بِفاتِنِينَ‌) [1]

"Except those who are to burn in the Hellfire!" (إِلَّا مَنْ هُوَ صالِ الْجَحِيمِ‌). Contrary to what the proponents of the school of determinism (Jabr) have imagined, these verses are a proof against that school and an allusion to the reality that no one can consider themselves excused in the face of deviations, claiming, "I was deceived and led into idolatry." It states: You idolaters do not have the power of "fitnah" and deceiving people, except for those who, by their own will, choose the path of Hell.

The evidence for this is the expression "صَالِ الْجَحِيمِ" (burning in Hell), because "صَالِي" (Saali) is originally an active participle (Ism al-Fa'il). Usually, when the active participle form is used for a rational being, its meaning implies performing an action out of will and choice, similar to "Qatil" (killer), "Jalis" (sitter), and "Darib" (striker). Therefore, "Saal al-Jahim" means one who desires to burn himself in the fire of Hell, and in this way, it closes the path of excuse for all the deviated.

[1]: Grammatically, according to the famous view, this verse and the one above are structured as follows: "Ma" in "Ma Ta'budun" is a relative pronoun (Ma al-Mawsulah) and is conjoined to the noun of "Inna," and the sentence "Ma antum 'alayhi bi-fatinin" is its predicate (Khabar), with the proviso that the "Ma" in "Ma antum" is negating (Nafiyah), and the pronoun in "'alayhi" refers to Allah. Collectively, the result is: "Indeed, you and your deities that you worship are not able to mislead anyone away from Allah because of them, except those who burn in the fire of Hell due to their own bad choice." Others have considered the verse "Fa-innakum wa ma ta'budun" as an independent sentence meaning "Be with your deities," then the next verse says: "You cannot mislead anyone by means of them except those who wish to be hell-bound."

It is surprising that some well-known commentators have translated the verse as: "You cannot deceive anyone except those for whom it has been predestined to be hellish!" Truly, if this is the meaning of the verse, why do the Prophets come? For what purpose were the Heavenly Books revealed? What meaning do the accounting, the Book, and the blame and reproach of the idolaters in the verses of the Quran have? And where would the justice of God go?! Yes, it must be accepted that admitting to the school of determinism completely invalidates the authenticity of the school of the Prophets, distorts all its concepts, and leads all divine and human values to destruction.

It is necessary to note that "Saali" is from the root "Sala" (rhyming with "Sard"), meaning to kindle a fire, to enter the fire, or to be burned and roasted by it. "Fatin" is the active participle from the root "Fitnah," meaning a troublemaker or a deceiver.

After these three verses, which clarify the issue of human free will in the face of the mischief-seeking and seduction of the idolaters, within three other verses, it speaks of the high status of the angels of Allah—the same angels whom the idolaters imagined to be the daughters of God. Interestingly, it expresses the words from their own tongues, saying: "And there is not one of us but has a known station" (وَ ما مِنَّا إِلَّا لَهُ مَقامٌ مَعْلُومٌ‌) [1]

"And indeed, we are those who stand in ranks (to obey Allah's command), and we keep our eyes on His order" (وَ إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الصَّافُّونَ‌).

"And indeed, we are those who glorify Him, and we sanctify Him from that which is not worthy of His Pure Essence" (وَ إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْمُسَبِّحُونَ‌). Yes, we are servants who have our souls and hearts in our hands; we always have our eyes on His order and our ears to His command. How far are we from being the children of God? We sanctify Him from these ugly and false attributions, and we are disgusted and repelled by these superstitions and delusions of the polytheists.

In reality, the three verses above refer to three aspects of the attributes of the angels:

First, each has a rank and position that they do not exceed.

Second, they are constantly ready to obey Allah's command in the realm of creation and to execute His orders across the expanse of the universe. This statement is similar to what appears in verses 26 and 27 of Surah Al-Anbiya: "بَلْ عِبادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَ هُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ‌": "Rather, they are honored servants. They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command."

Third, they constantly glorify Allah and sanctify Him from what is not worthy of His status.

Since these two sentences (إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الصَّافُّونَ and إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْمُسَبِّحُونَ‌) imply "exclusivity" (Hasr) in Arabic literature, some commentators have inferred that the angels wish to say with this expression: "Only we are truly obedient to Allah's command, and we are His true glorifiers," implying that the "obedience" and "glorification" of the children of Adam are not significant compared to the work of the angels.

It is noteworthy that a group of commentators, under these verses, have narrated a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله who said: "There is no place in the heavens the size of a span except that there is an angel there praying and glorifying Allah" [1]! In another narration, the same meaning is expressed as: "There is no place in the heaven the size of a footstep except that there is an angel there in prostration or standing!" [2] And in another narration from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: One day he said to his companions who were sitting in a circle: "The heaven groaned (from the weight of its burden), and it has the right to groan! There is no place in it the size of a footstep except that there is an angel upon it in bowing (Ruku') or prostration!" Then he recited: وَ إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الصَّافُّونَ وَ إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْمُسَبِّحُونَ‌ [3]. These various expressions are a subtle metaphor for the fact that the universe is full of those who obey the Lord and glorify Him.

Then, in the final four verses, it points to one of the unjustified excuses of these polytheists regarding this issue of idolatry and other matters, and responds to them, saying: "And indeed, they used to say..." (وَ إِنْ كانُوا لَيَقُولُونَ‌) 4

"If only we had with us a reminder (a Heavenly Book) from the ancients..." (لَوْ أَنَّ عِنْدَنا ذِكْراً مِنَ الْأَوَّلِينَ‌).

"We would have been the sincere servants of Allah" (لَكُنَّا عِبادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ‌). Do not speak so much of the sincere servants and those whom God has purified, and do not boast to us of great Prophets like Noah, Abraham, Moses, and others; we too, if we had been included in Allah's grace and one of the Heavenly Books had been revealed to us, would have been among these sincere servants! This is exactly like the talk of backward and failing students who, to cover up their laziness, say: "If we also had a good teacher and master, we would have been among the top-ranking students!"

The next verse says that this wish of theirs has now been fulfilled, and the greatest Heavenly Book of Allah, the Glorious Quran, has been revealed to them. However, these boastful liars "disbelieved in it, and they took the path of opposition, denial, and enmity; but soon they will know the result of their actions" (فَكَفَرُوا بِهِ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ‌) [1]. Do not boast and brag so much, and do not consider yourselves worthy of being in the ranks of the sincere servants of Allah; your lie has been exposed, and your claims turned out to be hollow. No book better than the Quran can be imagined, and no school is better than the educational school of Islam, but see how you treated this Heavenly Book? Wait for the painful consequences of your disbelief and lack of faith.

[1]: This sentence actually has an omitted part and is estimated as: "So when the Book came to them, and it is the Quran, they disbelieved in it; so they will soon know the consequence of their disbelief."

[1] and [2]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 8, p. 5581. [3]: Ad-Durr al-Manthur (as cited in Al-Mizan, Vol. 17, p. 188).

[1]: In some narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, we read that this verse has been interpreted as referring to the Infallible Imams. It is possible that this interpretation is of the nature of likening the status of the Imams to the angels; meaning, just as they have specific stations and duties, so do we.
