Commentary

Hizbullah is Victorious!

Following the various discussions regarding the struggles of the great Prophets and the obstructions caused by the## Commentary

Hizbullah is Victorious!

Following the various discussions regarding the struggles of the great Prophets and the obstructions caused by the faithless polytheists throughout the verses of this Surah, as we approach the final verses, the most important issue in this regard is stated. The conclusion is presented in the most sublime manner: the news of the final victory of the army of God over the army of Satan and the enemies of Truth. This is so that the few believers who were under the pressure of the enemies of Islam in Mecca at the time of the revelation of these verses—as well as all deprived believers in every age and time—may be heartened by this great divine promise, wash the dust of despair from their hearts and souls, and become prepared and steadfast for the continued struggle against the army of falsehood.

He says: "Our word has already been established for Our servants, the messengers" (وَ لَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنا لِعِبادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ‌).

"That they shall indeed be the ones helped" (إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنْصُورُونَ‌).

"And that Our army shall indeed be the victors" (وَ إِنَّ جُنْدَنا لَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ‌). What explicit and eloquent phrasing, and what a soul-nourishing and hope-inspiring promise! Indeed, the victory of the army of Truth over falsehood, the triumph of the soldiers of Allah (Jund-Allah), and Allah's aid to His sent and sincere servants are among His certain promises and definitive Sunnahs (divine laws). This is introduced in the above verses with the phrase "سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنا" (Our word/decree has preceded), meaning this promise and law have existed from the beginning.

Similar themes appear in many other verses of the Holy Quran. In Surah Ar-Rum, verse 47, we read: وَ كانَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنا نَصْرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌ "And it was a duty incumbent upon Us to help the believers!" And in Surah Al-Hajj, verse 40: وَ لَيَنْصُرَنَّ اللَّهُ مَنْ يَنْصُرُهُ‌ "Allah will certainly help those who stand up to help His cause."

In Surah Ghafir, verse 51, we read: إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رسُلَنا وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ "We will indeed help Our messengers and those who believe in the life of this world and on the Day when the witnesses will stand forth."

Finally, in Surah Al-Mujadila, verse 21, He speaks with absolute decisiveness of this triumph and victory as a definitive law: كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَ رُسُلِي‌ "Allah has decreed: 'I and My messengers shall certainly prevail'!" It is obvious that Allah, who is Powerful over all things and whose promises never fail, can fulfill this great promise of His and, like the immutable laws of the universe, grant victory to the men of Truth without fail.

This divine promise is one of the most important matters that heartens the wayfarers of the path of Truth and gives them spirit and life. Whenever they grow weary, they refresh their breath with it, and new blood flows through their veins.

An Important Question

A question arises here: If the divine will and intention are set upon helping the Prophets and the victory of the believers, why do we observe throughout the eventful history of mankind that some Prophets were martyred and groups of believers faced defeat? If this is an immutable divine law, then what is the reason for these exceptions? In response, we say:

Firstly: Victory has a broad meaning and is not always synonymous with an outward and physical triumph over the enemy. Sometimes victory is the victory of the school of thought (Maktab), and this is the most important kind of victory. Suppose the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله had been martyred in one of the battles, yet we see his religion has encompassed the world; could we interpret such martyrdom as defeat?

A clearer example is that Imam Hussain عليه السلام and his companions truly drank the nectar of martyrdom in Karbala, but their goal was to expose the ugly face of the "Umayyads" who claimed the caliphate of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله but were in reality returning Islamic society to the age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah). They achieved this great goal, alerted the Muslims to their danger, and saved Islam from collapse. Can it be said they were defeated in Karbala?! The important thing is that the Prophets and the divine hosts—the believers—were able to advance their goals in the world despite all the continuous and organized efforts of the enemies of Truth, gaining many followers and continuing their ideological line. They stood tall against all those storms and even in today's world, they have drawn the attention of the majority of the world's people.

There is another type of victory, which is gradual victory over the enemy over centuries. Sometimes one generation enters the field and does not achieve victory, but future generations take up their work and reach victory (like the final victory of the army of Islam over the Crusaders after two hundred years!). This too is considered a victory for the collective.

Secondly: It must not be forgotten that Allah's promise regarding the triumph of the believers is a conditional promise, not an absolute one. Many mistakes stem from a lack of attention to this reality.

For in the verses under discussion, the words "عبادنا" (Our servants) and "جندنا" (Our army), or other similar expressions found in other verses of the Quran—such as حِزْبَ اللَّهِ (the party of Allah), الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا (those who strive in Our cause), and وَ لَيَنْصُرَنَّ اللَّهُ مَنْ يَنْصُرُهُ (Allah will surely help those who help Him)—are all clear evidence of the conditions for victory.

We want to be neither a striving believer nor a sincere soldier, yet we expect to triumph over the enemies of truth and justice! We want to move in the divine path using satanic thoughts and programs, and then we wonder why we are defeated by enemies. Have we fulfilled our promises that we should demand Allah fulfill His?! In the Battle of Uhud, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله had promised victory to the Muslims, and they were indeed victorious in the first stage of the battle. However, a group began thinking about collecting spoils, creating division and hypocrisy, and abandoning the command of the Messenger of Allah. They were negligent in protecting the gains of the initial victory and the pass of Uhud, and this very matter caused their final defeat in that battle.

It seems a group who considered themselves entitled came to the Prophet of Islam and asked in a particular tone: "So what happened to the promise of victory?"

The Quran gave them a very interesting answer which supports our point. It said:

وَ لَقَدْ صَدَقَكُمُ اللَّهُ وَعْدَهُ إِذْ تَحُسُّونَهُمْ بِإِذْنِهِ حَتَّى إِذا فَشِلْتُمْ وَ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي الْأَمْرِ وَ عَصَيْتُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما أَراكُمْ ما تُحِبُّونَ مِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُرِيدُ الدُّنْيا وَ مِنْكُمْ مَنْ يُرِيدُ الْآخِرَةَ ثُمَّ صَرَفَكُمْ عَنْهُمْ لِيَبْتَلِيَكُمْ وَ لَقَدْ عَفا عَنْكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌:

"And Allah did indeed fulfill His promise to you (regarding victory over the enemy at Uhud) when you were killing them by His permission, until you lost heart and fell to disputing about the order and disobeyed after He had shown you that which you loved. Among you were some who desired this world, and among you were some who desired the Hereafter. Then He turned you away from them that He might test you. And He has certainly forgiven you, and Allah is full of bounty to the believers" (Al-Imran, verse 152).

Expressions such as "فشلتم" (you lost heart/became weak), "تنازعتم" (you disputed), and "عصيتم" (you disobeyed) clearly show that they abandoned the conditions for divine assistance and victory over the enemy; consequently, they did not reach their objective.

Indeed, Allah never promised that anyone who simply calls himself a Muslim or a soldier of Islam, or speaks of being part of Jund-Allah or Hizbullah, will prevail over the enemy in every scene. This divine promise is reserved for those who desire Allah's pleasure from the depths of their hearts and souls, and who follow His commands in action, never forgetting piety and trustworthiness.

We have provided a similar question and answer regarding "supplication" (Dua) and the divine "promise of acceptance" [1].

Then, in the continuation of these verses, both to comfort the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers and to emphasize victory, as well as to threaten the oblivious polytheists, He says: "So turn away from them and leave them alone for a time" (فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّى حِينٍ‌).

This is a meaningful and terrifying threat that stems from the certainty of final victory, especially since the expression "حتى حين" (until a time) is stated vaguely. But until when? Until the time of Hijrah? Until the Battle of Badr? Until the Conquest of Mecca? Or until the conditions for the final and general uprising of Muslims against these blind-hearted people are met? It is not precisely specified.

Similar expressions are seen in other verses of the Quran. Sometimes it says: فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللَّهِ‌ "So turn away from them and put your trust in Allah" (An-Nisa, 81). Elsewhere it says: قُلِ اللَّهُ ثُمَّ ذَرْهُمْ فِي خَوْضِهِمْ يَلْعَبُونَ‌ "Say: 'Allah,' then leave them to play in their vain discourse" (Al-An'am, 91).

Then He emphasizes this sentence with another threat, saying: "And watch them (how hollow are their obstinacies, their lies, their superstitions, and their stubbornness), for soon they too shall see (the grim result of their actions)" (وَ أَبْصِرْهُمْ فَسَوْفَ يُبْصِرُونَ‌). Soon they will see your victory and that of the believers, and their own humiliating defeat in this world, as well as the divine punishment in the next world.

And since these shameless, stubborn people constantly repeated the question: "What happened to the promised divine punishment? And if you are telling the truth, why the delay?" the Quran says in response to them with a threatening tone: "Do they seek to hasten Our punishment?"

Sometimes they say: مَتى‌ هذَا الْوَعْدُ (When is this promise?) and sometimes they say: مَتى‌ هذَا الْفَتْحُ‌ (When will this victory come?) (أَ فَبِعَذابِنا يَسْتَعْجِلُونَ‌).

"But when it descends into their courtyard and their days become dark and gloomy, that day they will realize how evil and dangerous is the morning of those who were warned" (فَإِذا نَزَلَ بِساحَتِهِمْ فَساءَ صَباحُ الْمُنْذَرِينَ‌) [1]. The use of the word "ساحة" (courtyard or the space between houses) is to visualize the descent of punishment into the very heart of their lives and to show the transformation of their center of peace into a center of terror and anxiety.

The expression "صَباحُ الْمُنْذَرِينَ‌" (the morning of those warned) may refer to the fact that divine punishment descends upon this stubborn and oppressive people—like many previous nations—in the early morning.

Or it means that while everyone expects their morning to begin with goodness and blessing, these people have a bad, dark, and gloomy morning ahead of them.

Or it could mean that morning is the time of waking up; they too will wake up at a time when no way of salvation remains and the matter is already decided.

[^1]: This sentence has an omitted part, and the estimation is: "فساء الصباح صباح المنذرين" (Then evil is the morning, the morning of those warned). [^1]: Refer to Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, under verse 186 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Commentary

Do Not Pay Them Heed!

We have noted that the final verses of this Surah serve as a means of consolation for the Prophet and the true believers, and a threat to the stubborn disbelievers.

The first two verses of the passage under discussion are the same as those mentioned previously, repeated here once more for emphasis. In a threatening tone, He says: "Turn away from them and leave them to themselves for a specified time" (وَ تَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّى حِينٍ).

"Observe their stubbornness, obstructionism, denial, and rejection, for they too shall soon see the consequence of their actions!" (وَ أَبْصِرْ فَسَوْفَ يُبْصِرُونَ). As mentioned, this repetition is for emphasis, so they may know it is a certainty that they will soon witness their punishment, defeat, and failure, and will be ensnared by the bitter results of their deeds, while the victory of the believers is certain and undeniable.

Alternatively, it may be because He first threatens them with worldly punishment and the second time with Divine punishment and retribution in the Hereafter.

Then, He concludes the Surah with three meaningful sentences regarding "Allah," "the Prophets," and "the worlds."

He says: "Exalted is your Lord, the Lord of Might and Power, above these baseless descriptions that the polytheists and the ignorant attribute to Him" (سُبْحانَ رَبِّكَ رَبِّ الْعِزَّةِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ).

Sometimes they call the angels His daughters, sometimes they claim a kinship between Him and the Jinn, and sometimes they place worthless objects like pieces of stone and wood on par with Him.

The emphasis on ‘Izzah (absolute and invincible power) is, in fact, a way of drawing a line of nullification through all these imaginary deities.

In the verses of this Surah, mention was sometimes made of the glorification and sanctification by the "sincere servants of Allah," and sometimes the glorification by the angels; here, the mention is of Allah's own glorification and sanctification regarding His Pure Essence.

In the second sentence, He bestows His endless grace upon all the Prophets, saying: "Peace be upon the messengers" (وَ سَلامٌ عَلَى الْمُرْسَلِينَ). A peace that is a sign of safety and well-being from every kind of torment and retribution on the Day of Resurrection; a peace that is a security against defeats and a proof of victory over enemies.

It is noteworthy that in the verses of this Surah, peace was sent individually upon many Prophets: in verse 79 He said: سَلامٌ عَلى‌ نُوحٍ فِي الْعالَمِينَ (Peace be upon Noah among the worlds), in verse 109 سَلامٌ عَلى‌ إِبْراهِيمَ (Peace be upon Abraham), in verse 120 سَلامٌ عَلى‌ مُوسى‌ وَ هارُونَ (Peace be upon Moses and Aaron), and in verse 130 سَلامٌ عَلى‌ إِلْ‌ياسِينَ (Peace be upon Elias).

But here, He summarizes and concludes all these greetings and others in a single sentence, saying: "Peace be upon all the commissioned messengers."

Finally, He concludes the last sentence of the discourse with Divine praise, saying: "Praise be to Allah, the Lord of the worlds" (وَ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

These final three verses can be seen as an allusion to and a brief review of all the issues in this Surah. Since a significant portion of this Surah concerned Monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against various forms of polytheism, the first verse recounts all of that by glorifying and sanctifying Allah from the descriptions of the polytheists.

Another part of this Surah detailed aspects of the lives of seven great Prophets; the second verse refers to them.

And finally, another part spoke of Divine blessings, especially the various blessings of Paradise, and the victory of the Divine hosts over the army of disbelief; the praise and glorification of God at the end of the work is an allusion to all of these.

Some commentators have another analysis regarding these final three verses of the Surah, which is as follows:

The most important matters that occupy a human being are the knowledge of three things:

First, the knowledge of the Lord of the world to the extent of human capacity, and the final thing a human can do in this regard consists of three matters: 1. Sanctifying Him from what is not worthy of His status, expressed by the word "Subhan." 2. Describing Him with all attributes of perfection, alluded to by the word "Rabb," which is evidence of Allah's wisdom, mercy, ownership, and nurturance of all beings. 3. Being free from any partner or peer, which is mentioned in the phrase "above what they describe" (عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ).

The second important issue in human life is the matter of perfecting deficiencies, which is impossible without the existence of Divine leaders and heavenly guides; the phrase "Peace be upon the messengers" (سَلامٌ عَلَى الْمُرْسَلِينَ) is an allusion to this.

The third important issue in human life is to know what his fate will be after death. Here, paying attention to the blessings of the "Lord of the worlds" and His status of self-sufficiency, mercy, and grace gives man peace—"And praise be to Allah, the Lord of the worlds" (وَ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ). [1]

Key Point: What should be considered at the end of every task

In several narrations, sometimes quoted from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, sometimes from the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام, and sometimes from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read:

"Whoever wishes to be measured with the fullest measure (of reward on the Day of Resurrection), let his last words in his gathering be: سُبْحانَ رَبِّكَ رَبِّ الْعِزَّةِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ وَ سَلامٌ عَلَى الْمُرْسَلِينَ وَ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ" [1]

Yes, one should end their gathering with the sanctification of Allah's Essence, sending blessings upon His Prophets, and praise and gratitude for the blessings of the Lord, so that if any improper action or unseemly word occurred in that gathering, it may be compensated.

In the book al-Tawhid by Saduq, it is mentioned that one of the scholars of Sham came to Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and said: "I have come to ask you a question that no one has yet correctly explained to me. I asked three groups, and each gave an answer contrary to the other."

Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام asked: "What is your question?"

He said: "My question is, what was the first thing that Allah Almighty created? Some told me it was 'Power,' some said 'Knowledge,' and some said 'Spirit'."

He عليه السلام said: "None of them gave you the correct answer. Now I inform you...

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verses discussed, Usul al-Kafi, and Man La Yahduruhu (as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 440).

...that in the beginning, there was Allah and nothing other than Him, and yet He was Powerful and Mighty, and no 'might' had yet been created" (He possessed both power and knowledge in His Pure Essence without needing to create knowledge and power). Then he added: "This is what Allah says: 'Exalted is your Lord, the Lord of Might, above what they describe' (سُبْحانَ رَبِّكَ رَبِّ الْعِزَّةِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ)." [1] This alludes to the fact that the words others told you were polytheistic statements covered by this verse; Allah has been Powerful, Knowing, and Mighty from eternity.

O Lord! You Yourself have promised to help Your messengers and make Your hosts victorious. Place us on the path of the messengers and in the ranks of Your hosts, and grant us victory over the bloodthirsty enemies who have risen from the East and West of the world to destroy and extinguish the light of the Quran! O God! Protect and safeguard us from being tainted by any form of polytheism or deviation from the path of Monotheism. O Allah! The difficulties that the commissioned Prophets faced throughout history against the army of polytheism and disbelief are now manifested before our Islamic society; grant us that same peace which was the source of safety for the commissioned Prophets in these battles.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah as-Saffat. Friday, 22nd of the Blessed Month of Ramadan, 1404 (July 1, 1984).

(38) Surah "Sad" - This Surah was revealed in Mecca and contains 88 verses.

Content of Surah "Sad"

This Surah is, in fact, a complement to Surah "as-Saffat," and the structure of its topics bears a great resemblance to the structure of Surah "as-Saffat." Since it is a Meccan Surah, it contains all the characteristics of these Surahs regarding the discussion of the Origin and the Resurrection and the mission of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, blending them with other sensitive topics. Overall, it provides a healing potion for all seekers of the path of truth.

The content of this Surah can be summarized into five sections:

The first section speaks of the issue of Monotheism, the struggle against polytheism, the issue of the prophethood of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and the stubbornness and persistence of the polytheist enemies against these two matters.

The second section reflects aspects of the history of nine of Allah's Prophets, discussing "David," "Solomon," and "Job" in more detail. it reflects their difficulties in life and in calling towards God so that it may be an instructive lesson for the early believers who were under severe pressure at that time.

The third section speaks of the fate of the rebellious and transgressive disbelievers on the Day of Resurrection and their disputes and quarrels in Hell, showing the polytheists and faithless individuals where their end will lead.

The fourth section speaks of the creation of man, his high status, and the angels prostrating to Adam. It shows how vast the distance is between the ascending and descending arcs of man, so that these unaware, blind-hearted people may realize their existential value, reconsider their deviant programs, and emerge from the ranks of the devils.

The fifth and final section is a threat to all stubborn enemies and a consolation for the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, stating the reality that he seeks no reward or wage from anyone for his call, nor does he wish any pain or suffering for anyone.

Virtue of Reciting this Surah

Regarding the virtue of this Surah, which is named Surah "Sad" because of its beginning, we read in a narration from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Whoever recites Surah 'Sad,' Allah will give him rewards equal to the weight of every mountain He subjected to David, and He will protect him from persisting in sins, whether small or great." [1] And in another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, it is stated:

"Whoever recites Surah 'Sad' on the night of Friday will be granted so much of the good of this world and the Hereafter (by Allah) that has been given to no one among the people except a commissioned Prophet or a near-stationed angel, and Allah will admit him and everyone he loves from his family into Paradise, even the servant who used to serve him." 2

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah Sad (Vol. 8, p. 463).

Whenever we place the content of this Surah alongside these rewards, the link and connection between these rewards and those teachings become clear. It is once again an emphasis on the fact that the purpose is not a dry and soulless recitation, but rather a thought-provoking and decision-inspiring recitation—a thought and decision that becomes the motive for action and implements the content of the Surah in human life.
